The Government will make award to one HUBZone small business.

L.3.1. Exceptions: Offerors are required to meet all solicitation requirements, such as terms and conditions, representations and certifications, and technical requirements, in addition to those identified as evaluation factors. Failure to meet a requirement may result in an offer being ineligible for award. If the offeror finds it necessary to take exception to any of the requirements specified in this solicitation, the offeror shall clearly identify the applicable Volume and exceptions with a complete explanation of why the exception was taken, what benefit accrues to the Government (if any), and its impact, if any, on the performance, schedule, price, and specific requirements of the solicitation. Each exception shall be specifically related to each paragraph and/or specific part of the solicitation to which the exception is taken. Offerors are cautioned that taking an exception may render the offer ineligible for award.

IAW FAR 12 The Government intends to make a source selection as part of a HUBZone competitive set-aside utilizing the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) process. The LPTA process does not permit tradeoffs among price and non-price factors. Offerors shall assume that the Government has no prior knowledge of their facilities and experience. The Government will base its evaluation on the information presented in the Offeror's proposal.
Factor 1. Management/Technical Approach.
The Government will evaluate the offeror's management approach to determine if the proposed approach demonstrates an understanding of the need for a highly qualified, stable contractor workforce and will result in a highly qualified, stable contractor workforce capable of performing awarded TO requirements.
The Government will evaluate the offeror's Technical Approach to determine if the offeror has demonstrated the ability to successfully perform and a clear understanding of the PWS Task Areas in the OPI field.
The Government will evaluate the offeror's proposed Staffing and Management Approach to determine whether the offeror has proposed a comprehensive approach to plan, execute, and manage a task order and to provide qualified key personnel.

SECTION I – CONTRACT CLAUSES

The following Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) clauses are incorporated by reference and apply to this solicitation and any resulting contract:

52.219-3 Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole-Source Award (NOV 2020)
52.219-4 Notice of Price Evaluation Preference for HUBZone Small Business Concerns (OCT 2022)
52.219-8 Utilization of Small Business Concerns (OCT 2022)
52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting (OCT 2022)
52.219-28 Postaward Small Business Program Rerepresentation (MAR 2023)

This solicitation is issued as a total HUBZone small business set-aside pursuant to FAR 19.1305. Only offers from HUBZone small business concerns that are certified by the U.S. Small Business Administration (SBA) at the time of offer submission and that maintain their HUBZone certification through contract award will be considered for award. Offerors must be listed as a certified HUBZone small business concern in the SBA's Dynamic Small Business Search (DSBS) at the time of initial offer and at the time of award.

The applicable NAICS code for this requirement is 561210 – Facilities Support Services, with a size standard of $47.0 million. The HUBZone price evaluation preference factor of 10 percent shall apply in accordance with FAR 19.1307 when evaluating offers from HUBZone small business concerns against offers from other than HUBZone small business concerns, should the set-aside be withdrawn. Contractors must comply with FAR 52.219-14, Limitations on Subcontracting, ensuring that at least 50 percent of the cost of contract performance incurred for personnel shall be expended for employees of the HUBZone concern.

The U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service, Southeast Area, anticipates awarding a single Firm Fixed-Price contract for facilities maintenance and custodial support services at the Jamie Whitten Delta States Research Center in Stoneville, Mississippi. The period of performance includes a 12-month base period and four 12-month option periods.

SOLICITATION NUMBER: W912EP-25-R-0043
ISSUING OFFICE: U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Jacksonville District
SET-ASIDE: Total HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside

1.0 DESCRIPTION

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE), Jacksonville District, is soliciting proposals from qualified HUBZone certified small business concerns for environmental remediation and restoration services at multiple Formerly Used Defense Sites (FUDS) throughout Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands. This requirement is set aside exclusively for HUBZone small business concerns in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.13 and DFARS 219.13.

The contracting officer has conducted market research in accordance with FAR Part 10 and has determined there is a reasonable expectation that at least two responsible HUBZone small business concerns will submit offers at fair market prices. This determination supports the total HUBZone set-aside pursuant to FAR 19.1305(b).

2.0 CONTRACT TYPE AND ESTIMATED VALUE

The Government intends to award a single Indefinite-Delivery/Indefinite-Quantity (IDIQ) contract with a guaranteed minimum of $250,000 and a maximum ceiling of $22,000,000 over a five-year ordering period. The applicable NAICS code is 562910 – Remediation Services, with a size standard of $25.0 million. Individual task orders will be issued on a Firm Fixed-Price or Cost-Plus-Fixed-Fee basis depending on the nature of the work.

3.0 ELIGIBILITY

To be eligible for award, offerors must be certified as a HUBZone small business concern by the SBA at the time of offer submission and must represent their HUBZone status in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). The clause at FAR 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole-Source Award, is incorporated herein. Failure to maintain HUBZone certification may result in disqualification from award consideration.

JUSTIFICATION FOR OTHER THAN FULL AND OPEN COMPETITION
HUBZone Sole Source Award

1. Agency: Department of Veterans Affairs, Network Contracting Office 4, Pittsburgh, PA
2. Contracting Activity: Veterans Health Administration, VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System
3. Description of Action: Sole source award to a HUBZone small business concern for biomedical equipment preventive maintenance and repair services.
4. Authority: FAR 6.302-5(b)(5) – Set-asides for HUBZone small business concerns authorized under FAR 19.1306.

5. Basis for Sole Source Justification:

Pursuant to FAR 19.1306(a), a sole source HUBZone award may be made when the contracting officer does not have a reasonable expectation that offers would be received from two or more HUBZone small business concerns. Market research, including review of SAM.gov registrations, SBA's HUBZone certification database, and prior procurement history, identified MedServ Solutions, Inc. (DUNS: 078451239, CAGE: 8JKP2), located at 2490 Sidney Street, Suite 300, Pittsburgh, PA 15203, as the only certified HUBZone small business concern in the local area with the requisite OEM certifications and trained personnel to service the specific biomedical equipment models currently installed at VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System.

The anticipated total contract value, including options, is $3,850,000, which falls below the $4.5 million threshold for non-manufacturing NAICS codes as required by FAR 19.1306(a)(2). The applicable NAICS code is 811210 – Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance, with a size standard of $34.0 million.

MedServ Solutions, Inc. has previously performed similar work under Contract No. 36C24420C0087 and received satisfactory to exceptional performance ratings in the Contractor Performance Assessment Reporting System (CPARS). The firm has been determined to be a responsible contractor. The requirement is not currently being performed by an 8(a) participant under FAR Subpart 19.8.

SECTION L – INSTRUCTIONS, CONDITIONS, AND NOTICES TO OFFERORS

L.1 GENERAL INFORMATION

Solicitation No.: 70RCSA25R00000081
Agency: Department of Homeland Security, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)
Title: Cybersecurity Assessment and Vulnerability Scanning Support Services
NAICS Code: 541512 – Computer Systems Design Services (Size Standard: $34.0 million)
Set-Aside: Total HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside
Contract Type: Time-and-Materials with Not-to-Exceed Ceiling

L.2 ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS

This procurement is a total HUBZone set-aside in accordance with FAR 19.1305. Proposals will only be accepted from concerns that are certified by the SBA as HUBZone small business concerns at the time of proposal submission, at the time of award, and throughout the duration of contract performance. Offerors shall include in their proposals a copy of their current HUBZone certification letter issued by the SBA or a screenshot from the SBA's HUBZone certification database confirming active status.

The clause at FAR 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole-Source Award, applies to this solicitation. Additionally, FAR 52.219-14, Limitations on Subcontracting, requires that at least 50 percent of the cost of contract performance incurred for personnel be expended for employees of the concern. The HUBZone price evaluation preference described at FAR 52.219-4 shall apply in the event a HUBZone concern's offer is evaluated against a non-HUBZone concern, should the set-aside be partially withdrawn.

L.3 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE

The anticipated period of performance is a 12-month base period commencing on or about September 1, 2025, with three 12-month option periods and a six-month extension option pursuant to FAR 52.217-8. The estimated total contract value across all periods is $8,500,000.

SOURCES SOUGHT NOTICE / REQUEST FOR INFORMATION
Solicitation Number: 1333ND25SNB340019
Agency: Department of Commerce, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), National Weather Service
Subject: Marine Observation Buoy Refurbishment and Deployment Services

The National Weather Service (NWS) is conducting market research to determine the availability of qualified HUBZone certified small business concerns capable of performing refurbishment, calibration, and at-sea deployment of marine meteorological observation buoys along the Gulf Coast region. This sources sought notice is issued in contemplation of a HUBZone small business set-aside under FAR Subpart 19.13.

The anticipated requirement includes the overhaul and sensor recalibration of approximately 35 Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) stations and 12 moored buoy platforms annually, including hull cleaning, anti-fouling treatment, battery replacement, data logger programming, and vessel-based deployment. Work will be performed at a contractor-furnished facility within 100 miles of the Gulf Coast and at sea locations as directed by task order.

The applicable NAICS code is 336611 – Ship Building and Repairing, with a small business size standard of 1,300 employees. The estimated contract value is $6,200,000 over a base year and three option years.

Responses to this notice should include: (1) company name, address, CAGE code, and UEI number; (2) confirmation of active HUBZone certification with SBA certification number; (3) a brief capability statement not to exceed five pages; and (4) documentation of past performance on similar marine instrumentation or vessel-based deployment contracts.

If a sufficient number of qualified HUBZone small business concerns respond and the contracting officer determines there is a reasonable expectation that at least two HUBZone concerns will submit competitive proposals at fair market prices, NOAA intends to issue the formal solicitation as a total HUBZone set-aside with the FAR 52.219-3 clause and applicable HUBZone price evaluation preference under FAR 52.219-4. Interested firms must respond no later than June 20, 2025, at 2:00 PM Eastern Time.

SECTION L – INSTRUCTIONS TO OFFERORS

L.1 GENERAL INFORMATION

Solicitation Number: 693JJ3-24-R-000417
Issuing Agency: Federal Highway Administration (FHWA), Office of Acquisition and Grants Management
NAICS Code: 541330 – Engineering Services
Small Business Size Standard: $25.5M
Product Service Code: R425 – Support: Professional – Engineering/Technical

This procurement is a 100% HUBZone Set-Aside in accordance with FAR 19.1305. Only offerors that are certified by the Small Business Administration (SBA) as HUBZone small business concerns are eligible to submit proposals. Offerors must be a certified HUBZone small business concern at the time of proposal submission and must maintain HUBZone certification through contract award. The contracting officer will verify HUBZone status through the SBA's Dynamic Small Business Search (DSBS) prior to award.

The clause at FAR 52.219-13, Notice of Set-Aside of Orders (Nov 2020), is incorporated by reference. Additionally, the HUBZone price evaluation preference per FAR 19.1307 applies to this solicitation.

L.2 EVALUATION METHODOLOGY

The Government will evaluate proposals using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology. Proposals will be evaluated against the following factors: (1) Technical Capability, (2) Past Performance, and (3) Price. Technical Capability and Past Performance, when combined, are significantly more important than Price.

L.3 PROPOSAL SUBMISSION

Offerors shall submit proposals electronically via email to fhwa-acquisitions@dot.gov no later than 2:00 PM Eastern Time on April 18, 2025. Late submissions will be handled in accordance with FAR 15.208. All proposals shall consist of a Technical Volume (not to exceed 30 pages), a Past Performance Volume, and a Price Volume submitted as separate files.

SECTION B – SUPPLIES OR SERVICES AND PRICES

B.1 GENERAL

This solicitation is issued as a HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside in accordance with FAR 19.1305 and FAR 19.1307. Only concerns that are certified by the Small Business Administration (SBA) as HUBZone small business concerns are eligible to submit offers. The clause at FAR 52.219-13, Notice of Set-Aside of Orders, applies to this acquisition. Offerors must be a certified HUBZone small business concern at the time of offer submission and must remain certified through the date of contract award.

The applicable NAICS code for this procurement is 561210 – Facilities Support Services, with a small business size standard of $47.0 million in average annual receipts. The Product Service Code is S216, Housekeeping – Facilities Operations Support.

The U.S. General Services Administration (GSA), Public Buildings Service, Region 4, intends to award a single firm-fixed-price contract for comprehensive facilities maintenance and operations support services at the James H. Quillen Federal Building, located in Kingsport, Tennessee. The period of performance shall consist of one (1) base year and four (4) one-year option periods.

B.2 PRICE EVALUATION PREFERENCE

In accordance with FAR 19.1307, offers from HUBZone small business concerns will receive a price evaluation preference. When the lowest responsive offer is from a large business, the contracting officer shall apply a price evaluation factor of 10 percent to offers from non-HUBZone concerns for evaluation purposes only. This preference shall not apply to the actual contract award price.

B.3 ELIGIBILITY VERIFICATION

Prior to award, the Contracting Officer will verify the apparent successful offeror's HUBZone certification status through the SBA's Dynamic Small Business Search (DSBS) database and SAM.gov. Failure to maintain active HUBZone certification may result in the offeror being deemed ineligible for award.

SOLICITATION NO. 75H71025R00038
U.S. DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES
HEALTH RESOURCES AND SERVICES ADMINISTRATION (HRSA)

SECTION L – INSTRUCTIONS TO OFFERORS

L.1 SET-ASIDE DESIGNATION

This requirement is being procured as a total HUBZone set-aside pursuant to FAR Subpart 19.13 and FAR 19.1305(a). The clause at FAR 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole-Source Award, is incorporated by reference. Only offerors that are SBA-certified HUBZone small business concerns under the applicable NAICS code 541611 – Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services (size standard $24.5M) are eligible to submit proposals.

L.2 MARKET RESEARCH JUSTIFICATION

Market research conducted between September 2024 and January 2025 confirmed that there is a reasonable expectation that at least two responsible HUBZone small business concerns will submit offers at fair market prices for the required program evaluation and technical assistance services in support of HRSA's Bureau of Primary Health Care. Responses to the Sources Sought Notice (No. 75H71025SNR0012) posted on SAM.gov on September 16, 2024, yielded capability statements from seven (7) SBA-certified HUBZone firms demonstrating relevant experience.

L.3 PROPOSAL SUBMISSION REQUIREMENTS

Offerors shall submit proposals in two (2) separate volumes:
  Volume I – Technical Proposal (limited to 50 pages)
  Volume II – Price Proposal (no page limit)

Each offeror must include documentation of current HUBZone certification issued by the SBA, including the firm's certification date, certification number, and principal office address located within a designated HUBZone. Offerors must also certify that at least 35% of their employees reside in a HUBZone as required by 13 CFR 126.200.

PART I – SECTION A: SOLICITATION/CONTRACT FORM

This solicitation remains a 100% HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside as stated in the original solicitation. A.3 QUESTION AND ANSWER LOG (EXCERPT)

Q7: Can a joint venture between a HUBZone firm and a non-HUBZone firm submit a proposal?
A7: Yes, provided the joint venture meets the requirements of 13 CFR 126.616. The HUBZone small business concern must be the managing venturer and perform at least 40% of the work. The joint venture must be SBA-certified as a HUBZone joint venture prior to proposal submission.

Q8: Is subcontracting to non-HUBZone firms permitted?
A8: Yes, however the HUBZone prime contractor must comply with the limitations on subcontracting requirements at FAR 52.219-14 and perform at least 50% of the cost of contract performance incurred for personnel with its own employees for this service contract.

The applicable NAICS code remains 237990 – Other Heavy and Civil Engineering Construction, with a size standard of $45.0 million.

Network Contracting Office (NCO) 19 – Rocky Mountain Region
Solicitation Number: 36C25925Q0187

SUBJECT: Biomedical Equipment Preventive Maintenance and Repair Services – VA Eastern Colorado Health Care System

1.0 OVERVIEW

This is a combined synopsis/solicitation for commercial services prepared in accordance with FAR Subpart 12.6 and FAR 13.5, as supplemented with additional information included in this notice. This announcement constitutes the only solicitation; proposals are being requested and a separate written solicitation will not be issued.

2.0 HUBZone SET-ASIDE

This procurement is set aside exclusively for HUBZone small business concerns in accordance with FAR 19.1305 and Veterans Affairs Acquisition Regulation (VAAR) 819.1305. The clause at FAR 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole-Source Award, applies to this acquisition. Only SBA-certified HUBZone small business concerns may submit quotations.

The applicable NAICS code is 811210 – Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance, with a small business size standard of $34.0 million.

3.0 EVALUATION METHODOLOGY

Award will be made to the responsible offeror whose quotation, conforming to the solicitation requirements, represents the best value to the Government considering the following factors: (1) Technical Capability, (2) Past Performance, and (3) Price. Technical Capability and Past Performance, when combined, are approximately equal to Price.

In addition, in accordance with FAR 19.1307 and the HUBZone price evaluation preference, offers from HUBZone small business concerns will be evaluated with a 10 percent price preference applied against non-HUBZone offers, should any such offers be considered in the competitive range.

4.0 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
Base Year: July 1, 2025 – June 30, 2026
Option Year 1: July 1, 2026 – June 30, 2027
Option Year 2: July 1, 2027 – June 30, 2028

National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
National Weather Service – Office of Observations
Notice Number: 1305-AQ-25-00091-SS

The National Weather Service (NWS) is conducting market research to determine the availability of qualified HUBZone small business concerns capable of providing calibration, maintenance, and logistics support services for the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) network across the continental United States and U.S. territories.

Based on the results of this sources sought notice, NOAA intends to issue a solicitation as a HUBZone small business set-aside under FAR Subpart 19.13 if a sufficient number of capable HUBZone concerns are identified. Specifically, the Contracting Officer must determine, pursuant to FAR 19.1305(a), that there is a reasonable expectation that at least two responsible HUBZone small business concerns will submit offers and that award can be made at a fair market price. If sufficient HUBZone firms are not identified, the Government may consider alternative set-aside strategies, including small business set-aside under FAR 19.502-2.

(b) Current SBA HUBZone certification status, including certification number and date of certification. Respondents should provide a screenshot or printout from the SBA's HUBZone certification database.
(c) Demonstration of relevant experience with meteorological or environmental monitoring equipment maintenance within the past five (5) years.
(d) Description of the firm's technical workforce and geographic coverage capability.

U.S. Army Corps of Engineers – Savannah District
Solicitation No.: W912HN-25-R-0063

Environmental Remediation Support Services – Fort Stewart, Georgia

This announcement constitutes the only solicitation; proposals are being requested and a written solicitation will not be issued separately. This solicitation is issued as a Request for Proposals (RFP) in accordance with FAR Part 15, Contracting by Negotiation.

SET-ASIDE STATUS: This acquisition is 100% set aside for Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns under the authority of FAR 19.1505 and the Women-Owned Small Business Program.

NAICS Code: 562910 – Remediation Services
Small Business Size Standard: $25.0 million (average annual receipts)

To be eligible for award under this WOSB set-aside, offerors must meet all of the following criteria: (1) the concern must be at least 51 percent unconditionally and directly owned and controlled by one or more women who are United States citizens; (2) the concern must be small under the applicable NAICS code; (3) the concern must be certified as a WOSB by the U.S. Small Business Administration; and (4) the concern must perform at least 50 percent of the cost of the contract incurred for personnel with its own employees, per FAR 52.219-14, Limitations on Subcontracting.

The following provisions and clauses apply to this acquisition:
FAR 52.219-29 – Notice of Set-Aside for, or Sole-Source Award to, Women-Owned Small Business Concerns Eligible Under the Women-Owned Small Business Program (Mar 2023)
FAR 52.219-14 – Limitations on Subcontracting (Oct 2022)
DFARS 252.219-7003 – Small Business Subcontracting Plan (DoD Contracts) (Dec 2019)

Offerors shall submit proof of current WOSB certification with their proposal. The Government reserves the right to verify WOSB status at any point during the procurement process.

Agency: U.S. Army Contracting Command – Aberdeen Proving Ground
Office: ACC-APG, Research Triangle Park Division
4300 S. Miami Blvd, Suite 300, Durham, NC 27703

Set-Aside Determination: Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) Program Set-Aside

Pursuant to FAR Subpart 19.15 and FAR 52.219-29, this solicitation is issued as a total Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) set-aside. The Contracting Officer has determined, based on market research conducted between 12 January 2025 and 04 February 2025, that there is a reasonable expectation that two or more responsible WOSB concerns will submit offers at fair market prices for the services described herein.

NAICS Code: 561210 – Facilities Support Services
Small Business Size Standard: $47,000,000.00

Description of Services: The contractor shall provide comprehensive facilities management, operations, and maintenance support services at multiple U.S. Army Research Laboratory installations in the Research Triangle Park area. Services include preventive and corrective maintenance of HVAC systems, electrical distribution, fire suppression systems, plumbing, building automation systems, grounds maintenance, and custodial services. The contractor shall maintain a qualified on-site workforce and provide 24/7 emergency response capability.

Contract Type: Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ), Time-and-Materials
Ordering Period: Five (5) years
Minimum Guarantee: $50,000.00
Maximum Ceiling: $18,500,000.00

Offerors must be certified as a WOSB concern in SAM.gov at the time of initial offer and must maintain such certification through the duration of any resulting contract. Joint ventures must comply with 13 CFR 127.506.

Agency: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs
Facility: VA North Texas Health Care System
Notice Number: 36C25725S0093
Posted Date: March 18, 2025
Response Deadline: April 01, 2025, 2:00 PM CDT

The Department of Veterans Affairs, Network Contracting Office 17 (NCO 17), is conducting market research to identify potential Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns capable of providing Medical Transcription and Clinical Documentation Improvement (CDI) Services for the VA North Texas Health Care System, Dallas, TX and its associated Community Based Outpatient Clinics (CBOCs).

NAICS Code: 561410 – Document Preparation Services
Product/Service Code: Q999
Size Standard: $14,000,000

Based on preliminary market research, this procurement is anticipated to be set aside exclusively for Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns under the WOSB Program in accordance with FAR 19.1505(b) and the applicable provisions of FAR 52.219-29. The VA has identified this NAICS code as one designated by the Small Business Administration for WOSB contracting. The Contracting Officer seeks to validate that sufficient WOSB competition exists in this service area.

Interested WOSB firms are requested to submit a capability statement (no more than 10 pages) demonstrating relevant past performance, technical qualifications, and staffing resources. Responses must include: (1) firm name, address, DUNS/UEI number, and cage code; (2) current WOSB certification status through SBA's certification portal; (3) at least two references for similar medical transcription/CDI contracts within the last five years; and (4) brief narrative of approach.

Point of Contact: Sandra K. Villanueva, Contract Specialist, sandra.villanueva@va.gov, (214) 857-1033. This is a sources sought notice for informational and planning purposes only and does not constitute a solicitation.

RFP No.: 15BRCS25R00000012
Agency: U.S. Department of the Interior, Bureau of Reclamation
Region: Columbia-Pacific Northwest Region
Address: 1150 N. Curtis Road, Suite 100, Boise, ID 83706

L.1 GENERAL INSTRUCTIONS

This solicitation is issued as a Total Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) Set-Aside under the authority of FAR Subpart 19.15 and Public Law 111-240. The clause at FAR 52.219-29 applies to this acquisition. Offers received from firms that are not certified WOSBs will not be evaluated and will be rejected as non-responsive.

L.2 ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS

To be eligible for award under this WOSB set-aside, the offeror must: (a) be a small business concern under NAICS 237110 (Water and Sewer Line and Related Structures Construction), size standard $45,000,000; (b) be at least 51 percent unconditionally and directly owned and controlled by one or more women who are United States citizens; (c) have one or more women manage the day-to-day operations and make long-term decisions for the business; and (d) be certified as a WOSB in the SBA's certification program through SAM.gov (or legacy certify.sba.gov portal) at time of offer and through contract performance.

L.3 PROPOSAL SUBMISSION

Offerors shall submit proposals electronically via the Bureau of Reclamation's e-Procurement portal no later than June 12, 2025, 4:00 PM Mountain Time. Proposals shall consist of three separate volumes: Volume I – Technical Approach (50-page limit), Volume II – Past Performance (30-page limit), and Volume III – Price. Each volume shall be submitted as a separate PDF file not exceeding 25 MB.

L.4 SUBCONTRACTING LIMITATIONS

In accordance with FAR 52.219-14, Limitations on Subcontracting, the WOSB prime contractor must perform at least 15 percent of the cost of the contract with its own employees for construction contracts.

Set-Aside: This requirement is set aside exclusively for Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns. A review of the Dynamic Small Business Search, SAM.gov profiles, and GSA Schedule holders identified no fewer than six (6) certified WOSBs with relevant past performance in GIS services for federal civil works or environmental mission areas. Work will be performed primarily at the Fort Worth District offices with occasional travel to project sites across the Southwestern Division area of responsibility.

C.3 VAAR 852.219-73 VA NOTICE OF TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR CERTIFIED SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESSES (JAN 2023) (DEVIATION)

(a) Definition. for the Department of Veterans Affairs, ''Service-disabled Veteran-owned small business concern or SDVOSB'':
(1) Means a small business concern—
(i) Not less than 51 percent of which is owned by one or more service-disabled Veterans or, in the case of any publicly owned business, not less than 51 percent of the stock of which is owned by one or more service-disabled Veterans or eligible surviving spouses...

(b) General. In order for a concern to submit an offer and be eligible for the award of an SDVOSB set-aside or sole source contract, the concern must qualify as a small business concern under the size standard corresponding to the NAICS code assigned to the contract and be listed as an SDVOSB participant in the SBA certification database as set forth in 13 CFR 128.
(1) Offers received from entities that are not certified SDVOSBs and listed in the SBA certification database at the time of offer shall not be considered.
(2) Any award resulting from this solicitation shall be made to a certified SDVOSB listed in the SBA certification database who is eligible at the time of submission of offer(s) and at the time of award.

100% SDVOSB SET ASIDE

This is a Pre-Solicitation Notice that the Veterans Health Administration (VHA) intends to solicit quotes to provide non-personal Initial Outfitting, Transition, and Activation (IOT&A) services for The Villages, FL Outpatient Clinic (OPC). This will be a firm-fixed-price contract with an estimated Period of Performance of 40-months.

and

meet the small business size standard for NAICS 541614.

This contract action is subject to the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 6.302-5 Authorized or required by statute, Sole source awards under the Veterans Benefits Act of 2003 (15 U.S.C. 657f). This is also known as Service-Disabled Veteran Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) Program (FAR 19.14). This is a notice of intent to negotiate a firm-fixed price (FFP) with a base performance period of one (1) year, and two (2) optional period of performance consisting of six (6) months each for a total possible performance period of two (2) years. The intent of award is to sole-source award to the incumbent under the previously reference authority. This is NOT a request for competitive quotations. However, contractors who want to challenge this action must submit an interest letter and a capabilities statement that demonstrates their ability to perform this work. The information must demonstrate the organization's ability to provide the product(s) and service(s) as described within this notice, as well as the associated justification. All interested organizations must respond to this Special Notice by March 19, 2026, 10:00 AM (MT), to the identified PoC's E-Mail (shawn.borisow@nist.gov). Responses received will be evaluated; however, the Government's determination not to compete with the proposed procurement based on responses to this notice is solely within its discretion.

Area of Interest 1: Air Mobility Command (AMC) A6 – Base Information Technology Modernization – Please note that responses will only be accepted from Service-Disabled Veteran Owned Small Businesses (SDVOSBs)

Introduction & Mission Objective
Air Mobility Command (AMC) seeks innovative partners to lead a comprehensive transformation of its Information Technology (IT) infrastructure. The current legacy environment is outdated, inefficient, and hinders the "Global Reach" mission by compromising operational speed, data sharing, and overall warfighter effectiveness.

The objective is to overhaul this system by assessing existing architecture, developing a forward-looking roadmap, and integrating modern technologies across core, cloud, and edge environments. The goal is to field a secure, scalable, and purpose-built IT infrastructure that enables data-driven, globally maneuverable airpower for modern joint warfare.

Solicitation No.: USDA-APHIS-2025-RFQ-0438
Issuing Agency: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service
Office: Veterinary Services – Field Operations

1. DESCRIPTION OF REQUIREMENT
The USDA Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service (APHIS) requires contractor support to develop an updated training curriculum and associated digital learning modules for field veterinary medical officers regarding Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) response protocols. The curriculum must align with the current APHIS Foreign Animal Disease Preparedness and Response Plan (FAD PReP) and shall incorporate lessons learned from the 2024-2025 outbreak season.

2. CONTRACT TYPE AND VALUE
This requirement will be fulfilled through a single firm-fixed-price purchase order. The total contract budget for this effort is $38,750.00. This amount is the maximum obligation of the Government and includes all costs associated with curriculum development, instructional design, multimedia production, one round of revisions, and final delivery of 508-compliant materials.

- FAR 52.212-1, Instructions to Offerors – Commercial Products and Commercial Services
- FAR 52.212-3, Offeror Representations and Certifications – Commercial Products and Commercial Services
- FAR 52.212-4, Contract Terms and Conditions – Commercial Products and Commercial Services
- FAR 52.219-1, Small Business Program Representations

4. PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
Base Period: Date of Award through 90 calendar days.

5. EVALUATION
Award will be made on a best-value basis considering technical capability and price.

Ordering Agency: U.S. Small Business Administration (SBA), Office of Entrepreneurial Development

A. BACKGROUND AND PURPOSE
The U.S. Small Business Administration's Office of Entrepreneurial Development seeks contractor support to conduct a program evaluation of the Women's Business Center (WBC) mentorship pilot initiative launched in FY2024 across six regional sites. The evaluation shall assess program outcomes against stated objectives, analyze participant satisfaction data, and deliver actionable recommendations for national scaling.

B. SCOPE OF WORK
The contractor shall perform the following tasks:
1. Develop an evaluation framework and methodology, including logic model refinement

5. Deliver a final report incorporating agency feedback and a 30-minute executive briefing to senior leadership

C. TOTAL CONTRACT VALUE
The total obligated amount for this task order is $28,400.00. This is a firm-fixed-price order and represents the full and complete compensation for all work described herein. No additional funding or modifications for scope growth are anticipated.

D. PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
Date of Award through September 15, 2025 (approximately 120 calendar days).

E. GOVERNMENT FURNISHED INFORMATION
SBA will provide access to program performance data, prior annual reports, WBC site contact information, and a SharePoint workspace for document collaboration. All Government Furnished Information (GFI) shall be handled in accordance with FAR 52.245-1.

SECTION B – SUPPLIES OR SERVICES AND PRICES/COSTS

B.1 CONTRACT TYPE
This is a firm-fixed-price purchase order issued pursuant to FAR Part 13, Simplified Acquisition Procedures.

The total contract value shall not exceed $47,500.00 (Forty-Seven Thousand Five Hundred Dollars and Zero Cents). This amount represents the total obligation of the Government for all services, materials, travel, and deliverables described herein.

B.3 SCOPE OF WORK
The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Region 3 – Midwest Region, requires contractor support for the completion of a comprehensive wetland delineation and habitat assessment report for a 120-acre parcel adjacent to the Upper Mississippi River National Wildlife and Fish Refuge. The contractor shall provide all personnel, equipment, transportation, and supplies necessary to perform jurisdictional wetland delineations in accordance with the 1987 Corps of Engineers Wetlands Delineation Manual and applicable regional supplements.

The Contracting Officer's Representative (COR) shall review and approve all invoices prior to payment processing.

SECTION C – STATEMENT OF WORK

C.1 BACKGROUND
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE), Savannah District, requires professional land surveying services to support ongoing civil works projects at Fort Stewart Military Installation, Hinesville, Georgia. Current installation mapping data for a specific 85-acre tract along the Canoochee River corridor requires updating to reflect recent construction activities, boundary adjustments, and topographic changes resulting from flood mitigation earthwork.

C.2 SCOPE
The contractor shall perform a boundary and topographic survey of the designated 85-acre parcel, produce a georeferenced CAD drawing and associated digital terrain model, and provide certified survey plats signed by a Georgia-licensed Professional Land Surveyor. All work shall conform to the minimum technical standards set forth in the Georgia Minimum Standards for Property Surveys (Rules 180-7-.04 and .05) and USACE EM 1110-1-1005 (Topographic Surveying).

C.3 TOTAL OBLIGATION AMOUNT
The total obligation amount for this purchase order is $22,750.00 (Twenty-Two Thousand Seven Hundred Fifty Dollars). This amount constitutes the maximum Government liability and includes all costs for labor, equipment, travel, field supplies, data processing, and deliverable production. No additional funds will be obligated.

C.4 DELIVERABLES
(a) Boundary survey plat (PDF and hard copy, 2 sets) – 30 days ARO
(b) Topographic survey CAD file (.dwg format, NAD83/NAVD88) – 30 days ARO
(c) Digital terrain model – 30 days ARO
(d) Final certified survey report – 45 days ARO

C.5 INSPECTION AND ACCEPTANCE
Inspection and acceptance shall be performed by the designated Contracting Officer's Representative (COR) in accordance with FAR 52.246-4, Inspection of Services – Fixed Price.

AMENDMENT 0002 – QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
Solicitation: HHS-ACF-2026-SS-0034
Agency: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Administration for Children and Families (ACF), Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR)

The following questions were received during the inquiry period and are answered below:

Q3: Section C.4 states the contract ceiling is $75,000. Given the requirement to conduct site visits to up to 12 unaccompanied children (UC) care provider facilities across four states, develop an assessment report with individualized recommendations for each facility, and provide a final briefing to ORR leadership, can the Government confirm that the total contract obligation amount is $75,000? This figure appears to leave limited room for travel and subject matter expert labor rates.

A3: Confirmed. The total contract ceiling is $75,000.00 (Seventy-Five Thousand Dollars) as stated in Section B.3 of the solicitation. This is a firm-fixed-price contract and the ceiling includes all costs associated with performance, including but not limited to labor, travel, per diem, materials, and reporting. Offerors should plan their approach accordingly and may propose efficiencies such as combining site visits or utilizing virtual assessment methods where appropriate and approved by the Contracting Officer's Representative (COR).

Q4: Is there potential for follow-on work or option years beyond the base period?

A4: The current solicitation is for the base period only (September 1, 2026 through February 28, 2027). While ORR may have future requirements related to this program area, no option periods are included in this solicitation. Any follow-on requirement would be subject to a separate procurement action.

Q7: What is the basis for the $75,000 budget estimate?

A7: The estimated budget was developed using historical cost data from comparable assessment efforts conducted in FY2024 and FY2025 and reflects available program funding for this requirement.

SECTION C – DESCRIPTION/SPECIFICATIONS/STATEMENT OF WORK

C.1 BACKGROUND
CBP requires contractor support to develop and deliver a specialized instructor-led cybersecurity awareness training curriculum tailored to field-deployed personnel at five (5) Southwest Border sector locations. The training shall address current threats specific to law enforcement IT environments, phishing recognition, mobile device security, and incident reporting procedures consistent with DHS 4300A Sensitive Systems Handbook.

C.2 SCOPE OF WORK
The contractor shall: (a) conduct a training needs assessment through stakeholder interviews and review of existing CBP security awareness materials; (b) develop a 4-hour instructor-led training module with accompanying participant handbooks, presentation slides, and knowledge assessment instruments; (c) deliver the training at five sector headquarters locations (Tucson, El Paso, San Diego, Laredo, and Rio Grande Valley) to groups of approximately 30 participants each; and (d) provide a post-delivery summary report with assessment scores and recommendations.

All deliverables shall be submitted in accordance with the schedule in Section F. Draft materials are subject to Government review within 10 business days.

Partial payments shall be made upon acceptance of deliverables as follows: CLIN 0001 (Needs Assessment & Curriculum Development) – $28,500.00; CLIN 0002 (Training Delivery at 5 Locations) – $31,250.00; CLIN 0003 (Final Summary Report) – $4,000.00. CIRCLE will bring together integrated technical area (TA) performer teams that will develop this capability. CIRCLE performers will develop and validate computational "digital twin" models of critical illness pathophysiology capable of predicting individual critical illness trajectories and serving as the basis for both novel diagnostics and defining novel immune system control points. These digital twins should incorporate individualized, dynamic, tissue-specific patient data. These models will be capable of describing and predicting the disease trajectories of individual patients as well as patient sub-populations, explain underlying pathophysiologic processes, suggest therapeutic control points that can be leveraged to treat individual and subgroup manifestations of critical illness, and be capable of serving as the basis for simulated (in silico) clinical trials of putative therapies. In silico clinical trials are a use of computational modeling to predict how treatments interact with the human body. Simulations are not expected to substitute for real-world clinical trials. However, in silico trials can be used as a cost- and time-efficient way to make pre-trial predictions, inform the design of clinical trial protocols and inclusion/exclusion criteria, optimize dosages and treatment schedules, assess safety, and, through the use of virtual populations with varying genetics, health backgrounds, and presenting etiologies, enable mechanistic insights into disease processes, and the possibilities for personalized care. To achieve this goal most optimally, CIRCLE expects performers to be integrated, multi-component teams with expertise in clinical care of critically ill patients, animal models of critical illness, data platforms, computational modeling, and the specific intervention modalities on which the team will focus. Performer teams may include academic institutions, not-for-profits, corporate entities, or a combination thereof.

6 Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

After the POC phase, CMS anticipates the down-select of contractors by not exercising option(s) for further work based on unilateral determination of CMS' best interest. This approach ensures solutions are validated in practice, stakeholder confidence is built incrementally, and only Contractors with proven capability at scale advance.

Proof of Concept Scope
During the challenge, contractor solutions will be run in parallel with CMS's legacy shared systems. This head-to-head approach ensures that performance, accuracy, and reliability can be directly measured against current operations. Contractors must demonstrate that their platforms can adjudicate Part A, Part B and DME claims in near real-time, deliver outputs consistent with legacy results, and provide transparent explanations for any variances. Success will be evaluated based on clear, measurable metrics defined in the POC Metrics table and QASP including accuracy, throughput, interoperability, and stakeholder satisfaction. CMS anticipates that only one solution will advance to production.

PoC scope may include, but is not limited to:
1. Adjudicating large claim volumes quickly and accurately.
2. Successfully integrating with Tier 1 Systems as described below.
3. Demonstrating user interfaces and operational workflows (including both end-user and administrative functions) relevant to the PoC claim scope including any pre-configured Original Medicare rule sets, workflows, or configuration.
4. Configuring and demonstrating flexibility and speed in adding or updating edits, workflows, integrations, business rules, reference data, and related configuration artifacts needed to achieve target performance, accuracy, and variance metrics.
5. Demonstrating security compliance and an ATO-ready posture appropriate to the PoC environment.

Element 4: Human Capital
Offerors must provide relevant experience examples that demonstrate human capital orchestration using the self-score worksheet. Offerors may provide up to two (2) examples for this element.

Offerors must assign a score for each example that meets the criteria in the self-score worksheet based on the number of full-time equivalent (FTE) development staff employed on a single experience example.

As a result of the RFO initiative in support of Executive Order (E.O.) 14275, Restoring Common Sense to Federal Procurement, there were significant changes made to the acquisition strategy, period of performance, total estimated procurement value, and total number of teams needed for ODOS IV. While the Agency continues to finalize its long-term strategy for ODOS IV, it was determined that it was in the Government's best interest to pursue a smaller, competitive version of ODOS (referred to as ODOS 3.1). The succeeding competitive procurement, ODOS 3.1, was expected to be in place prior to the expiration of the current task order but has faced numerous delays in both the solicitation and evaluation phases, consequently prolonging the Government's efforts to transition work from the ODOS III contractors to new awardees. The Government has determined that a non-competitive extension to the current award is the most efficient method to continue system maintenance and the current iteration of ODOS III requirements under the ADG-REI task order until the ensuing awards are made and sufficient time is given to the new awardees to transition in.

FACTOR 1: CHALLENGE SCENARIO
Offerors shall provide a (1) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 – Challenge Scenario 1a) as well as a (2) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 – Challenge Scenario 1b) based on both of the following scenarios.

The Program Executive Office Defense Healthcare Management Systems (PEO DHMS) is modernizing its global healthcare capabilities, which includes the multi-faceted deployment of DHMS products (Federal Electronic Health Record (EHR) and Operational Medicine (OpMed)). The objective is to create a secure, seamless, and interoperable health information ecosystem that supports warfighters from the point of injury in austere, forward-deployed locations to definitive care.

Offerors shall propose an innovative and scalable deployment plan addressing two interconnected lines of effort as outlined below. The proposed approach shall address hardware and/or component installation and configuration across various networks, continuous patient data access, and comprehensive user training tailored to the operational environment and a diverse user audience. Additionally, the plan shall incorporate proactive risk mitigation strategies to address deployment complexities and ensure mission success.

The Government may require a PoC demonstration from selected offerors. The demonstration shall be conducted remotely via a secure, virtual environment provided by the offeror. Government personnel will not travel for this event. Offerors are solely responsible for all costs associated with the PoC demonstration, including providing the virtual environment, personnel, and all necessary equipment. This test plan will detail the specific elements of the offeror's solution requiring validation. Offerors will be notified if they are required to participate in the PoC, which will be scheduled to take place within 30 days of that notification. The demonstration will be conducted over two consecutive days, with a maximum of eight hours per day allotted for presentation and testing.

4. Final Price Evaluation and Award Decision: After the completion of all technical evaluation phases, the Government will conduct a detailed price analysis of the complete and final price proposals, including the pricing submitted in Attachment 5 and the supporting price narrative, for all offerors remaining in the competition. Award will be made on a best-value tradeoff basis between the final technical ratings and evaluated price.

1.8 Task 8 – Program Management Support

1.8.0.1 The Contractor shall provide the comprehensive program management, project planning, and personnel management required to ensure the successful and efficient execution of all contract requirements. This includes providing a designated Program Manager as a single point of accountability, developing and maintaining a detailed Project Management Plan (PMP), and managing a qualified workforce with the specific expertise necessary to support the DAF/A10 mission.

Key Personnel positions include: PM, International Treaties SMEs, FCP SME, CWMD SME, CWMD Computer Programming SME, Nuclear Stockpile Stewardship SME, IAMD SME, A10 Portfolio Intelligence SME, and NC3 SME.

Key Personnel Qualifications:
Program Manager: Must have demonstrated experience planning, directing, and managing complex projects of similar size and scope, including experience with DAF/DoW leadership.
International Treaties and Agreements SME: Must have demonstrated knowledge and experience with specific treaties (e.g., New START, CWC, NPT), compliance activities, and reporting systems (e.g., ACES).
Foreign Clearance Program (FCP) SME: Must have demonstrated experience with the DoW FCG, international entry requirements, sovereignty issues, and APACS policy.
CWMD SME: Must possess an active TS/SCI clearance and have extensive experience with CWMD/Counterproliferation programs, CBRN science, operational impacts, and Modeling & Simulation (M&S) tools.
CWMD Computer Programming SME: Must be an expert programmer in Python, C++, and Visual Basic, capable of modeling complex systems involving chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear effects on air operations.
Nuclear Stockpile Stewardship SME: Must have demonstrated knowledge of nuclear stockpile stewardship at all government levels and experience working with the broader nuclear enterprise.
IAMD SME: Must possess an active TS/SCI clearance and have demonstrated expertise in IAMD strategy, policy, doctrine, technologies, and the SPPBE process.
A10 Portfolio Intelligence SME: Must have demonstrated knowledge and research experience in Nuclear and CWMD-related topics.
Nuclear Command, Control and Communications (NC3) SME: Must have demonstrated experience with NC2 authorities, C2 relationships, and the waveforms, technologies, and platforms associated with the NC3 enterprise weapon system.

a. Subfactor 1: Offerors must brief a network design showcasing their approach to designing an enterprise network supporting multiple data centers as well as cloud environments utilizing the proposed solutions to meet the requirements of PWS Section 6.1 and PWS Appendix 5. The purpose of the network design is to demonstrate the offeror's approach to the proposed solution within the context of a Data Center network. The offeror may include network diagrams of the proposed design within the presentation slides as visual aids. Offerors may reference manufacturers' published data sheets for network components to demonstrate how solutions are meeting the requirements of PWS Appendix 5. The offeror must include the OEM data sheets for the devices used in the network design; the data sheets must be included as attachments/handouts to the presentation. The network design must use the criteria below.

(1) Design a network for two data centers, each with the following requirements:
(a) Traditionally hosted compute:
(i) Support for 1000 physical hosts per data center.
(ii) Support for 3000 virtual hosts.
(iii) Support 10Gbps throughput per host interface.
(iv) Future support of up to 100Gbps per host interface.
(v) Logical separation and control of Production and Management with further separation of Web, Database, App, Infrastructure Management, Enterprise Services, Data Replication, and Backup functions.
(vi) Solution must support micro segmentation and Zero Trust.
(vii) Support for fully architected enclaves requiring only external connectivity.
(2) Provide secure connectivity between two data centers.
(3) Provide secure connectivity between each data center and one or more commercial cloud environments.

Phase III Proof of Concept:
(cid:120) The Government may require a PoC demonstration from selected offerors. Offerors are solely responsible for all costs associated with the PoC demonstration, including location, personnel, and equipment.

Instructions for Phase III Proof of Concept:
The Government may require a PoC demonstration from selected offerors. The demonstration shall be conducted remotely via a secure, virtual environment provided by the offeror. Government personnel will not travel for this event. Offerors are solely responsible for all costs associated with the PoC demonstration, including providing the virtual environment, personnel, and all necessary equipment. The PoC will validate the integrated technical solution presented in Phase II. The test plan will be primarily focused on the requirements of Subfactor 2.5, but a successful demonstration will require the offeror to incorporate and demonstrate capabilities related to Subfactors 2.1 through 2.4. To ensure consistent evaluation, the Government will provide offerors with a comprehensive test plan and any necessary data 21 days before the scheduled demonstration. This test plan will detail the specific elements of the offeror's solution requiring validation. Offerors will be notified if they are required to participate in the PoC, which will be scheduled to take place within 30 days of that notification. The demonstration will be conducted over two consecutive days, with a maximum of eight hours per day allotted for presentation and testing.

Phase III PoC (Acceptable/Unacceptable)
The PoC will evaluate the offeror's ability to successfully demonstrate the integrated technical solution presented in Phase II. The demonstration must incorporate the capabilities described across Subfactors 2.1 through 2.5. A single "Acceptable" or "Unacceptable" rating will be assigned based on the overall success of the demonstration as measured against the Government's test plan. To be considered 'Acceptable', the offeror's solution must successfully demonstrate all mandatory pass/fail criteria identified in the test plan. Failure to meet any single mandatory criterion will result in an 'Unacceptable' rating for the Proof of Concept.

3. Phase III Evaluation (If activated): The Government reserves the right to activate Phase III if, after the completion of Phase II evaluations, the confidence ratings of the most highly rated offerors are too close to permit a clear best-value determination. If required, selected offerors will conduct a PoC demonstration. The PoC demonstration will be evaluated on an Acceptable/Unacceptable basis. Failure to meet any single mandatory criterion in the Government's test plan will result in an 'Unacceptable' rating.

The initial Planning, Programming, Budget, and Execution (PPBE) anticipated a need for 22 work-year equivalents (WYE) in FY26, with an estimated value of . However, the FY26 PPBE has been revised and shows a need for 26 WYE, an increase of for a total of . The addition of the proposed to the contract results in a total revised potential contract value of . Adding this additional 1-year option does not increase the potential contract value above the the original estimated total contract value of while remaining within the original scope and magnitude of the project. Additionally, the one-year extension creates a total potential contract POP of six years, and if the FAR 52.217-8 clause is exercised, an additional six months, beyond the five-year period of performance limitation required by FAR 17.204 and NASA FAR Supplement (NFS) 1817.204(e)(1). A request to deviate from the above provisions of the FAR and NFS to extend the LENS POP beyond five years is currently being concurrently routed for approval. Though the pricing for the one-year extension was not evaluated at the time of award, it was evaluated as part of this modification. Therefore, the addition of additional option year to the LENS contract is justified.

b. Subfactor 2 - Manpower
The Government estimates thirteen (13) FTEs to complete tasks detailed in the PWS. This quantity represents recommended staffing levels developed by the Government. Quoters may propose adjustments to these recommended FTE quantities, as necessary, to effectively accomplish the tasks detailed in the PWS. Any proposed adjustments to staffing levels must be clearly identified and highlighted in your technical package. All staffing adjustments are subject to review and acceptance by the Government.

Quoters shall propose the labor mix (i.e., labor categories and hours) necessary to complete the tasks outlined in PWS for key and non-key personnel in the format below. A full-time equivalent is considered 1880 hours.

# Name Billet Labor Category Hours per YR Key Personnel
1 Chuck Norris Project Manager Project Manager 940 Yes
2 Salley Sue Sr. Data Analytics Manager Data Analytics 1880 No
Total FTEs 1.5

The Government estimate of 13 FTEs is provided for planning purposes only and should not be interpreted as a mandated staffing structure. Quoters are encouraged to align proposed labor categories to the PWS task areas and clearly demonstrate how their approach effectively supports all required functions.

The contractor shall provide application lifecycle management for supported applications during the TO period of performance. GSA is responsible for the implementation, support, management and operation of GSA's applications by providing resilient, flexible, and secure technologies and services including DevOps, Database management, Operating Systems (OS) and system integration for the support of 50 applications on over 800 virtual servers spread across Red Hat Enterprise Linux, Windows, and Solaris physical servers, in addition to systems deployed on FAS Cloud Services. For the purpose of this TO, O&M is defined as activities and functions carried out to ensure existing applications perform as intended or for minor development, maintenance or enhancements. The primary technical objectives of this requirement are as follows: Objective 1 – Task Order Management. The contractor shall provide program management support under this TO. This includes the management and oversight of all activities performed by contractor personnel, including subcontractors, to satisfy the requirements identified in this Performance Work Statement (PWS). Objective 2 – Transition Management Services. The Contractor shall provide a Transition-Out Plan NLT 90 calendar days prior to expiration of the Task Order.

15.3 CONTENTS: The Contractor's Management Plan must include:

a. Description of organizational management structure, listing essential personnel and additional Contractor identified vital positions, by title, and rationale to describe how these vital positions contribute to the success of the contract. The Contractor must describe the qualifications, responsibilities, and authorities of each essential personnel and vital position.

b. Estimation by year, Contractor labor category, title, and Work Year Equivalent (WYE) of the indirect support needed for management of the ESSCA II contract. This information should specifically call out labor related to ATOMS Suite.

c. Management concepts, plans, practices, organizational approach, and communication channels between the Contractor, subcontractors, teammates, and the Government. This must include descriptions, flow charts, authority channels (including level of local autonomy), a communication plan, schedules, and how the work maps to its organization and other documentation necessary to give a comprehensive plan.

g. Staffing management approach to include recruitment and retention plans, for both the prime and subcontractors mentoring, cross-training, flexibility to accommodate changes in requirements, fluctuation in workload, such as sharing skills across organizations, and unexpected attrition. Description of an approach that provides workforce flexibility necessary to accommodate short-term and long-term increases and decreases in the level of support in order to mitigate impact to the Government.

DEMONSTRATION AGENDA
Digital Competency Management System

I. Introductions – 5 mins
II. Quick Overview of Product Options – 10 mins
a. Benefits of your Digital Competency Management System
b. Why Choose your Digital Competency Management System?
III. Presentation and Demonstration – 45mins
a. Showcase Overall Digital Competency Management System
b. Competency, Skills, and Role Management
c. Reporting, Analytics, and Workforce Visibility
i. Describe the data that can be exported, including competency information, assessment results, and system usage.
ii. Describe the reporting and dashboarding capabilities the platform provides for competency and education tracking, skill progression, and organizational oversight?
iii. Describe how the platform supports regulatory surveys and mitigates risk related to competency validation deficiencies?
iv. ROI and Value Measurement
d. Platform Configuration, Architecture, and Usability
e. Integration and Interoperability
f. Explain how competency and learning systems interact for tracking, assignments, and reporting.
g. Support, Maintenance, and Vendor Management
h. Technical and Architecture
i. Security, Privacy and Compliance
j. Implementation, Training, Change Management, Adoption, Testing, and Validation

Questioning and Closing
Presentation shall not exceed one (1) hour. There will be thirty (30) minutes for questions and answers after the presentation.

2.2.2.2 Subfactor 2b: Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation

Product Demonstration/Oral presentations shall be provided using Microsoft PowerPoint. The number of slides should not exceed 45. Offerors are instructed to submit their PowerPoint presentation with their proposal. Offerors will not have an opportunity to revise the PowerPoint after the submission due date has closed.

Offerors will be contacted about scheduling the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation. The Government anticipates the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation to take place within two (2) weeks from the proposal submission due date. The Government reserves the right to reschedule the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation at the sole discretion of the Contracting Officer. Government attendees will be identified at the time of the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation. If an Offeror fails to schedule the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation as instructed, the Offeror's proposal will not be evaluated and will make the Offeror ineligible for award.

The location for the Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation will be at Joint Base Andrews, Maryland or Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling, DC. The Government reserves the right to change the site at the sole discretion of the Contracting Officer. Offerors may not present any classified information in either their proposals or their Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation.

During the Offeror's Product Demonstration/Oral Presentation, they will be given time to set up and then be allowed 60 minutes to demonstrate the live capabilities of their product. Offerors are instructed to demonstrate the objectives labeled 'presentation' in Table 2 above.

Presentations must be done in person and no more than two presenters per Offeror. Offerors shall not use any type of video during the oral presentation, whether as pre-recorded presentations or as part of the Offeror's briefing materials. No external consultants will be allowed to represent an Offeror in the product demonstration.

Note 1: The Government reserves the right to ask questions after the 60-minute presentation following a break for consultation. The Questions & Answers (Q&A) session will be considered in the Offeror's technical evaluation. Q&A does not constitute formal discussions.

Note 2: IAW RFO 15.202(a)(3), Oral Presentations will be recorded. If an Offeror refuses to have their Oral Presentation recorded, they will be determined Unacceptable and not further evaluated.

The Fly-Off will consist of several phases:
a. Solicitation Phase (specific Call procedures are explained in Section 7)
i. Pitch-Deck Submission and Evaluation (which includes an eligibility determination)
ii. Request for Proposal
iii. Proposal Evaluation
b. Contract Award
i. Negotiation
ii. Other Transaction for Prototype Award
c. Test Planning
i. Adjudication of any required modifications to base Fly-Off test plan based on specific system under test.
d. System Integration onto Autonomy Prime Testbed Aircraft
i. Integration of test subject hardware + software into Autonomy Prime Testbed (Interface Control Document (ICD) and Application Program Interface (API) will be provided during RFP phase of solicitation) at Eglin AFB, FL.
ii. Ground Checks of the system completed.
e. Flight test events
i. The Autonomy Prime testbed will attempt to fly predetermined course(s) using navigation solution(s) provided by the test system. Divergence from optimal course will be allowed and monitored up to a predetermined geofence. Error from optimal course will be measured.
f. System Disassembly from Autonomy Prime Testbed Aircraft
i. Test subject hardware + software should be removed from the Autonomy Prime Testbed and the Testbed returned to its original configuration.
ii. Inspection by the USAF test team to ensure the Testbed configuration has returned to its baseline and all performer assets have been returned.

B.4. Problem Statement:
Applicants will present an agentic AI tool that integrates advanced machine learning, natural language processing (NLP), behavioural analytics, and influence strategies.

The tool should possess capabilities to autonomously identify target audiences, craft personalised messaging, and adapt strategies in real time based on feedback and changing societal contexts, all while staying aligned with NATO's principles and ensuring legitimacy is always maintained.

Ideally the solution would have a Technology Readiness Level (TRL) of 5-7; with the possibility of immediate Minimum Viable Product (MVP) delivery preferred (under 3 months).

B.5. Scope
In General: The Agentic AI Tool should have the following characteristics:
i. Enhanced support to Decision-Making: Show the AI Tool analysing available data, identifying issues, and making actionable recommendations.
ii. Problem Solve: Allow the AI Tool to address a complex, multi-step task or find creative solutions under constraints for creating and managing channels of delivery, formulating effective messaging, and amplifying communication efforts.
iii. Supports Strategic Communication by:
Increasing tempo without sacrificing structure
Reducing analyst and coordination overload
Expanding planning option space
Creating persistent situational awareness
Enabling continuous adaptation
iv. Human-led judgment: Importantly, it should be framed as: Human-led judgment + AI-enabled scale and synthesis not autonomous influence decision-making.
v. Learn and Adapt: Incorporate unforeseen variables or novel situations during the demonstration to showcase the AI adjusting on the fly.

Effort Description & Summary

Missile Defense Agency (MDA) Agile Professional Services Solutions (MAPSS) is MDA's fourth generation procurement of Advisory and Assistance Services (A&AS) support and is the direct follow-on professional services program to Technical, Engineering, Advisory, and Management Support (TEAMS) Next (2020-2029). Previous instantiations of A&AS support programs for the Agency include Missile Defense Agency Engineering and Support Services (MiDAESS) (2009-2016) and TEAMS (2015-2024). The MAPSS portfolio will provide the A&AS support spanning Fiscal Years 2026 (FY26) through FY36 and captures twenty-eight (28) contract requirements. In general, the A&AS support to MDA provides for the continuous assessment of engineering, technology, process, production, and programmatic practices used to develop, test, field, and operate an integrated, layered, Missile Defense System (MDS). Types of support required include: 1) engineering & technical support; 2) studies, analyses, & evaluations; and 3) management & professional services. More specific taskings are detailed by requirement and by stakeholder to ensure MDA's manpower needs are met to execute mission requirements. The activities associated with the MAPSS acquisition will be performed worldwide, but predominately in the National Capital Region (NCR)/Washington, DC, Redstone Arsenal/Huntsville, AL; Dahlgren, VA; and Schriever SFB/Colorado Springs, CO.

At this time, the total MAPSS portfolio encompasses approximately 3,000 Full Time Equivalent (FTE) positions with an estimated value of $5.517B.

C.1. INTRODUCTION

The Office of the Chief Administrative Officer ("CAO") House Information Resources (HIR) is seeking contractor support to provide Technology Service Support to staff of the U.S. House of Representatives ("House") in both Washington, DC and district offices. The House environment is composed of 441 Member offices and over 50 Committee, Leadership, and support offices. There are approximately 16,000 staffers of the House and approximately 950 district offices across the United States and Territories.

C.3. SCOPE OF TECHNOLOGY SERVICE SUPPORT

This Statement of Work (or "SOW") defines the scope, objectives, and deliverables for the provisioning of Technology Support Services. These services include system maintenance and system administration to ensure optimal performance, security, and a reliable IT Infrastructure in support of Member, Committee, Leadership, or other House offices.

The Office of IT Customer Solutions Directorate (ITCS) is seeking qualified contractor support to provide Technical Support Operations in alignment with the functional and technical requirements as described herein.

ITCS provides a comprehensive range of Tier 1, 2, and 3 end-user technology support services to House Members, Committees, Leadership Offices, CAO Business Units, and staff. A key strength is our team's ability to effectively support users of current technology products and services within the House environment. This capability requires ITCS to have adequate staffing to ensure requests from House staff needing technical assistance are responded to and addressed within established service level agreements (SLAs). Our goal is to guarantee the continued operations of the computers, mobile phones, and systems used to conduct House business.

F.3. PLACE OF PERFORMANCE

Generally, Capitol Hill House Office Buildings in Washington, D.C. or Washington Metropolitan area and area District Offices located across the United States.

Challenge cycles will occur on a periodic basis beginning with an API check where Proposers will be evaluated on their ability to create API-compliant DTs and AI Test Agents, to validate their software development capabilities.

1. DT Challenges: Proposers will be provided with either as-flown or simulated flight test maneuvers. The Proposers' DTs will be evaluated on the accuracy of the predictions and uncertainty quantification for pre-defined engineering quantities of interest, relative to either hold-out flight test data or simulated truth data. Given further hold-out flight test data or simulated truth data, the Proposers' DTs will be evaluated on their ability to assimilate new data and subsequently improve future predictions with reduced uncertainty for pre-defined engineering quantities of interest. In addition, the Proposers' DTs will be evaluated on the speed of their predictions, uncertainty quantification, and data assimilation.

2. AI Test Agent Challenges: Proposers will deliver AI Test Agents that will be integrated with a Government-supplied, uncertainty quantifying DT. The Proposer's AI Test Agents will be evaluated on their ability to generate safe, information-maximizing test maneuvers as the underlying DT learns, in real time. Specifically, the AI Test Agents will be evaluated on the degree to which they comply with user-defined constraints (fuel use, dynamically changing airspace, etc.), identify and avoid unsafe conditions consistent with user-defined risk tolerance, and the efficiency with which they achieve uncertainty reduction targets.

3. CyPhER Challenges: Proposers will deliver DTs and AI Test Agents for integration within the IV&V environment. The combined DT and AI Test Agents, called CyPhERs, will be evaluated on their ability to generate safe, information-maximizing test maneuvers as the underlying DT learns in real time. Specifically, CyPhERs will be evaluated to the degree to which they comply with user-defined constraints, identify and avoid unsafe conditions consistent with user-defined risk tolerance, the efficiency with which they achieve uncertainty reduction targets, and the accuracy of their predictions and uncertainty quantification regarding pre-defined engineering quantities of interest.

Phase 2 Option (planned for 18 months) is targeted to mature and integrate the DTs and AI Test Agents. The challenge cycles in Phase 2 Option will provide quantitative evidence of the Proposers' abilities to develop a real-time, knowledge and safety maximizing AI Test Agents and will serve as the primary readiness criteria for flight tests. Phase 2 Option will culminate in a full-scale demonstration wherein the integrated CyPhERs will autonomously execute a test plan on an experimental aircraft. Direct comparisons will be drawn between the efficiency of the CyPhER-driven test plan and the conventional test plan previously executed on that aircraft.

The CyPhER Forge program metrics will use HFMs and flight test data from an experimental aircraft as benchmarks. Each challenge cycle shown in the program schedule will evaluate the metrics identified in Table 4, with progressive increases in scenario complexity, model fidelity and model speed requirements. To meet the goals of the program, Proposers are required to achieve the metrics identified in Table 4 and Table 5. The primary metric for program success is achieving a decrease by ten times (10X reduction) in the number of test points required to characterize key multi-physics quantities of interest to a specified global confidence interval, compared to the conventional test plan of the experimental aircraft.

Proposers successfully achieving objective targets for AI Test Agents in Phase 2 Option, will introduce adaptive test planning based on rigorous measures of knowledge gain subject to user constraints, and provide statistical safety guarantees including low false alarm rate per maneuver, low missed alarm rate per maneuver, and highly accurate safety violation probability characterization. This will far surpass the state-of-the-art in test safety and planning, which is driven by historical heuristics and fixed intervals.

L.4 Phase II - Oral Presentations (Flyoff)
After evaluation of the written proposals, the Government will establish a competitive range in accordance with FAR 15.306(c). Offerors included in the competitive range will be invited to participate in an Oral Presentation, or "flyoff."
• Purpose: The Oral Presentation is intended to validate the offeror's proposed technical approach and allow the Government to assess the problem-solving and communication skills of the proposed Key Personnel.
• Participants: The presentation must be led by the proposed Key Personnel identified in Volume I.
• Format: During the session, the Government will present the offeror's team with one or more scenario-based challenges directly related to the PWS. The team will be given a defined period to collaborate and develop a solution, which they will then present to the Government evaluation panel.
