1.3.1 On-site Program Manager
1.3.1.1 It is mandatory that the contractor provides at a minimum one (1) full-time on-site Program Manager (PM) to oversee and direct the contractor employees.
1.3.1.2 The PM will be responsible for learning the basic responsibilities of the three positions: Test Examiner, OPI/Web-based Test Scheduler and OPI Facilitator.
1.3.1.3 The PM will coordinate daily with both the section supervisors and the COR.
1.3.1.4 The PM must complete all orientation for their position and all orientations and certifications required for the Test Examiner position.
1.3.1.5 The PM will be present during the initial orientation of all employees.
1.3.1.6 The PM will also fill the position of OPI Facilitator when required.

1.4 Objectives:
The Contractor shall provide non-personal service(s).
1.4.1 No less than six (6) Defense Foreign Language Program (DFLP) Test Examiners.
1.4.1.1 Contractor shall administer approximately 10,000-20,000 language tests throughout a one-year period.
1.4.2 No less than Two (2) Oral Proficiency Interview (OPI)/Web-based Test Schedulers.
1.4.3 One (1) OPI Test Facilitator/Test Examiner
1.4.4 One (1) Program Manager

Step Two – Detailed Approach:
After White Paper evaluation(s), selected offerors will be invited to design and implement a solution for AMC's IT challenges for step two. Air Force subject matter experts (SMEs) will be available for consultation. The proposed solution must provide a detailed technical approach that meets or exceeds the following requirements:

A. Assessment & Analysis Methodology:
• Describe the technical approach for conducting a comprehensive assessment of the existing IT infrastructure at two representative AMC installations.
• This must include a detailed methodology for evaluating legacy systems to identify specific operational inefficiencies, performance bottlenecks, and security vulnerabilities.
• Proposals must specify the tools, diagnostic techniques, and personnel expertise that will be used to map the current "as-is" architecture and operational workflows.
• Explain how findings will be analyzed and documented to establish a clear performance and security baseline for the transition roadmap.

B. Proposed Future State Architecture:
• Present a high-level concept for the target "to-be" architecture that is secure, scalable, and resilient. This design must encompass core data centers, edge computing environments, and cloud components (hybrid and/or multi-cloud).
• Specifically address the approach for implementing a secure and accredited Cross-Domain Solution (CDS) to enable seamless data exchange between NIPRNET, SIPRNET, and other required domains.
• Describe the strategy for migrating legacy applications and on-premises data to the proposed modernized environment while ensuring mission continuity and adhering to strict cybersecurity requirements.

3.2. Evaluation Stages
The proposal evaluation process will lead to the identification of a finalist or finalists and will take place in two stages:
1. Evaluation of Proposal responses;
2. Interviews/Equipment and Service demonstration.

Upon completion of each stage in the process, the Committee will score each Proposer and select those who are short listed for the subsequent stage.

3.3.2. Stage 2, Interviews/Demonstrations.
A short-list of firms may be invited to Stage 2, Interview/Demonstration, step of the evaluation process. Interview/demonstrations are an important aspect of the evaluation process that offers the City an opportunity to see how the Proposer's solution meets the critical components of the RFP. Evaluation of Respondents in Stage 2 consists of oral interviews and presentations by Proposers short listed in Stage 1, along with an in-person equipment demonstration and testing of proposed services. One hundred (100) points will be the maximum score for Stage 2. Interviews and presentations may be conducted in-person, virtual, or some combination thereof. Proposers invited to participate in Stage 2 will be given additional information regarding the City's desired content in a reasonable time before the scheduled Stage 2 oral interviews and presentations are held.

For equipment and service demonstration, Proposers shall provide at least two (2) vehicles for each type of Fleet Vehicle offered for the proposed service. Evaluation standards include verification of the Proposers' ability to deliver proposed services with available equipment, and verification of the Proposers' proposed solutions to provide equitable access to qualifying users.

1. Equipment Demonstration: The mobile phone app, RFID card, keypad, text-to-unlock and/or any other method for reserving, unlocking and/or holding must be functional and presented for testing by the Committee to be considered as part of the Equipment Demonstration score.

Compliance with the City's safety requirements and any proposed safety technology, including, but not limited to, geofencing, speed governing, and sidewalk detection features, must be functional and presented for testing by the Evaluation Committee and will be considered as part of the Equipment Demonstration score.

If the Proposer includes any new or enhanced parking technology in its proposal, a demonstration of such technology must be provided as part of the Equipment Demonstration.

If the Proposer proposes features such as audible or visual warnings, detection of sidewalk riding, detection of multiple riders on a single device, or similar rider behavior monitoring technologies, these features must be demonstrated during the Equipment Demonstration.

The Equipment Demonstration factors will include riding on different surfaces, flat ground, inclines, accelerating, stopping, turning, starting, stopping, handlebar height adjustments, and other riding factors.

FACTOR 1: CHALLENGE SCENARIO

Offerors shall provide a (1) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 - Challenge Scenario 1a) as well as a (2) proposed Deployment Approach (A003) (Volume 1: Factor 1 - Challenge Scenario 1b) based on both of the following scenarios.

The Program Executive Office Defense Healthcare Management Systems (PEO DHMS) is modernizing its global healthcare capabilities, which includes the multi-faceted deployment of DHMS products (Federal Electronic Health Record (EHR) and Operational Medicine (OpMed)). The objective is to create a secure, seamless, and interoperable health information ecosystem that supports warfighters from the point of injury in austere, forward-deployed locations to definitive care.

Offerors shall propose an innovative and scalable deployment plan addressing two interconnected lines of effort as outlined below. The proposed approach shall address hardware and/or component installation and configuration across various networks, continuous patient data access, and comprehensive user training tailored to the operational environment and a diverse user audience. Additionally, the plan shall incorporate proactive risk mitigation strategies to address deployment complexities and ensure mission success. Offerors shall document any assumptions made within their proposed approach.

1a) Federal EHR product modernization and deployment: This effort focuses on the full implementation of the Federal EHR at established, high-capacity treatment facilities.

Scope: One large accredited Outside Continental U.S. (OCONUS) medical center (250 beds), including two community hospitals (125 beds) and five associated out-patient clinics spread across multiple time zones with approximately 5,000 total users that includes foreign national employees and 50,000 beneficiaries.

1b) OpMed products' modernization and deployment: This effort delivers mobile and interoperable OpMed solutions to medical forces in deployed and tactical environments in both the Continental U.S. (CONUS) and OCONUS. This includes theater hospitals, hospital ships, forward resuscitative sites, and submarines. These scalable deployments can occur concurrently across multiple sites, countries, or theaters, in remote or austere environments with each location receiving one or more products tailored to mission requirements. The deployment includes the OpMed product suite: Battlefield Assisted Trauma Distributed Observation Kit-Joint (BATDOK-J), Operational Medicine Care Delivery Platform (OpMed CDP), Theater Blood-Mobile (TBLD-M), and Medical Hub (MedHub).

Scope: Rapidly deploy globally to no less than five sites/units simultaneously across forward-deployed locations managed by multiple Services in EUCOM and INDOPACOM (Role 1-3) with uncertain schedule and complex infrastructure. This includes deployments from Naval vessels and field hospitals to forward resuscitative and enroute care teams.

Note: For the purposes of the Challenge Scenario, the following terms are defined.
Innovative: A new or significantly improved technology, process, or method as of the date of the submission of the proposal.
Scalable: Resources can be quickly scaled up or down to match demand.
Feasible: The ability to achieve deployment with the available resources, technology, time, and schedule. It is a pragmatic assessment of whether an idea can be successfully executed in the real world, while actively minimizing risk.
Coherent: The approach is logical and understandable.

7.3.1-17 - Lowest Price Technically Acceptable Evaluation Method - November 2025
(a) After receipt and evaluation of proposals, FDIC may award a contract to the responsive offer that is the lowest priced and technically acceptable.
(b) After obtaining all pricing information, the FDIC may, at its discretion, limit the evaluation to the lowest priced offers or proposals. In such cases, additional proposals may not be evaluated. The FDIC also reserves the right to request additional price reductions from the lowest priced technically acceptable offeror, if it is in the FDIC's best interest.

Re-platforming the shared systems ensures timely, accurate payments, reduces provider burden, prevents fraud, and creates long term savings. ClaimsCore replaces Original Medicare's core systems with a sustainable, policy responsive platform. Further, ClaimsCore implements an underlying preference for Commercial supplies and services to avoid additional costs and schedule (See FAR 12.201).

The current legacy systems require lengthy, high-risk change cycles that delay critical updates and heighten fraud exposure.

Opportunities include:
 Mainframe risk retirement. Replacing 1970s mainframe/COBOL systems cuts obsolescence risk and dependency on scarce skill sets.
 Faster emergency responsiveness. Configuration-based changes allow rapid policy updates during public health events without waiting for lengthy code releases.

Business Logic migration: Extensive COBOL code logic with little documentation needs to be analyzed and the relevant logic imported into to the new system. CMS is undertaking experimental efforts to extract and provide business logic and edits to awardees, but Contractor(s) are ultimately responsible for fully and correctly migrating business logic, in order to achieve parity with the outcomes of the existing systems.

This procurement is for the proprietary, commercial off-the-shelf (COTS), brand-name Menlo Security, Inc.'s Cloud Browser (MSCB) product, as a Menlo Security, Inc. reseller-provided managed service, providing the Department of War's (DoW's) remote browsing isolation (RBI) solution for CBII. The action includes the renewal of existing subscription licenses, the procurement of new subscription licenses, and the incorporation of Menlo Security, Inc.'s Highly Evasive and Adaptive Threats (HEAT) Shield with Artificial Intelligence/Machine Learning (AI/ML) capabilities to enhance cybersecurity measures.

Since 2020, the DoW has leveraged CBII through the MSCB platform under a production Other Transaction Authority (OTA) agreement. Over the past five years, MSCB has continuously evolved to safeguard DoW users from cyber threat actors by proactively engineering defenses against malicious tactics, techniques, and procedures.

Originally hosted on Amazon Web Services, this capability has been further enhanced with the strategic expansion of MSCB to a second cloud environment, the Google Cloud Platform. This expansion improves system redundancy, optimizes load balancing, and ensures uninterrupted availability of the CBII environment, even during outages. MSCB guarantees continuous, secure web browsing for DoW personnel worldwide.

The CBII Program Management Office (PMO) projects the total user base under OPORD 21-0006 to range between 3M and 3.2M, with the capacity to scale up to 3.4M users to accommodate surge requirements under an enterprise licensing model.

Only the specific OEM proprietary, brand-name specific MSCB, which is available through authorized resellers, will satisfy the needs and can support the infrastructures currently in place. This requirement is for proprietary products and/or product support, and no competitor functional replacement products exist in the market at this time to meet the Government's needs.

This requirement cannot be set aside for a small business or any other socio-economic group because the ostensible subcontract rule, 13 C.F.R. 121.103(h)(3) would apply. The Government will continue to conduct market research to determine if there are changes in the marketplace that would enable future competitive actions; i.e., there are similar products and/or product support in the marketplace that provides the same functionality, the original equipment manufacturer releases ownership rights of the product, or it is economically feasible to procure a new system.

a. Basis for Contract Award

Award will be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is determined to represent the overall best value to the Government using a lowest price technically acceptable (LPTA) evaluation process. The Government anticipates awarding to the lowest price technically acceptable respondent from initial contractor responses, without engaging in exchanges with respondents. Respondents are strongly encouraged to submit their best technical solution and price/cost in response to this solicitation.

The technical proposal should include all information the offeror wants the Government to consider and evaluate regarding its ability to perform all work described in the evaluation subfactors. To be eligible for award, the offeror's proposal must sufficiently address all aspects of the evaluation factors and subfactors. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the offeror being ineligible for award.

Section 508 Accessibility Standards do apply to this requirement. Section 508 requirements are listed in the attached PWS (Attachment 1). Compliance with Section 508 standards will be considered prior to evaluating any of the selection criteria listed below. Any proposal failing to meet the Section 508 Accessibility Standards, as listed in the PWS, may be eliminated from consideration for award.

The Maine Department of Secretary of State, Division of Elections (Division) is seeking proposals to lease and receive support services for a statewide Ballot Scanning and Tabulating System (BSTS) with compatible Accessible Voting System (AVS) that includes functionality to accommodate Maine's ranked-choice voting (RCV) process with implementation required prior to the November 3, 2026 General Election as defined in this Request for Proposals (RFP) document. There will be an initial 2.5-year contract period with the option for two (2) additional 3-year extensions at the discretion of the Division. The successful Bidder will provide election management software to allow the Division to independently create ballots and program removable memory devices for use with the tabulators and AVS. The term of the lease will be for an initial 2.5-year period, with the option to renew for two 3-year extensions at the discretion of the Division.

SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Basis for Award
The Government intends to award a single contract to the responsible offeror whose quotation is determined to be Technically Acceptable and offers the Lowest Price. The Government will evaluate quotations and may make award without discussions. Therefore, the offeror's initial quotation should contain its best terms.

M.2 Evaluation Approach
The Government will use a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical quotations will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis. Price will not be evaluated for tradeoff against technical factors.

M.3 Technical Acceptability
To be rated Technically Acceptable, the quotation must clearly demonstrate compliance with all requirements of the solicitation, including:
- Relevant experience performing similar facility condition assessments
- Qualified personnel, including a licensed Professional Engineer (PE)
- Technical approach that meets all Statement of Work requirements
- Ability to meet the required schedule
Failure to meet any of the above criteria will result in a rating of Technically Unacceptable, and the quotation will not be considered for award.

M.4 Price Evaluation
Price will be evaluated for completeness and reasonableness in accordance with FAR 13.106-3.

BASIS FOR AWARD: This award selection will be conducted using Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) procedures. To be eligible for an award, a proposal must meet all technical requirements, comply with all required terms and conditions, and include all required information. All non-price evaluation factors must be rated as "ACCEPTABLE" in order to be eligible for award. An "UNACCEPTABLE" rating in any factor will result in the proposal being rated "UNACCEPTABLE" unless corrected through discussions. A proposal rating of "UNACCEPTABLE" makes a proposal ineligible for award. Since the LPTA methodology prohibits trade-offs, all evaluation factors and sub-factors are equally important. Proposals are evaluated for acceptability but not ranked based on non-price factors.

A price evaluation will be performed for all offerors, and proposals will be ranked from lowest to highest price based on the Total Proposed Price (TPP) provided by the offeror on the price schedule sheet. Next, each factor will be evaluated and, subsequently, the overall proposal will be prepared, beginning with the lowest-priced proposal. The award will be made to the lowest-priced proposal that meets the acceptability standards for the non-price factors.

1. INTRODUCTION. This document defines the materials, training, and support needed to provide advanced training on the Oracle Health Electronic Health Record (EHR) platform and associated tools to the Veterans Health Administration (VHA) for administration of Health Informatics roles and responsibilities.

1.1. Background. VHA provides informatics support for both the VistA-based and Oracle Health-based Federal EHR and associated tools to monitor VistA and Federal EHR use, configuration, and quality. VHA staff members who are or will be responsible for such support during and following the Federal EHR deployment require training on the Oracle Health platform and tooling architecture.

1.2. Oracle Health maintains a catalog of training opportunities available to VA's EHRM-IO and all Oracle Health clients. Training opportunities include instructor-led, virtual instructor-led, and self-paced online courses. Online courses range from 15 minutes to several hours. Instructor-led and virtual instructor-led courses range from 3 hours to 8 days. The training opportunities and catalog include per-course pricing ranging from approximately $200 to $4,850. Oracle Health also offers a 'Premier Pass' option, which allows a holder of the 'Premier Pass' to enroll in as many courses as they wish during the period of performance of the pass (typically, one fiscal year).

The North Carolina Department of Labor (NCDOL) is seeking information from qualified vendors regarding solutions to modernize and replace its existing Elevator and Amusement Device inspection and permitting systems. The current systems have been in place for over 15 years and no longer adequately support modern business processes, data integration, or reporting needs. NCDOL is pursuing a comprehensive, scalable solution that supports end-to-end operations including: Device inspection request, Permit submission and approval, Inspection scheduling and execution, Reporting and compliance tracking, Invoicing and financial system integration. This RFI is issued for informational and planning purposes only. Responses will be used to inform a potential future Request for Proposals (RFP). NCDOL's existing systems present several operational and technical limitations, including: Reliance on legacy technologies with limited flexibility, Manual and fragmented workflows, Limited integration with financial and external systems, Insufficient reporting and analytics capabilities, Lack of mobile and offline functionality for inspectors. Vendors are encouraged to address how their proposed solutions resolve these challenges.

Inquiry Charter School, located at 1301 Belmont Avenue, is seeking proposals for the acquisition and implementation of a comprehensive emergency response and notification system designed to enhance safety and emergency preparedness within the school. The desired system should support a 'one-touch' alert capability that enables rapid communication throughout the building in the event of emergencies such as fires or lockdown situations. Ideal system should be capable of broadcasting alerts via multiple channels, including: Text messages displayed on LED signs, Pre-recorded voice announcements, Automated emails, Smartphone app notifications, Visual alerts such as strobes, Automatic door locking. To ensure accurate and effective proposals, all prospective bidders are required to schedule a site survey with the designated project liaisons at Belmont Charter Network. SOLE SOURCE JUSTIFICATION FOR THE PURCHASE OF CYBERFEDS
This letter is to certify that LRP Publications, Inc., is the sole source of the unique combination of case decisions, statutes, regulations, manuals and guides, pay tables, bulletins and newsgroups. These research and analytical tools comprise cyberFEDS® on the Web.

Below is a list of resources that are available exclusively on cyberFEDS® on the Web:
1. Quick Start Guides (QSGs) — provide overviews of more than 260 EEO, employee relations, labor relations, classification and staffing topics and links to noteworthy cases, statutes, regulations and additional resources.
2. Summaries of Case Decisions and Arbitration Awards — permits users to quickly scan search results and call up the full text if further detail is desired.
3. Case Index Numbering System — a copyrighted system that enables users to speed and focus their research.
4. Forms Library — contains standard forms, optional forms, OPM forms, FLRA & FMCS forms, OGE forms and more.
5. News and Legislation — read the latest articles posted daily broken out by topic or search the News Archive.

For these reasons, this acquisition is being pursued as a sole source.

B. Online System Requirements: Proposers that provide an online auction service for the purpose of selling surplus property and equipment must provide a fully functional service that includes:
1. Proposer will provide all hardware, software, and servers needed to operate an online auction service. Proposer must give the Cooperative member full access to the member's assigned online auction account that allows the member to load its own equipment via a common internet browser. The system should be available to Cooperative members 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year.
2. Proposer will provide and absorb all cost of a secure hosting facility to operate the online auction system and allow the Cooperative member full access to their account. The Cooperative member will not be required to install any additional computer hardware or software at the Cooperative member's location; the only requirement will be that the Cooperative member has an internet connection via a common web browser and a digital camera. Proposer's auction service shall not interact with a Cooperative member's computer hardware, software or data base systems, except to allow access to the member's account through a common web browser.
3. Proposer will provide maintenance and upgrades to the system hardware and software at no cost to the Cooperative member.
4. Proposer will supply all operating software and applications needed for the auction site to function.
5. Connectivity to the internet shall be accessible through industry standard internet connections, web browsers, and email. The system must allow access from both Windows and Apple Mac operating systems. The system provided will not require the installation of any special software on the computer systems of the Cooperative member.

PCI Compliance Technical Support & Sustainment Services
Evaluation Factors for Award Selection
Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA)

1. BASIS FOR CONTRACT AWARD
This is a competitive Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) Request for Quote (RFQ) in which price is considered the most important factor for Contractors deemed technically acceptable. If the lowest priced quote is rated technically acceptable, no further evaluations of submitted quotes will be completed. Contract award will be made to the Contractor deemed LPTA. Quotes not technically acceptable cannot be selected, regardless of price. The Nonappropriated Fund Instrumentality (NAFI) will select the quote of the responsible Contractor in accordance with the factors indicated in evaluation factor paragraphs below.

2. NUMBER OF CONTRACTS TO BE AWARDED
The NAFI intends to award one contract for the performance of this requirement.

Quotes are intended to be evaluated, and an award made, without discussions with the Contractors, unless discussions are determined to be necessary by the Contracting Officer. Therefore, each initial quote should contain the Contractor's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. NAFI reserves the right to award all, some, or none of the deliverables requested in this Solicitation.

7. EVALUATION AND BASIS FOR AWARD (LPTA)
Award will be made in accordance with FAR 13.106-2 using a Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. The Government intends to establish a single award with a responsible ISBEE vendor whose quotation is determined to be technically acceptable and offers the lowest evaluated price.

Under the LPTA process, quotations will not be ranked or scored. Instead, quotations will be evaluated on an acceptable/unacceptable basis for non-price factors. Award will be made to the vendor whose quotation:
• Is determined to be technically acceptable under all non-price factors,
• Is compliant with all solicitation requirements,
• Is determined responsible in accordance with FAR Part 9, and
• Offers the lowest evaluated price.

7.1 Eligibility and Compliance Review
Quotations will first be evaluated for eligibility and compliance with all solicitation requirements. This review will include:
• Verification of ISBEE eligibility status
• Submission of the required IEE Representative Form
• Completion of all required representations and certifications
• Adherence to quotation preparation instructions
Quotations that fail to meet solicitation requirements or are determined noncompliant will be eliminated from further consideration.

BASIS FOR AWARD

The selected vendor shall provide a "turn-key" solution including all necessary hardware, cloud-based software, implementation services, and ongoing support. The system must be accessible via district-issued student 1:1 devices (Chromebooks/iPads/MacBooks/Windows) and staff workstations.

A. Student & Teacher Workflow
● Request & Approval: Students must be able to request a pass from their device; teachers must have a "one-click" approval/denial interface.
● Auto-Expiration: Passes must feature a countdown timer. If a student exceeds the allotted time (e.g., 5 minutes for a restroom), an automated alert must be sent to designated staff.
● Kiosk Mode: Support for "Quick-Pass" stations (dedicated tablets) in classrooms or common areas for students without devices.

B. Safety & Conflict Mitigation
● Encounter Prevention: The ability for administrators to "lock" specific student pairs or groups so they cannot have active passes simultaneously.
● Room/Area Capacity: Real-time limits on how many students can be in a specific location (e.g., "Max 3 students in North Restroom").
● Restricted Zones: Ability to "close" certain hallways or restrooms during specific periods (e.g., lunch or passing periods).

C. Administrative Monitoring
● Live Hallway Dashboard: A real-time "active pass" list for security and administrators to see every student currently in the hallway, their destination, and time remaining.
● Emergency Mode: A "Global Override" to instantly cancel all active passes and notify students to return to their classrooms during a lockdown or drill.

SECTION M - EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD
In order to permit efficient competition and evaluation, the Navy may utilize the following methodology. Quotes will initially be assessed for price and ranked in order (lowest priced to highest priced). The Navy will then evaluate the technical factors of the lowest priced quote. In the event that this quote is deemed Unacceptable, the next lowest priced quote will be evaluated as many times as necessary, until the Government identifies a technically acceptable quote. The Navy will make award to the lowest priced technically acceptable quote, if possible. Accordingly, under this methodology, the technical factors of some quotes may not be evaluated by the Navy. The Government reserves the right to evaluate additional higher priced quoters to support a fair and reasonableness determination or for other considerations. If exchanges are deemed necessary by the Contracting Officer, a maximum of five (5) lowest priced quotes will be evaluated (both non-price and price) for the purpose of establishing a competitive range.

A 'brand name or equal' purchase description cannot be used because no other commercially available product offers an equivalent, fully integrated solution that meets all required technical and operational criteria. Attempting to define 'equal' characteristics would either be overly restrictive—effectively describing only Security Onion—or too broad, resulting in solutions that lack critical capabilities or require extensive modification. In either case, the Government would incur increased costs, schedule delays, and technical risk associated with integrating multiple disparate tools to approximate the required functionality. Market research conducted across Department of War Enterprise Software Initiative (DoW ESI), National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement (SEWP), and SAM.gov confirmed that while other cybersecurity and analytics platforms exist, none provide the same combination of capabilities in a unified, mission-aligned solution. No authorized reseller was identified that could deliver the complete requirement, including software, licensing, and certified training. Furthermore, similar products either lack full packet capture integration, require significant customization to achieve comparable functionality, or depend on additional third-party components and licensing structures. These limitations prevent such products from meeting the Government's needs without substantial modification, which would introduce unacceptable cost, schedule, and performance risks. Accordingly, the specific brand name Security Onion Solutions, LLC, is essential to meet the Government's minimum requirements. The use of a brand name specification is therefore justified, as no other product can meet the requirement without compromising mission effectiveness, increasing risk, or requiring fundamental changes to the Government's operational objectives.

A. PROJECT BACKGROUND:
The Township of East Brunswick ("Township") wishes to purchase a solution that provides a secure cloud fax and emails for the Township's IT Department.
Quotes must be received by the Township's Designated Contact Person, via email, mail, or hand delivery, by 11:00 a.m. prevailing time on April 8, 2026. Quotes received after 11:00 a.m. on the Deadline Date will not be considered.

B. SCOPE OF WORK:
The Township is issuing this request for quotation via a fair and open process in accordance with N.J.S.A. 19:44A-20.4 et seq.
The Township seeks to procure the following services:
Cloud-Based Fax Services
The Township seeks to procure a secure cloud fax platform that eliminates the need for analog lines and fax hardware. The services should enable seamless, compliant faxing, designed for regulated industries, ensure the highest level of privacy, audit readiness, and global data sovereignty.
Email Platform Services
The Township seeks to procure a secure file exchange email platform designed for safe, encrypted sharing of sensitive documents and information and the transmittance of large files. The platform should ensure that sensitive information remains encrypted and protected from unauthorized access.
The solution must be able to handle 1 terabit of files.

I. BACKGROUND
The Washington Township School District is a suburban school district located in Gloucester County, NJ. The District serves approximately 7,700 students. The District has approximately 1400 employees, of which approximately 900 are full and/or part-time certified teaching positions. The District's current annual budget is in the amount of $150,000,000.
The District operates the following schools: 2 Early Childhood Center, 6 elementary schools, 3 middle schools and 1 high school. The District serves approximately 1,400 children with special needs. The District operates a Central Administration Building as well as an Operations Building.
II. SCOPE OF SERVICES
The Washington Township Board of Education is seeking priced proposals for an online data management, analytics, and assessment system for students in grades PreK-12, along with accompanying Professional Development Staff Training for the 2026-2027 school year. Services to be provided shall include the following:
A. Assessment and Data Dashboard
1. Warehouse all assessment data from any platform.
2. Create and administer assessments across all grades and subjects.
3. Collect results in customizable templates.
4. Analyze results and generate reports.
B. RTI/NJTSS and I&RS Software
1. Leverage warehoused multiple-measures and custom performance criteria to auto-tier students for NJTSS/RTI, determine programmatic eligibility, and roster students.
2. Set measurable goals, collect data to monitor progress, quantify outcomes, and evaluate effectiveness.

Evaluation Process and Basis of Award
The purchase order resulting from this solicitation will be issued as a FIRM-FIXED PRICE purchase order. The purchase order will be made to the LOWEST-PRICE technically acceptable quote.
The USCG intends to award the purchase order based on the initial quote without engaging in exchanges. However, the USCG reserves the right to conduct exchanges with one or more of the vendors if determined by the Contracting Officer to be necessary and in the best interests of the USCG. The USCG may reject any or all quotes if such action is in the public interest and waive informalities and minor irregularities in quotes received. Vendors are strongly encouraged to submit their best quote in response to this solicitation.
The Government will first review the quotes for completeness/compliance (everything required to be submitted has been submitted). If a quote is found to be incomplete/non-compliant, it may be removed from the evaluation process, and it will not be further evaluated. Then the Government will list the remaining quotes by price, lowest to highest, and will begin the technical quote evaluation with the lowest priced Offeror. Upon finding the Offeror who meets the technical acceptability, the Offeror's price will be evaluated for reasonableness. If the first quote evaluated is technically acceptable with a price that is reasonable, evaluation will stop. If the first quote evaluated is either not technically acceptable or has a price that is not reasonable evaluation will continue. Once the lowest reasonably priced, technically acceptable quote is found evaluations will stop.

B. BASIS FOR AWARD (Section M)

The award will be made to a single responsible small business Offeror whose proposal is determined to be the lowest price, technically acceptable offer (FAR 15.101-2). To receive consideration for award, an "Acceptable" rating must be achieved for the Combined Technical/Management factor (to include all sub factors) and the Past Performance factor. The Cost/Price factor will not be rated, but will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness and for realism as specified in this solicitation. Any proposal receiving a rating of "Unacceptable" in any non-price Factor will not be eligible for award. The evaluation criteria detailing the Factors and Subfactors are contained in Section M of the solicitation and are herein incorporated by reference.

The Government reserves the right to award without discussions; therefore, offerors will be cautioned to ensure that their proposals contain all required information and are complete in ALL respects.

FACTORS AND SUB-FACTORS TO BE EVALUATED

The following evaluation factors and sub factors will be used to evaluate each proposal. Award will be made based on the lowest price of the proposal that is deemed technically acceptable.

A detailed explanation of the criteria for evaluation is set forth in the "Evaluation Approach," paragraph C of this section. During evaluation of each proposal, the Government will assign each factor/sub factor, excluding Price, an Acceptable/Unacceptable rating. Each sub factor will be evaluated to determine if the proposed approach associated with each sub factor meets or does not meet the minimum performance or capability requirements. To receive an overall "Acceptable" factor rating, all sub factors must receive an "Acceptable" rating.

Decision to Use the RFP Process
Pursuant to Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 30B, Section 6, the Town of Easton is utilizing a Request for Proposals (RFP) process for the procurement of an Integrated Financial Accounting Software System.

This procurement involves complex and integrated services that cannot be evaluated solely on the basis of price. The system must support governmental fund accounting, comply with applicable GASB and regulatory requirements, integrate with existing municipal systems, ensure data security, and provide implementation, training, and ongoing technical support.

Selection of a vendor requires evaluation of qualitative factors including technical capabilities, system functionality, vendor experience, implementation approach, cybersecurity safeguards, references, and long-term support capacity. These criteria necessitate a comparative evaluation process to determine the most advantageous proposal, taking into consideration both price and non-price factors.

Accordingly, the RFP process under Chapter 30B, Section 6 is appropriate and in the best interest of the municipality to ensure a fair, competitive, and value-based procurement.

The Town intends to select the most advantageous proposal, taking into consideration technical merit, experience, and price.

PURPOSE
The purpose of this Request for Proposals is to solicit interest from qualified firms to provide an inmate management system to the detention center.

INMATE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM SPECIFICATIONS
The Campbell County Detention Center, referred to as "CCDC", is requesting proposals from providers of inmate identification, security rounds, and activity logging. CCDC is particularly interested in proposals that include the use of technology in automating key operational workflows, tasks, and correctional reporting requirements as mandated by state corrections standards. This includes, but not limited to, tracking inmate movements and activities, logging cell checks, security checks, headcounts, inmate facial recognition software, commissary pass and other logs generated by CCDC for the purpose of tracking inmate interaction.

The Inmate Management System must be able to track inmate movements via passive, electronic means and logging of cell checks and security checks via fixed electronic scannable tags. All electronic log entries must record inmate name, inmate jacket number, deputy identification, date and time of entries. Electronically captured data cannot be edited, deleted, or altered in any way.

The Inmate Management System must: Interface with Jail Tracker, CCDC's existing jail management system; Use of a ruggedized handheld device with capabilities of sustaining a minimum of a 12 hour shift of average usage before recharging battery and have the ability to retrieve reports of inmate activity.

CCDC houses a daily population range of 400 to 500 inmates and maximum population of approximately 656 inmates.

Proposals will be evaluated on multiple factors to ensure the best overall value for the government. This experience will be evaluated in conjunction with other technical, management, and performance considerations and will not serve as the sole basis for determination:

• Proficiency in threat detection, incident response, and endpoint protection
• Understanding of GCC-specific considerations and limitations

D.2 Evaluator Scoring Rubric (Illustrative Weights)
Criterion Weight Indicators of Strength
Purview DLP across workloads 25% — Intent→config mapping; SITs+EDM; monthly KPIs; exfil tests; tuning cycles
Defender stack + Sentinel 20% — Runbooks, triage workflows, SIEM integration use cases
Insider Risk & Adaptive Protection 15% — IRM policies; Adaptive Protection scoping; rollout notes
GCC/GCC High/DoD awareness & licensing 15% — Clear feature differences, compliance handling for CUI/PII
Email exfil protection 10% — Subject/body/attachments; domain scoping; Policy Tips/wait-on-send
Endpoint & Browser/Domain controls 10% — Copy/paste/print/USB restrictions; sensitive domain groups; approved browsers
Documentation & Knowledge Transfer 5% — Playbooks, naming conventions, exception handling, user education

Section M - Evaluation Factors for Award
52.212-2 ADDENDUM
BASIS FOR AWARD AND EVALUATION CRITERIA
This acquisition will be solicited, evaluated and awarded using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable, Source Selection, as supplemented by the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (DFARS), and the Army Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement (AFARS). A contract will be awarded to the Offeror deemed responsible in accordance with the FAR, as supplemented, whose proposal conforms to the solicitation's requirements and is judged the lowest price technically acceptable to the Government.
As a basis for award, trade-offs between price and non-price factors are not permitted. Offers received for less than the stated requirement will be considered ineligible for award. As set forth in FAR 52.215-1(f)(1), the Government intends to award a contract resulting from this solicitation to the responsible Offeror whose proposal represents the lowest price technically acceptable. Therefore, the Offeror's initial proposal should contain the Offeror's best terms from a price and technical standpoint. IAW FAR 15.408-2, discussions may be held. However, the Government may choose to award a contract without discussions. If discussions are determined unnecessary and award is made from initial proposals, proposals rated as 'Unacceptable' in Evaluation Factors 1 and/or 2 will not be considered for award.

SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.1 Basis for Award
The Government intends to award a single contract to the responsible offeror whose quotation is determined to be Technically Acceptable and offers the Lowest Price. The Government will evaluate quotations and may make award without discussions. Therefore, the offeror's initial quotation should contain its best terms.

M.2 Evaluation Approach

M.3 Technical Acceptability

-Qualified personnel, including a licensed Professional Engineer (PE)

-Ability to meet the required schedule
Failure to meet any of the above criteria will result in a rating of Technically Unacceptable, and the quotation will not be considered for award.

M.4 Price Evaluation
Price will be evaluated for completeness and reasonableness in accordance with FAR 13.106-3.

3.1 Guardian Automation Layer (GAL): The GAL will serve as the Single User Interface for Guardians and HR Practitioners to interact with military HR Data. Historically, Guardian data has been stored within disparate systems, making process improvements and automation cumbersome. With no single source of truth for Guardian records or port-of-call for completing military HR actions, practitioners and end users rely on manual workarounds to reach data needed for decision making.

The primary purpose of the Space Force GAL is to track and manage Guardians in a service without component structure throughout the military HR lifecycle. The desired solution will allow the Guardian a continuous experience within the GAL throughout the entirety of the HR lifecycle, from onboarding and career development to retirement and beyond. The GAL will be flexible, with a positive user experience based in human-centered design practices that are reflective of modern commercial capabilities.

The USSF is pursuing a solution that leverages role-based authorities within a unified interface to analyze data and create interactive dashboards. Low code/no code configurability will allow practitioners to adapt to the evolving policy and process requirements, such as PMA.

Automation and AI capabilities are a highly desired function of the GAL. The USSF aims to greatly reduce administrative burden by freeing Guardians from routine and repetitive tasks and will allow for increased focus on higher level work to deliver results for the USSF. It will also improve data quality, timeliness of service delivery, Guardian self-service, and HR data transparency to Guardians.

In line with current USSF and DOW policies, the Government is seeking to minimize custom software development and maximize the use of commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS), software-as-a-service (SaaS), and configurable solutions for the GAL.

SCOPE OF WORK

The minimum qualifications for the software solution are as follows:
● The software should allow schools and the district to connect school leaders, teachers, and families through one central platform
● Two-way multilingual platform that allows teachers and parents to communicate in over 100 different languages
● The software should allow users to:
○ Send SMS messages to all families with one click
○ Provide emergency notifications
○ Provide privacy and security
○ Allow for Student Information System (SIS) integration
○ Provide centralized analytics
○ Provide onboarding support and professional development
○ Provide an analytics dashboard and downloadable reports, showing usage from parents and staff
● The platform will need to accommodate a minimum of 160,000 students, and an unlimited number of administrators/teachers at more than 200 different school sites.
● The service must include an unlimited number of teacher and administrator accounts

The Federal Bureau of Prisons (BOP) will utilize the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR Part 12. The Government intends to make a firm-fixed-unit price IDIQ award to the responsible business whose quotation provides the lowest price technically acceptable to the Government.
Proposals will be evaluated for technical acceptability based on the factors and sub-factors set forth below. No tradeoffs between price and non-price factors are permitted. A proposal must receive an "Acceptable" rating in every non-price factor to be eligible for award.
Definitions: For purposes of better understanding the decisions made based upon evaluation criteria selected for the Accreditation Services Contract for FY2026-2030 the evaluation terms for Technical have been defined as follows:
1.0 Technical Acceptability Definitions
For the purposes of this evaluation, the following definitions apply to all Technical Factors:
Acceptable: The proposal meets the minimum requirements of the solicitation and the Statement of Work.
Unacceptable: The proposal fails to meet the minimum requirements of the solicitation or contains deficiencies that increase the risk of unsuccessful contract performance to an unacceptable level.
2.2 Price Evaluation
Price will be the deciding factor among all proposals rated as "Technically Acceptable." The Government will evaluate price for:
1. Total Evaluated Price: Inclusion of the base period and all option years.
2. Reasonableness: Comparison of quotes to ensure the price is fair and reasonable.
3. Realism: Review to ensure the proposed price is not so low as to indicate a risk of performance failure.
Basis for Award: The Government intends to award a contract to the responsible offeror whose proposal is the lowest evaluated price and is technically Acceptable.

This acquisition will be conducted under the procedures of FAR Part 12 -- Commercial Items and FAR Part 13 -- Simplified Acquisition Procedures. This acquisition will utilize price as the only evaluated factor. Award will be made to the vendor with the lowest total evaluated price (TEP) and whose quotation conforms to the solicitation requirements (to include all stated terms, conditions, representations, and certifications). FAR 52.212-2 Addendum, section 1.3).

1.1 Solicitation Requirements (Terms and Conditions)
Vendors are required to meet all solicitation requirements, such as terms and conditions, representations and certifications, and items list requirements, in addition to the price factor. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of the solicitation may result in the vendor being ineligible for award. Vendors must clearly identify any exception to the solicitation terms and conditions and must provide complete supporting rationale. The Government reserves the right to determine any such exceptions unacceptable, and the quotation, therefore, ineligible for award.

This request is to approve a period of performance extension of six months and a total estimated cost (TEC) increase of $260,000 to the active, sole-source Task Order for FOIAXpress Licenses and Technical Support services to Opexus dba Casepoint (UEI: NEFMH93JD5J6). FOIAXpress is a Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) technology product built by Casepoint and is patent-protected and proprietary. There are no authorized resellers of FOIAXpress and the maintenance support services can only be provided by Casepoint. The current contract (7200AA24C00107 which is a Federal Supply Schedule award subject to RFO FAR Part 8.4) for enterprise license and product customizations was initially awarded in September 2024 and was subsequently extended for six months in September 2025 to its current expiration of March 29, 2026. The contract initially provided for 30 licenses for 22 users, covering both Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) and electronic document review (EDR) specialists. Under this proposed extension and TEC increase, only 20 licences will be provided and 14 users will be serviced; the Task Order end date would be September 28, 2026, and the TEC would be increased from $795,293.75 to $1,055,293.75.

The total estimated price of the 6-month task order is estimated at $260,000. All services to be provided under this action are within the scope of the existing task order and the price contractor's awarded GSA MAS contract. The action is accomplished under the authority in FAR 8.405-6(a)(1)(i)(C), as adopted by USAID on a class deviation basis to implement the Revolutionary FAR Overhaul (RFO FAR) in TCD 25-01, Rev 1, on a sole-source basis as a logical follow-on. GSAM 538.71— Federal Supply Schedule Ordering Procedures, specifically GSAM 538.7104-3, Sole Source Justifications, provides the relevant authority for this J&A. Although the regulation appears to contemplate the use of a sole source justification for issuing a new sole source order rather than extending an existing order non-competitively, the practical result is the same; the vendor would receive an additional six-months of performance. Executing a non-competitive extension is significantly more expedient and administratively efficient than issuing a new sole source order. Accordingly, the Agency has determined that applying the same sole source justification to support the non-competitive extension of the existing order is reasonable and appropriate under the circumstances. Only one source is capable of providing the supplies or services required at the level of quality required because the supplies or services are unique or highly specialized. FOIAXpress is a COTS technology product built by Casepoint and is patent-protected and proprietary. There are no authorized resellers of the FOIAXpress and the maintenance support services can only be provided by Casepoint.

Normal duty tasks identified within this work statement shall be accomplished at the Naval Research Laboratory, BPTF located at 10050 Blossom Point Road, Welcome, Maryland, 20693. Place of Performance: Monitoring may occur between Cochiti Dam and Elephant Butte Reservoir in the Cochiti, Angostura, Isleta, and San Acacia reaches.

The primary objective of this contract is to provide up to daily monitoring of river conditions to support river managers in their operations when there is the potential for river intermittency, generally from April to October each year. Monitoring may occur between Cochiti Dam and Elephant Butte Reservoir in the Cochiti, Angostura, Isleta, and San Acacia reaches (see attached map; these areas correspond to the Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District's Cochiti, Albuquerque, Belen, and Socorro Divisions) when gauged flows indicate that river drying is imminent or has already occurred. The majority of the work is anticipated to occur in the Isleta and San Acacia reaches.

The Contractor shall provide all personnel, equipment, tools, materials, vehicles, supervision, and other items and services necessary to safely complete the tasks defined in this Performance Work Statement. The Contractor shall perform to the standards in the contract.

PURPOSE: This SOP establishes guidelines for USAREUR-AF G4 Logistics Automation Division (LAD) Staging Facility operations and the supported customers for issuing and receiving of Tactical Enterprise Logistics Systems (TELS), Logistics Information Systems (LIS), and Commercial off the Shelf Information Technology (COTS IT) equipment for "UNCLASSIFIED" PM fielded U.S. Army Government property and commercial vendors. The USAREUR-AF G4 LAD Staging Facility, located in Miesau, Germany, is the centralized point in Europe for Army Program Managers dealing with TELS, LIS, COTS IT, Life Cycle Replacement (LCR), and shortages fielded to units within the USAREUR-AF Theater of Operations. Equipment is fielded and issued based on the unit's MTOE, TDA, and ATOE authorizations. This facility utilizes Global Combat Support System- Army (GCSS-Army) Property Book, the Army's supply system of record for accountability of transactions between gaining and losing UICs for TELS, LIS, and COTS IT. This ensures an accurate, auditable trail IAW DoD and HQ DAG4 policy. The USAREUR-AF G4 and G6 TELS, LIS, COTS IT system and program managers maintain fully mission capable (FMC) equipment to fill shortages and or mission requirements.

Fermi Research Alliance (FRA) intends to purchase a HPC farm (LQ3) of consisting of Infiniband 52 servers, Luster, and administrative nodes with specifications as detailed in this technical specification document.

provide the labor for this integration work, or
subcontract to a third party;

GCC Computer Rooms have a standard raised floor, providing a 3-foot tall plenum for forced cold-air cooling. Overhead electrical conduits provide two L6-30R (30A, 208V, 2 pole, 3 wire, grounding receptacles) per rack position.

This is a notice of intent to negotiate a sole source contract pursuant to the authority of 41 U.S.C. 1901 as implemented by Revolutionary Federal Acquisition Regulation Overhaul 12.102 "Restricting Competition." The Government intends to negotiate a five-year (5) firm-fixed-price contract with the University of New Hampshire (UNH) for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, National Ocean Service, Office of Coast Survey, for Information Technology Support for NOAA employees and NOAA contractors at the Joint Hydrographic Center located at UNH. This requirement is for annual Information Technology (IT) support for NOAA personnel and/or NOAA contractors working collaboratively with UNH researchers and in facilities provided by and shared with UNH. These personnel require IT services and support that is tightly integrated with the UNH IT infrastructure. Due to the combination of physical and IT security concerns, it is not possible to provide the needed support through a third-party contractor. Areas of service include: computers and services, basic IT services, data storage for high-resilience data; and data storage for bulk data. This action is intended to support the IT needs of 15 NOAA users throughout the period of performance, and up to four additional NOAA users (19 total) in any option years. The North American Industry Classification System code for this requirement is 541519 Other Computer Related Services. The small business size standard for this NAICS code is $34M.

NMCSD is a 24/7 critical medical facility supporting active-duty service members, veterans, and their families. The facility requires an on-site generation system capable of delivering uninterrupted utilities (electrical power, water, steam, compressed air) to critical care areas, operating rooms, and life-support. The MEGS must seamlessly integrate all utility systems without operational downtime, while providing redundant and load-balanced power generation with automated fault detection and correction. The risk of failure to meet these mission critical requirements to the support Naval Medical Center San Diego would pose unacceptable risk to the health and safety of patients and Government personnel. The incumbent contractor, Energy Technology and Service, Inc. (ETS), has been the primary provider of MEGS support. Consequently, ETS has unique and specialized knowledge of the system to ensure mission critical requirements are met. No other contractor could provide the localized knowledge and immediate services necessary to operate and maintain the existing MEGS System without a significant lead time and unacceptable delay. Such a delay would result in serious injury to the Government by creating an undue risk of loss of vital services at the Naval Medical Center San Diego.

Strategic Weapons Facility, Pacific (SWFPAC) requires in-person, instructor-led training for 15 personnel on VMware vSphere. The agency's server infrastructure and software-defined data center (SDDC) are standardized on the VMware vSphere 7 platform. This course provides the essential foundational knowledge for operating and maintaining VMware technologies within a software-defined data center environment. Because vSphere is the core virtualization platform upon which most other VMware solutions depend, this training is critical to ensuring SWFPAC personnel can effectively support, troubleshoot, and optimize the command's virtual infrastructure.

2. SCOPE
The contractor shall provide and deliver the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) authorized commercial training course, VMware vSphere: Install, Configure, Manage [V7]. The purpose of this training is to provide SWFPAC employees with the specific skills needed to manage the agency's standardized VMware virtual infrastructure.

The contractor shall provide a complete training package that includes the following:
2.1 Training Session: A single, five-day, in-person, instructor-led training session conducted onsite at SWFPAC facilities for up to fifteen (15) students.
2.2 One (1) currently certified VMware Instructor (VCI) to lead the training.

2. BACKGROUND AND ASSUMPTIONS
The RSC requires secure, high-performance signal extension and switching capabilities to support critical missions. The organization's goal is to accelerate mission design and ground integration, launch and on-orbit operations, ground system tests, and rapid fielding to provide reliable, low-cost access to space in support of the warfighter. Ground support maintains infrastructure, systems, IT, cybersecurity and integration activities that are critical to day-to-day mission execution.
The Jaguar mission is currently managed out of the Jaguar Program Management Office but is subject to change in the future. The contractor under this task order is responsible for the sustainment of the Jaguar ground system and supporting infrastructure at the Satellite Operations Center (SOC) and Payload Operations Center (POC). Contractor direction, guidance, and oversight will be provided by the Government Task Order (TO) lead.
The Jaguar mission resides within the RSC and occupies Payload Test Center (PTC) 2, 3, & 4. Personnel supporting Jaguar that require access to the POC require a Top Secret clearance with SCI eligibility. SCI clearance must be obtained within 120 days of hiring. The contractor will appoint a Task Order Manager to manage the work under this PWS to interface with the Government team. Travel (CONUS and OCONUS) is estimated to be <5 days per quarter.

1. Description of Services
The Hybrid Architecture and Development for Experimental Systems (HADES) contract is a procurement for prototype and operational satellite ground systems, satellite operations centers (SOCs), and support services. The contractor shall perform satellite ground system and satellite operations center engineering, development, integration, and sustainment, and associated support services primarily at, but not limited to, the Research, Development, Test, and Evaluation (RDT&E) Support Complex (RSC) at Kirtland Air Force Base, Albuquerque, New Mexico. The organization's goal is to accelerate mission design and ground integration, launch and on-orbit operations, ground system tests, and rapid fielding to provide reliable, low-cost access to space in support of the warfighter.

1.1. Background
1.1.1. The Research Development Space and Missile Operations (RDSMO) program provides research, development, and test operations for assigned Department of Defense (DoD) experimental, demonstration, and prototype satellites, space vehicles, satellite ground control systems, tracking systems, and commercial applications of these systems.

The Board of Assessors of the Town of Wellesley, MA invites proposals from qualified vendors for upgrading its existing Computer Assisted Mass Appraisal (CAMA) system pursuant to the guidelines of the Massachusetts Department of Revenue. The Board of Assessors currently utilizes the WinAppraisal valuation system version 4.0.10, developed by JF Ryan Associates, Inc. This system is based on SQL Server with custom reports and user interfaces built in Microsoft Access. The primary objectives of this project are to: Upgrade the current CAMA system: Enhance the functionality and performance of the existing system to ensure the new system meets the current and future needs of the Board of Assessors and meets all CAMA guidelines as required by the Massachusetts Department of Revenue, Division of Local Services/Bureau of Local Assessment. Ensure compatibility: Update the system to be compatible with the latest software and hardware standards, reducing risks associated with outdated or incompatible network software technology or connectivity. Improve user experience: The ability to develop customizable and user-friendly interfaces, improve workflow efficiency within the Assessor's Office, reduce the time required for data entry and retrieval, and enhance electronic data input from external sources, such as other Town Departments and Norfolk County Registry of Deeds.

Custom Reports and User Interfaces: Use the system to develop and integrate custom reports and user interfaces as per the Board's specifications to enhance usability, meet all Massachusetts Department of Revenue requirements for Certification of values, and address the requirements of the Mass-Appraisal Standards of the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP) of the Appraisal Foundation. The MSCB platform has demonstrated exponential growth, scaling from 100,000-user prototype OTA agreements in 2018 to a full production OTA agreement in 2020 that serves over three million DoW users. This follow-on recompete to the production OTA agreements highlights the platform's ongoing success and evolution.

Given the importance of the CBII platform in protecting the DoDIN, the Government will require future demonstrative test pilots or evidence-based success to ensure any non-Menlo Security, Inc. based CBII solution is rigorously tested to validate the efficacy of any stated solution in off-loading network-clogging commercial traffic, protecting against cyber threats in a secured cloud container, and ensuring their environment can manage the services of the DoW enterprise constituting up to as many as 3.6M DoW users, while integrating AI/ML in their cloud solution for early detection of zero-day threats.

The Government anticipates there will be an increase in the number of companies offering RBI technologies, similar to MSCB, that will have matured and may be capable of providing the CBII requirements. As these companies continue to expand their RBI offerings, the Government further believes that a follow-on contract at the conclusion of this contract (i.e. in approximately five years) would be better postured to evaluate and deploy a CBII environment that does not necessarily include MSCB.

MSCB is currently deployed and integrated within the Department of Defense Information Network (DODIN) and DISA requires the ability to maintain current systems while meeting future system and network capacity and performance requirements. The CBII solution must be interoperable and compatible with the DISN Joint Infrastructure (DJI) security stack located at DISA's ten global IAPs; must comply with DoW mandates for supporting IPv6 network traffic; and must adhere to risk management assessment and authorization requirements. The current capability, MSCB, complies with these requirements.

This follow-on effort focuses on maintaining and enhancing this system by continuing to utilize Menlo Security, Inc.'s cloud environment and adding smart, automated defenses (using artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML)) to proactively block cyberattacks, ensuring a strong and reliable defense against web-based threats. As of December 2025, DISA has successfully migrated 3.05M DoW users to the CBII environment with MSCB. This migration was implemented over 3.5 years and encompassed approximately 70 DoW organizations, which involved extensive coordination, training, testing, organizational collaboration, technical synchronization, and end-user validation. The complexity and scope of replacing the existing MSCB would result in major disruptions to DoW cyber operations, posing a significant threat to national security.

4.8.1 Transition-In Plan

4.8.1.1 The Contractor shall provide a Transition-In Plan (Section 2.2, Deliverable 16) at the Kickoff Meeting. The agenda shall be provided no later than three working days prior to the kick-off meeting. The Contractor shall ensure that there will be minimum service disruption to vital Government business and no service degradation during and after transition.

The Transition-In period shall begin at project start and shall conclude within 30 days of project start. The Contractor shall provide an updated Transition-In Plan, based on the Contractor's draft plan submitted with the proposal, to be approved by the Government. The Contractor shall identify how it will coordinate with the incumbent Contractor and Government personnel to transfer knowledge regarding the following:
a) Project management processes
b) Points of contact
c) Location of technical and project management documentation
d) Status of ongoing technical initiatives
e) Transition of key personnel
f) Schedules and milestones
g) Actions required of the Government
h) Coordination of IT related programs, issues

4.8.1.2 The Contractor shall also establish and maintain effective communication with the incumbent Contractor and Government personnel for the period of the transition via weekly status meetings per the COR's direction.

4.8.1.3 Incumbent employees may be interviewed for possible employment by the incoming Contractor; however, the Contractor will have the right of refusal.

This Request for Information (RFI) is issued by the State University of New York at Stony Brook, Division of Information Technology Enterprise Applications & Integrations (hereinafter referred to as "SUNY") to collect detailed information on the development, hosting, and maintenance of a middleware service to enable transactional data flow between Tripleseat and 25Live via API deliverables. This RFI is intended to gauge the interest and gather insights from experienced API specialists. The goal is to explore new technologies and solutions available in the market that can streamline and enhance the processes at SUNY Stony Brook. University Conferences and Special Events currently operate or utilize two separate platforms, Tripleseat and 25Live, for event-related processes. However, both platforms must be manually updated to exchange booking information for events and venue reservations. Conference Operations would find it more efficient to enter the data into either of these systems and have the data entry updated and synchronized between them. 25Live is SUNY internal, campus-wide scheduling platform, which is integrated with campus operations. 25Live is used for rooms reservation and this system is not an event management tool. Tripleseat is used for event planning and execution but does not book or reserve venues for an event.

Architects and Subject Matter Experts (SME) are expected to provide the technical guidance required to ensure the eA3 project system is maintained at the current or improved level of performance. These resources will be shared across all eA3 projects to provide support for shared infrastructure and to make the best use of knowledge and skills across the projects. Architects and SMEs will work closely with testers, developers, configuration management personnel, security experts, deployment engineers, and IRS personnel. • Technical inputs on development activities for ensuring high quality products through the implementation of COTS hardware and software in addition to custom software code.
• Enterprise, system architecture, and engineering support for the operational life cycle of a system in support of performance improvements, major changes and integrations.
• Troubleshoot eA3 applications to discover the root cause of issues and provide an analysis report on findings as needed.

4.1 INVESTIGATION PHASE – 3 Months

The Contractor shall conduct a comprehensive investigation of the FMCS Microsoft 365 environment during the initial three-month period. This phase runs concurrently with the O&M Phase.

4.1.1 Investigation Activities

Kickoff meeting within one (1) week of award.

Evaluate the current M365 environment (SharePoint, Teams, Power Apps, Power Pages, Power BI, LMS365, Collabspace, Dataverse, SQL, Azure Services).

Evaluate incomplete tasks from previous vendor.

Conduct interviews and review processes to identify issues and errors.

Collect requirements for new features and applications.

Submit documentation and present findings.

Provide modernization and mitigation recommendations, including data storage recommendations (SharePoint lists, Dataverse, SQL).

4.2 OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE PHASE – 12 Months (Concurrent)

This phase will run concurrently with ongoing operations and maintenance activities and is anticipated to be approximately three (3) months in duration.

The investigation phase includes evaluating incomplete tasks from the previous vendor, reviewing existing processes to identify issues and errors, and delivering modernization recommendations. The Contractor shall also review incomplete or ongoing work from previous contractors and provide a roadmap for future enhancements and system improvements.

4.3.6 Transition support.

This contract includes a Transition-In and Transition-Out period. The Transition process is defined as a transition of information and operations from one Contractor to another to maintain the program's integrity. The Government requires a seamless transition. The Contractor shall implement a transition methodology that ensures Contractor personnel and (if applicable) subcontractor personnel provide a seamless, risk mitigated, and effective transition. During the transition periods, the Contractor shall ensure that supported services are maintained without disruption to stakeholders and that there is no service degradation during the transition. The Contractor shall establish and maintain effective communication with Government personnel during the transition periods via weekly status meetings or other Government-approved transition communication methodology. The Transition Plan must be approved by the Government. The Government will provide comments on the plan within ten (10) business days after the plan is received. The Contractor shall submit a final plan for approval.

The Contractor is required to submit with its proposal a draft Transition-In Plan, and an updated version submitted no later than 15 days after contract award based on Government review and feedback. The Transition-In Plan shall describe the Contractor's transition activities in obtaining appropriate contract deliverables from the incumbent Contractor, including documentation, data, and licensing.

PWD's GIS Unit was established in 1996 with the remit of owning and maintaining the Department's geospatial data. In 2001, the unit undertook its first major initiative by scanning, attributing, and spatially indexing the Department's 250,000 engineering drawings. This resulted in the development of PWD's Engineering Record Viewer (ERV) application, which allowed Departmental users to search for and view scanned drawings. Data conversion followed, beginning in 2004, in which PWD's linear assets were converted to GIS features, resulting in the creation of six geometric networks for water, wastewater, stormwater, raw water, high pressure fire system, and green stormwater infrastructure. These networks were incorporated into ERV, with subsequent versions of the application further expanding its functionalities and the data available to Departmental users. Today, a daily average of 250 PWD employees with diverse skillsets and requirements interact with the Department's GIS data via ERV.

PWD's GIS datasets are maintained continuously by a team of editors in the GIS Unit, tracking the full lifecycle of every asset, and are made available to users across the Department for a wide range of purposes via ERV and other applications. The data are used for hydraulic modeling; flood risk modeling and sewer planning; and linear asset planning and risk-based pipe renewal asset planning.

4.8.1.1 The Contractor shall provide a Transition-In Plan at the Kickoff Meeting. The agenda shall be provided no later than three working days prior to the kick-off meeting. The Contractor shall ensure that there will be minimum service disruption to vital Government business and no service degradation during and after transition.

The Contractor shall provide an updated Transition-In Plan, based on the Contractor's draft plan submitted with the proposal, to be approved by the Government. The Contractor shall identify how it will coordinate with the incumbent Contractor and Government personnel to transfer knowledge regarding the following:
a) Project management processes
b) Points of contact
c) Location of technical and project management documentation
d) Status of ongoing technical initiatives
e) Transition of key personnel
f) Schedules and milestones
g) Actions required of the Government
h) Coordination of IT related programs, issues

4.8.2 Transition-Out Plan
4.8.2.1 The Contractor shall provide a Transition-Out Plan within six months of project start. The Government will work with the Contractor to finalize the plan. The plan shall facilitate the accomplishment of a seamless transition from the incumbent to an incoming Contractor (if applicable). The Contractor shall continue to accomplish all the tasks as outlined in the contract during this period. Additionally, the transition plan shall be reviewed and updated quarterly during Option Period 4, should the contract reach that option period.

The State of Washington is in the process of developing specifications for Mainframe COBOL Development support and is requesting input from the vendor community. OFM operates a legacy enterprise core processing platform, which includes AFRS (Agency Financial Reporting System) and CAMS (Capital Asset Management System), built on an IBM z/OS mainframe environment. AFRS has been in production for over four decades and supports mission-critical business accounting functions including but not limited to transaction processing and batch data integrations with internal and external stakeholders. AFRS and CAMS consist primarily of COBOL programs running under CICS for online transaction processing and batch workloads managed via JCL. Data persistence is handled through DB2, VSAM and sequential datasets. Supporting utilities and integrations leverage tools such as SORT, IDCAMS, FTP, and various scheduling and job control frameworks. AFRS and CAMS interface with distributed applications through file transfers and MQ messaging. Over time, AFRS has undergone incremental enhancements to meet evolving business and regulatory requirements. However, significant portions of the codebase remain monolithic and tightly coupled, with very limited documentation and institutional knowledge concentrated among a small number of senior technical resources approaching retirement.

For code developed under Tasks 1 through 3, the following specifications shall be met: 1. All code shall be covered with unit tests to at least 95% to verify functionality and to guard against regressions from future refactoring, extension, and/or bug fixes. 2. All code shall be supplemented by software test benches that enable simulation and unit testing without the need for hardware. Testing code may be written in Matlab or Python. 3. All code shall be documented by appropriate comments and naming conventions (in addition to Task 2 above.) 4. Proprietary or closed-source software, including FPGA IP cores, shall not be used unless the Contractor has previously verified NIST already owns such IP cores. 5. Code shall be organized to be reviewed by the Government on a regular basis, as specified in the deliverable table (Section IV).
