This code shall be compatible with the existing firmware and custom NIST real-time processor for optical two-way time transfer. 7. All project software being developed shall be maintained in a suitable repository (e.g., GitHub) to allow Government to monitor development progress and provide rapid feedback when changes are needed. 8. The documentation shall include a description of the basic algorithms and their implementation in the DSP or FPGA. The primary justification for this sole-source extension is the alignment with NASA's acquisition strategy and the imminent transition to the Next Generation Scalable Data Engineering, Operations, and Informatics Support for Open Science (Next Gen) contract. The Next Gen acquisition, which is being conducted under full and open competition, is scheduled to begin its phase-in on April 30, 2026. Attempting to award a new, standalone contract at this time would be impractical and contrary to the Government's best interest for the following reasons: Ensuring Continuity of Operations During Transition — The current effort directly supports mission-critical Earth Science data visualization and exploration capabilities. Disrupting this work by introducing a new contractor would jeopardize continuity during the transition period, creating operational risk and potential delays in meeting stakeholder requirements. Time Constraints and Integration Risks — The extension period is limited to three months, with an optional one-month contingency. Any new contractor would require significant time to gain sufficient understanding of the existing infrastructure and deliverables. This timeline is incompatible with the short-term nature of the extension and the scheduled phase-in of Next Gen. While Element 84's unique technical expertise and familiarity with the existing system reinforce this determination, the overriding factor is that the Government has already committed to a competitive follow-on acquisition. Maintaining Element 84 for this brief extension ensures a seamless transition, minimizes risk, and upholds the Government's acquisition strategy.

The Period of Performance (PoP) for this contract will consist of a base year of one (1) year, and two (2) additional option periods, each structured as a six (6)-month extension. The base period will commence on July 1, 2026, and will end on June 30, 2027. 1.6 VERSION UPGRADE
The contractor shall provide the expertise necessary for the upgrade of Maximo Asset Management from one version to the next. Data migration is an essential part of the upgrade and is the responsibility of the contractors. Once the upgraded has been completed it is the contractor's responsibility to ensure end to end testing is conducted. Any error that results in Maximo being substantially or completely nonfunctional or inoperative the contractor will make reasonable efforts to correct or provide a work-around solution for errors. A final resolution to work-arounds shall be completed within 15 business days. The contractor shall prepare a work-up plan that will be evaluated and accepted prior to the start of the upgrade. The contractor shall submit to the Contracting Officer or the NIST Maximo Administrator by e-mail, the upgrade plan in an electronic Word document. The NIST Maximo Administrator shall review the upgrade plan and provide a written response within 10 working days. No work shall start on the actual upgrade until the plan is reviewed and approved.

The Office of Naval Intelligence (ONI) has a requirement for evaluation-driven, mission-aligned artificial intelligence (AI) applications and systems that accelerate analyst workflows in areas critical to national security. The purpose of this Sources Sought announcement, a market survey for written information only, is to identify sources capable of providing support and expertise for this effort. The following items are expected requirements for this effort:

1. Production and Delivery
The Contractor shall provide a phased AI delivery program built around the following ONI objectives that are a continuation and further development of work performed under SBIR Phase III FA259520D0001:
- Further model development includes data collection, model training, and preliminary validation steps to ensure the model's foundational performance is aligned with ONI requirements. Further development shall adhere to state-of-the-art methodologies and align with mission goals.
- The Contractor shall establish a model evaluation score to be used to evaluate the initial model's performance based on benchmarks provided by ONI.
- Develop cold-start evaluators, including tools such as large-language models as judges and heuristic rules, to provide initial assessment of the model's performance and identify areas requiring further refinement.
- Extend the model to additional mission threads by modifying to handle new types of data, adjusting algorithms, and ensuring computability with a broader range of operational conditions.

The FAR 52.217-8 clause will increase the Task Order not-to-exceed value from $35,120,045.81 to $52,680,068.71. These services ensure uninterrupted support for mission-critical applications supporting acquisition, e-commerce, lifecycle management, and digital experience functions. The importance of these applications cannot be overstated, as they are central to the operational continuity and effectiveness of federal procurement activities. A failure or significant disruption within these systems would have negative impacts which are described in section IV of this SSJ.

REQUIREMENT DESCRIPTION:
Minimum Viable Product Requirements:
NCIS is seeking to conduct a proof of concept for a minimally viable product (MVP) to show a proof of concept for future CMS development. The Government intends to award multiple contracts in a phased approach; an initial contract for the proof-of-concept and a follow-on contract for the implementation of the enterprise-wide CMS. The base period of the initial contract will consist of a two (2) month proof of concept period to support functional and technical review of the Offeror's solution. The focus for this initial phase will be to provide a 'new' user interface (UI) that works concurrently with NCIS' existing data structure. Users will have the ability to use both the existing UI and the 'new' UI during the contract period.

Vendors will be given an opportunity to prove out the scalability and accuracy of their illegal transshipment solutions over the course of a no-cost, 180-day base period of performance. Scalability will be measured by the number of actionable referral packages vendors submit to CBP. Accuracy will be measured by the percentage of referral packages that lead to the prevention of high-risk imports from entering the U.S. and/or the identification of additional duties owed. CBP will have full discretion over which referral packages to investigate based on confidence levels, potential revenue impact, ongoing or previous enforcement actions, and resource availability. Only referral packages that CBP chooses to investigate and act upon will be factored into its assessment of accuracy.

Vendors who achieve the base period of performance milestones defined in the SOW may be invited to perform up to two option periods with CBP. If a vendor successfully achieves all of the base period performance thresholds, CBP may, in its discretion, exercise the first option period. If the first option period is exercised and the vendor successfully achieves the performance thresholds for option period 1, then CBP may, in its discretion, exercise the second option period. The proposed pricing for each respective option period must fall within the range of $1,250,000 or less to be considered for award. Price proposals outside of this range will be deemed unacceptable.

The proposed contract will provide services to operate and maintain the MEGS for the Navy Medical Center San Diego (NMCSD) within the NAVFAC SW area of responsibility (AOR). The period of performance for the proposed contract is an extension for 6-months. The MEGS is an Iconics Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition System (SCADA). MEGS provides a control system that continuously monitors the operation and performance of the NMCSD Co-Generation (COGEN) utility system. The COGEN system provides continuous, reliable, on-site utilities (electrical power, water, steam, compressed air) for the NMCSD. The system includes generation units, control systems, automatic transfer switching, emissions and compliance equipment, and integration with NMCSD's existing electrical infrastructure. Estimated Dollar Value: The estimated dollar value of this contract action is approximately $255,000.00 based on the Independent Government Estimate (IGE), utilizing historical data, input from subject matter experts, and market research. The software development for CDM should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities:
• Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes.
• Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

 Management information including financials (invoices submitted or approved, estimate at completion, etc.) and other productivity data (e.g., velocity, burn down chart, burn up chart, etc.)

The software development for Case Development Modernization should be anchored in SAFe Agile framework and DevSecOps methodologies. The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. Automated testing, continuous integration, and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines will be central to this framework, enabling quick iterations and reducing certification and Authorization to Operate (ATO) delays. By aligning with modern software practices, Case Development Modernization (CDM) will ensure that value is delivered early and continuously throughout the lifecycle. CDM delivery includes, but is not limited to, the following activities and responsibilities: Identification and definition of case development product requirements (e.g. lightweight capability definitions, user-stories) based on both technical documentation and User-Feedback obtained through Human Centered Design and Agile processes. Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs. Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI)). Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.

The nature of this action being approved is to bridge contract to the Agile Web Presence II (AWP II) Firm-Fixed Price (FFP) contract, GSA Number 47QTCA19D00CY, Contract number HM0476-20-F-0193, to extend the period of performance and increase the contract value. The scope for the AWP-II contract was determined to be necessary to support national security and to protect life and property, both in the continental United States and overseas. The current contract is set to expire on 29 March 2026 and an extension of the current contract is required to serve as a bridge until a follow-on competitive contract can be awarded. The AWP-II program provides continued operations and sustainment to the AWP web-based capability known as 'The Globe', an enterprise-wide, centralized web presence for discovery of NGA products, services, and tools, m.Globe, and Discovery Catalog on the Unclassified, NIPR, SIPR, JWICS, CAPNET, and Stoneghost domains within the IC. 'The Globe' collectively supports 178,000+ unique users across multiple security domains in their discovery of mission-critical geospatial intelligence for the Department of War (DoW), Intelligence Community (IC), National System for Geospatial-Intelligence (NSG), and Allied System for Geospatial-Intelligence (ASG) through the integration of geospatial programs and services. In accordance with FAR 8.405-6(a)(1)(i)(C), the required scope to the AWPII contract represents a logical follow-on to the original order in the interest of economy and efficiency. This requirement will have a Period of Performance (POP) that consistent of a 12-month Base Period and two (2) 6-month Optional Periods. Total POP will be for 24 months.

3.2 (U) Software Development
(U) The Contractor shall provide software development services to ensure the definition, design, implementation, and testing of the software to meet the functional and performance of new or augmented mission requirements while maximizing the effectiveness and efficiency. These new capabilities might include new sensor data integration or a new capability transitioned at "Initial Operational Capability (IOC)" that requires some development to reach "Full Operational Capability (FOC)" capability maturity. These services include the following:
• Sprint/Release planning
• Software coding
• Software integration
• Software unit testing and reporting (CDRL-A009, CDRL-A010)
• Software deployment to operations (CDRL-A008)
• Software enterprise/user testing

(U) Software development services shall, to the maximum extent possible, follow the established NGA Agile software development processes and procedures. This includes an Agile requirements management system using the current Scaled Agile Framework for all work performed, and the integration of Contractor development requirements (story and features) with the higher-level government requirements system (Program Epics and Solution Epics in USMS Jira on COE). The Contractor shall maintain an USMS Jira project on COE as the authoritative source of truth for all system work, ensuring that Features reflect current information and status.

(U) Software testing data (plans, procedures, and reports) shall, to the maximum extent possible, utilize modern digital engineering tools. The Test Plan shall include test objectives with traceability to user acceptance criteria at the feature level.

5.0 SPECIFIC TASKS AND DELIVERABLES

The Contractor shall use an Agile DevSecOps approach to continuously deliver value across the LIPAS/VISION product to provide greater user visibility, clearly defined technical infrastructure, improved capabilities, as well as information security and accessibility as directed by VA Stakeholders. The Contractor shall provide expertise and support for Products and Software. The Contractor shall provide product performance management and Agile program management to improve quality and performance of features and value delivery. The Contractor shall provide support for centralized OIT intake and management of business and technical needs and requirements. The Contractor shall provide architecture support and design reviews to support operational value streams and all activities that span product objectives.

The Contractor shall comply with IT Development Security Operations (DevSecOps) methodologies for LIPAS in accordance with the working principles and definitions provided in Section 5.0. The Contractor shall provide dedicated teams of cross-functional staff to operate, maintain, and enhance the LIPAS/VISION platform and all associated interfaces with capabilities to streamline systems, improve scalability, increase availability of services, and enable more efficient delivery.

2.3.1 The Contractor shall perform software maintenance based upon priorities established by the Government in accordance with AGILE principles and in accordance with the Agile Development Management Plan (ADMP) (CDRL AXXX).

a) Corrective – System changes to correct error and operational degradation.
• Troubleshooting and fixing minor application bugs, including system restarts.
• Reconfiguring Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) for specific user / use case errors.
• Working with ISP, CSP and other system admins to resolve Discrepancy Reports (DRs) caused by external factors that arise in non-AWP capabilities.
• Fast, one-time fixes (aka "hot fixes") that do not involve agile software development, backlog creation, or using the more formal development and sustainment processes described in other service definitions.
• Restoring application or system services that are down.
b) Preventative - System changes to prevent degradation of service.
c) Adaptive – System changes to address changing operational environments
• Modify/enhance software code, including UX/UI, baseline in support of new/emerging software/system requirements in accordance with the Agile Development Management Plan (CDRL A003).
d) Perfective – System changes to optimize operations and performance and comply with relevant policy and directive.
• Modify/enhance software code baseline, including UX/UI, in support of new/emerging software/system requirements and design changes in accordance with the Agile Development Management Plan (CDRL A003).

THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY - EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

SOFTWARE METRICS
To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster and more consistently. To measure progress toward this intent, NGA will track three key metrics for each software product: availability, lead time for changes, and deployment frequency. Additionally, each software product is expected to have its own product-specific metrics to track how well the product is working for its users. Together, these metrics balance speed and reliability and describe a product's impact and value.

The metrics and elements in this document, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," serve as an implementation guide to supplement NGA's Technology Strategy and provide additional specific guidance for teams building and operating software.

For software teams using NGA's enterprise-managed version control and Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipeline, deployment frequency and lead time for changes will be automatically tracked within those services' dashboards. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years. DORA research shows high-performing teams deploy at least once per week.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users
Once user needs are deeply understood, the team must decide which of those needs to prioritize. To do this, the team must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. The product manager must break down the MVP into actionable work items that are easily understood by the entire team. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product.

Each day a product isn't actively solving user needs in production increases the risk the team is building the wrong thing. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years. If the initially defined MVP cannot be delivered to real users in production within single-digit months, it must be broken down further.

13. Iterate and Improve Continually
Products and services are never "finished." Getting real people using your product as early as possible, monitoring the product's performance, and making improvements throughout the lifetime of the product enable teams to adapt quickly. Making improvements means more than doing basic maintenance like fixing defects in code, deploying security patches, and keeping runbooks up to date; continual improvement means you can capture and respond to changes in user needs, technology, or government policy throughout the lifetime of the product.
Iteration isn't just for the early stages of a product's development; a team must be able to make substantial improvements throughout a product's lifetime. As product complexity grows, teams must focus on balancing paying down technical debt with new feature development, ensuring the product continues to be adaptable over time to respond rapidly to user needs.
Questions to Answer
► How has your product evolved in the past six months? ► What is your usability testing process and cadence?
► What are the biggest upcoming changes, and why are ► What changes have you made to the product based on they needed? usability testing?
► Can you share your product roadmap?
CONCLUSION
The above metrics and 13 elements, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," ensure each NGA software product has the necessary people, processes, and tools in place to successfully deliver. Together, they enable NGA to consistently build, release, and operate software that meets our users' needs and expectations and deliver on our mission.

• Collaborate with vendor(s) and IT organization(s) to address project interdependencies,

A NOTE FROM THE CTO

To deliver on its current and future mission, NGA needs to deliver useful software faster and more consistently. To do this, the NGA Technology Strategy describes changes in approach, tools, and behavior that will result in faster iterations, more flexible products, and better-satisfied users and customers. The NGA Software Way is an implementation guide to supplement NGA's Technology Strategy and provide additional specific guidance for teams building and operating software.

Our intent is to get to a place where NGA software products are designed and built with users, can be automatically updated in production daily, and use product-specific metrics to make decisions. We believe in small, empowered, cross-functional teams that deeply understand their users and constantly work directly with those users to deliver on their mission.

Implementing The NGA Software Way won't happen all at once; we must start with iteratively tracking and improving these metrics and meeting each element below. As we do this together, and with an understanding of this intent, we will successfully deliver software products with the speed, accuracy, and precision NGA's mission demands.

The three initial metrics all programs must track for each software product delivered to users are availability, lead time for changes, and deployment frequency. It is important to note that NGA is not unique in using these metrics. These are three of the primary metrics DevOps Research and Assessment (DORA) has studied over the past six years. These metrics provide visibility into how NGA balances speed (deployment frequency and lead time for changes) and stability (availability) during product development and operations.

2. Deeply Understand User Needs
Before beginning technical work, a product manager and team must engage with real and/or expected users to identify and understand users' underlying needs, as well as learn how the product or service will fit into the users' work. This is not just a government product manager (or anyone else) hearing users say what they "want." It requires deeply understanding their mission and goals as well as current approaches. Only by understanding the existing state and the reasoning behind it can the team design and engineer a solution.

Human-centered design involves engaging with users to understand their problems and concerns. Methods for doing so include interviews, usability testing, and prototyping. The identified user needs, including KPIs and key findings, must be regularly shared with stakeholders and kept in NGA's enterprise-managed collaboration tools.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users
Teams must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product. Market research must be conducted to understand if a solution already exists inside or outside of NGA that sufficiently meets user needs or can be easily altered to do so. Only after thorough analysis of the existing landscape can software product teams confidently determine whether to buy, augment, or build a solution to address user needs.

VETERAN FOCUSED INTEGRATION PROCESS (VIP) AND PRODUCT LINE MANAGEMENT (PLM)

The Contractor shall support VA efforts IAW the updated Veteran-Focused Integration Process (VIP) and Product Line Management (PLM) Playbook. The major focus of VIP is on Enterprise Oversight, Governance, reporting, and establishing practices that focus on outcomes and continuously delivering sustainable IT capabilities to benefit the Veteran. PLM is a framework that focuses on delivering functional products that provide the highest priority work to customers while delivering simplified, reliable, and practical solutions to the business, medical staff, and our Veterans. The VIP Guide is a companion guide to the PLM Transformation Playbook and can be found at: https://www.voa.va.gov/DocumentView.aspx?DocumentID=4371 and the PLM Transformation Playbook can be found at https://www.voa.va.gov/DocumentView.aspx?DocumentID=4946. VIP and PLM are the authoritative processes that IT projects must follow to ensure development and delivery of IT products.

The PLM Transformation Playbook details how OIT leadership expects Product Lines (PLs) to implement PLM, DevOps, and Lean-Agile principles, methods, practices, and techniques through levels of maturity. The PLM Transformation Playbook applies to all Information Technology (IT) products aligned to PLs in the Software Product Management, Infrastructure Operations and Product Engineering organizations within the OIT organization.

C.3.8.4 Software Development and Technical Labor Support

The contractor shall provide Software Development and Technical Labor Support for the NextGen Passport Personalization Printers within the Non-Production Environment (NPE), passport operations, and support for CA service desk tickets. 

The contractor shall: 

Provide support for the Print Controller software to successfully communicate with the TDIS. 

Develop and support the application to enable Passport staff to perform Government Acceptance Testing (GAT) for personalization printers in a standalone environment independent of the Print Controller and TDIS. This application will have an easy-to-use graphical user interface (GUI) and can run on a separate PC or laptop that can be connected directly to the personalization printers. The application will be able to accept the passport print jobs in XML format, perform chip and coding, engrave on the personalization page, and print the endorsement page.

Work with the Government to establish virtual development and testing environments on the NPE.

Fix performance, security, and code issues identified by the Government in NPE. The Government plans to use static code analysis to identify issues and will work with the Contractor to determine required fixes before moving to IV&V.

Design and develop new functions based on new user stories, e.g., encryption, page refresh, and labeling.

Develop comprehensive test cases and test scenarios, and perform end to end integration, usability and performance testing working with the support site location team to ensure robustness of software, and highest quality and accurate production of passport books.

Assist in supporting GitLab updates, logging defects in ELM, and work with CA Security in scanning and remediation, and enhancing any user level documentation.

C.3.4 Development Methodologies
The Contractor shall support a Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA), a flexible set of design principles applied during system development and integration phases, as specified at the TOs level.

Any SOA-based architecture shall be deployed on a secure, scalable, interoperable, and dynamic platform that provides end-to-end visibility and manageability – from application services through networking components – and supports use across multiple domains.

For HUD-specific TOs, the Contractor shall support HUD efforts in accordance with the following framework and processes:

Visual Innovation Platform (VIP) and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM):
 VIP enhances collaboration and decision-making through visual tools (e.g. digital concept boards, etc.).
 PLM serves as a central hub for data, connecting engineering, design, manufacturing, and supply chain teams.
 VIP and PLM work together to integrate workflows and drive digital transformation:
o VIP connects to PLM, pulling real-time product data to create interactive visual workspaces.
o Teams use VIP's visual boards to ideate, review, and make decisions, which then updates the central PLM system.
 This integration bridges strategic vision (VIP) with operational execution (PLM), accelerating innovation and information technology (IT) maturity.

The Contractor shall perform duties consistent with processes defined in the HUD Process Asset Library (PAL), a centralized digital repository that stores and shares process-related resources (templates, guidelines, policies, checklists, lessons learned, and metrics).

For HUD-specific TOs, the Contractor shall follow the HUD PAL, PLM, and VIP processes to ensure compliance with HUD policies, regulations, and quality standards. The Contractor shall use an incremental development methodology such as Agile (e.g. Scrum, Kanban, etc.), unless otherwise specified at the TOs level.

C.3.1.5 Project Management 

The Contractor shall provide project schedules to be integrated with CA/CST's Integrated Master Schedule (IMS). IMS aggregates individual schedule inputs from multiple projects to aid sequencing project tasks in an optimal order to meet CA/CST's overall priorities. The IMS is a vehicle for analysis and management of dependencies between projects, resource utilization, identification of resource constraints, and prioritization of activities across the enterprise.

Note: Management of the CST IMS is not within the scope of this contract. However, submission of project status into the IMS is a requirement and within scope. The Contractor shall maintain and update each project schedule to reflect progress after each COR/GTM status update, review, or milestone change. 

CST's SDLC process and approach for IT project management and execution incorporates SAFe Agile practices. Industry experience has shown these practices, when implemented correctly, produce higher quality products. CA has adopted a SAFe Agile approach for operations, maintenance, and engineering projects, and expects operations of CA/CST services to flow from this approach. The Contractor shall adhere to the CA/CST practices and guidelines accordingly.

THE NGA SOFTWARE WAY ELEMENTS

To consistently track these metrics and realize the above intent, the rest of this document provides a set of elements NGA and contractor teams must meet while delivering software products. These metrics and elements, collectively referred to as "The NGA Software Way," ensure each software product has the people, processes, and tools in place to effectively operate at NGA. They apply to any team building a new product or modifying one already in service, whether making small changes, adding large new features, or creating entirely new products. The NGA Software Way applies to software products of all phases, including existing software products across the agency (not simply new products). Except where noted below, these elements must also be used when evaluating and implementing commercial software products for use at NGA. We understand that for large, existing software products, implementing The NGA Software Way won't happen all at once; it should start with tracking the metrics above and iteratively meeting each of the elements below.

3. Prioritize Rapidly Delivering Value to Users

Once user needs are deeply understood, the team must decide which of those needs to prioritize. To do this, the team must define a minimum viable product (MVP), either in written form or, ideally, as a set of wireframes, and then share it with key stakeholders, customers, and users. The MVP is the smallest actual deliverable that can be reviewed by real customers to get feedback on if it solves a need and to test assumptions, learn quickly, and decrease risk upfront. The product manager must break down the MVP into actionable work items that are easily understood by the entire team. Teams must gather feedback as early as possible using mockups or interactive prototypes. As feedback is gathered, the prototype's fidelity should mature, and the team must transition to testing with the real product.

Each day a product isn't actively solving user needs in production increases the risk the team is building the wrong thing. Teams must prioritize rapidly getting a product to actual users in production to learn what works and how to successfully iterate; this first capability must be delivered in months, not years.

SPECIAL NOTICE FOR SB SET-ASIDE:
This RFP is a total small business set-aside. Offerors are strongly encouraged to ensure that the offeror is a small business based on the size standard for the NAICS Code assigned to this RFP. Reference 13 CFR Part 121, and the clauses and relevant FAR references contained within this solicitation. Ensure that your firm fits the definition including but not limited to any affiliations (domestic or foreign). The Small Business Administration determines whether an entity qualifies as a small business concern by counting its receipts, employees, or other measures including those of all its domestic and foreign affiliates, regardless of whether the affiliates are organized for profit. 13 C.F.R. 121.103(a)(6). SBA has a specific set of rules that explain when another person, business or entity is considered an affiliate for size standard purposes. SBA's rules on affiliation for its programs (except SBIR and STTR) are found at 13 C.F.R. 121.103.

52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting. (Deviation 2026-O0038)
Limitations on Subcontracting (Feb 2026) (Deviation 2026-O0038)
(a) This clause does not apply to the unrestricted portion of a partial set-aside.
(b) Definition. Similarly situated entity, as used in this clause, means a first-tier subcontractor, including an independent contractor, that—
(1) Has the same small business program status as that which qualified the prime contractor for the award (e.g., for a small business set-aside contract, any small business concern, without regard to its socioeconomic status); and
(2) Is considered small for the size standard under the North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code the prime contractor assigned to the subcontract.

(1) Contracts that have been set aside for any of the small business concerns identified in 19.000(a)(3);

For Lot B (Small Business Set-Aside) under NAICS 541512, the RFP states that only the Prime contractor and JV members will be evaluated for technical experience. When using experience of a Large Business JV mentor for Lot B, Offerors shall provide documentation that the JV Mentor-Protégé agreement has been approved by the SBA. Please see Section L, Part B, 1.2.2.c. and 1.3.3 b

Yes, if you would like to be considered for Lot A and Lot B, please submit separate proposals. Please note, Small businesses that qualify for Lot B award will be eligible to compete for both Lot A and B requirements at the Task Order level (see Section L, Part A 1.0)

A TO under OASIS Small Business (SB) is a Tier 3 Best-in-Class (BIC) Contract, the highest Category Management (CM) tier. The additional option year boost the continued achievement of KSC's CM goals. A statement of the actions, if any, the agency may take to remove or overcome any barriers that led to the restricted consideration before any subsequent acquisition for the supplies or services is made: One the agency's requirements are clarified a Request for Proposal for the follow-on LENS II procurement will be issued as soon as practicalable to all businesses on-ramped to the OASIS+ Total Small Business contract. The intention with the additional option year to the LENS contract is simply to prevent a disruption in work while the Agency navigates unprecedented uncertainties. Additionally, The CO will continue to examine the market in the future for alternative solutions or new sources before executing any subsequent acquisitions for the same requirements.

In accordance with FAR 15.503(a)(2), a SPECIAL NOTICE providing preaward notice to small business offerors will be posted to the GPE (SAM.gov) today, 5 March 2026, prior to the award of contracts pursuant to the CASS acquisition, which is anticipated for Wednesday, 11 March 2026. When using a small business set-aside, as here (see FAR Subpart 19.5), "the contracting officer shall notify each offeror in writing prior to award . . . ." FAR 15.503(a)(2). This preaward notification for small businesses allows small business offerors an opportunity to protest an apparent successful offeror's small-business size representation prior to award of a contract or immediately thereafter. See FAR 19.302(d). A size protest filed prior to notice being made of the selection of the apparent successful offeror(s) would be dismissed as premature by the Small Business Administration (SBA). FAR 19.302(d)(4). Upon receipt of the SPECIAL NOTICE identifying the apparent successful offeror(s), any concern or other interested party may file a protest with the contracting officer; however, in order to be considered "timely" it must be received by the contracting officer by the close of business of the fifth business day after the SPECIAL NOTICE is received. See FAR 19.302(d)(1). A protest must be timely in order to affect a specific solicitation. FAR 19.302(d).

EVALUATION of the technical approach will be based upon the extent to which the vendor thoroughly and accurately describes the overall technical solution. It is the Government's intention to issue a single firm-fixed price contract.

The Government will award a single contract resulting from this request for quotes to the responsible vendor whose quote, conforming to the RFQ; will be most advantageous to the Government, price and other factors considered.

The Contracting Officer reserves the right to evaluate quotes without the use of formal evaluation procedures such as establishing a competitive range, conducting discussions, scoring quotations, and to make an award to other than the lowest quote.

To evaluate price, quotes will be evaluated using a price/technical Tradeoff. This process is appropriate because it is in the Government's best interest to consider award to other than the lowest price. In evaluating the technical portion of the quote, the technical evaluators will evaluate the following factors, Technical, Past Performance and Price. The factors of Technical Capability and Past Performance are weighted equally. All evaluation factors other than price, when combined, are significantly more important than price.

L.2 Proposal Submission Tracks
Offerors must clearly identify which of the following two tracks they are competing under. An offeror may only submit a proposal for one track.
• Track 1: Unrestricted Competition (Full PWS Scope)
o This track is open to all businesses, regardless of size.
o Offerors under this track must submit a proposal that addresses all requirements across the entire Performance Work Statement (PWS).
o The Government intends to award a maximum of three (3) IDIQ contracts from this track.
• Track 2: Small Business Set-Aside (Partial PWS Scope)
o This track is a total small business set-aside. Only offerors certified as a small business under the applicable NAICS code for this acquisition may propose under this track.
o Offerors under this track may propose a minimum of one (1) and a maximum of four (4) major sections of the PWS. The major PWS sections are defined as: [List the major PWS sections here, e.g., 1. Program Management, 2. Engineering Support, 3. Cybersecurity, 4. Logistics, 5. Training].
o Offerors must explicitly state in their proposal which PWS sections they are proposing to perform. Their technical proposal, past performance, and price proposal must correspond only to the PWS sections selected.
o The Government intends to award a maximum of four (4) IDIQ contracts from this track.

This acquisition is set-aside for small business concerns. This solicitation incorporates provisions and clauses by reference. The full text of provisions and clauses may be accessed electronically at www.acquisition.gov.

The applicable North American Industry Classification Standard Code is 541611. This solicitation is being issued as 100% Small Business Set Aside. All responsible sources may submit a quotation which will be considered by the agency.

Anticipated Date of Occupancy/Period of Performance is June 1, 2026 – May 31, 2027, with two possible option years.

Statement of Requirement
The horticulture specialist will assist RMA personnel and other interested parties to provide advice on nursery issues related to subtropical and temperate plants in areas covered by the 10 RMA Regional Offices. The Contractor shall perform the following tasks as detailed below:
(i) Advise, assist, and coordinate with RMA personnel and other interested parties on questions and issues related to nursery plant production practices, plant classification and taxonomy, hardiness zone limitations, storage and plant protection procedures and requirements, risk assessment, wholesale marketing, and nursery crop insurance procedures.
(ii) Attend RMA and nursery industry meetings, as necessary.
(iii) Aid and participate with RMA personnel in large claims, as necessary.
(iv) Possess knowledge of the NVS pilot crop insurance program to advise and assist RMA in modifications to the nursery program.

Offerors are not required to purchase any video teleconferencing (VTC) software in response to this solicitation and will not be reimbursed for any costs associated. Offerors must state in their submission whether they can or cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams. Oral Proposals: Directions for oral proposal elements are set forth below:

(a) See Table 1 in this RFP - Proposal Organization for Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach is to be proposed orally. Contractors shall submit slides of the planned oral proposal in accordance with the proposal due dates contained within this RFP. Slides shall be prepared using Microsoft PowerPoint, in any style the contractor deems reasonable; however, font size for slide text must be 14 points or larger. Font used for graphics shall be no smaller than ten points. All Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach, requirements must be included and discussed within the oral proposal to be considered complete. Slides shall be submitted at the same time proposals are due, in order to establish that a complete proposal has been submitted. It is further noted that the slides submitted with the written portions of the proposal MUST be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted. The electronically submitted slides for the oral proposals will constitute the proposal submission for Factor 1, Subfactors 1 – 4 and shall contain all information to be covered in the oral proposal. There is a 36-page limit for the slides, and the proposal is limited to the time period identified below.

(b) Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 60 minutes per contractor. Any information not briefed within the time limit will not be evaluated. At the conclusion of the proposal, there will be a 15-30 minute break followed by an estimated additional 30 minutes for a Question and Answer (Q&A) period to allow the Government to seek clarifications, if required. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal and does not constitute discussions.

(d) Representation at the oral proposal is limited to four individuals per contractor. The four individuals shall consist of three speakers/presenters and one silent party driving the presentation. The three individuals that are speakers/presenters must include personnel who are being proposed to work on the effort and at least one individual must be either the individual who will be identified as key personnel upon award or proposing company's executive.

Oral Proposals: Directions for oral proposal elements are set forth below:

(a) See Table 1 in this RFP - Proposal Organization for Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach is to be proposed orally. Contractors shall submit slides of the planned oral proposal in accordance with the proposal due dates contained within this RFP. Slides shall be prepared using Microsoft PowerPoint, in any style the contractor deems reasonable; however, font size for slide text must be 14 points or larger using Arial, Times New Roman, or Courier New font. Font used for graphics, footer and page numbers shall be no smaller than ten points. Any other text present on any and all slides shall be 14-point font. Contractors may use both text slides and graphics slides. All Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach, requirements must be included and discussed within the oral proposal to be considered complete. Slides shall be submitted at the same time proposals are due, in order to establish that a complete proposal has been submitted. It is further noted that the slides submitted with the written portions of the proposal MUST be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted. Contractors shall not include narrative discussions (i.e., note pages, handouts, or other similar narrative material) with the slides. The electronically submitted slides for the oral proposals will constitute the proposal submission for Factor 1, Subfactors 1 – 4 and shall contain all information to be covered in the oral proposal. There is a 36-page limit for the slides, and the proposal is limited to the time period identified below.
oral_presentation

B. Oral Presentation Medium: The Government intends to hold oral presentations virtually via a Microsoft (MS) Teams meeting. Offerors are not required to purchase any video teleconferencing (VTC) software in response to this solicitation and will not be reimbursed for any costs associated. Offerors must state in their submission whether they can or cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams (see addendum to 52.212-1). If an offeror cannot present their oral proposal via MS Teams, the Government will make every reasonable attempt to accommodate the offeror via another VTC medium or voice-only telephone. By participating in the oral presentations, the offeror acknowledges that it complies with all solicitation rules and regulations, in accordance with applicable laws and statutes.

C. Recording Oral Presentations: Recording oral presentations by offerors or their representatives is strictly prohibited, notwithstanding local laws and regulations with regards to virtual meetings or voice-only telephone oral presentations. The Government reserves the right to record oral presentations. In accordance with FAR 15.102(e), the Government will maintain an official record of the oral presentation, which will be the basis of the evaluation. The official record will consist of:
1. A copy of the final presentation slides provided by the offeror.
2. The individual technical evaluation worksheets completed by each government evaluator during the presentation.
The Government's record will be considered source selection sensitive information and will be handled accordingly.

D. Exchanges During Oral Presentation: The Government intends to engage in interactive dialogue during the oral presentations. These exchanges are viewed as a component of the oral presentation itself and do not constitute discussions.

2. Oral Proposal. Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 90 minutes per offeror. At the conclusion of the oral proposal, the Government will convene for up to a 30-minute internal caucus to determine if there are any questions for the offeror. After the 30-minute caucus, there will be a 30-minute session for the Government to ask the offeror questions. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal, and offerors will not be given the opportunity to revise any part of their previously submitted written proposal, presentation slides, or submit any subsequent proposal revisions. The question-and-answer segment will be of clarifying nature only per FAR 15.306(a), which will not constitute discussions under FAR 15.306(b) and (d). Offerors may not exceed the 90-minute time limit for oral proposals.

3. Presentation Slides. Offerors must submit their presentation slides with their proposal submission for Subfactors 2.1 – 2.5 and must cover all presentation material. The slides must be received by the Contracting Officer via email no later than 48 hours prior to the scheduled presentation time. These slides will form part of the official record for evaluation. If the offeror does not include their slides, the Government will consider the offeror as non-responsive and exclude the offeror from further consideration. The presentation slides submitted must be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted between the time the slides are submitted and the time of the oral proposal.

4. Oral Presentation Participants. Each offeror is allowed four representatives at the oral presentation: three speakers (three key personnel or two key personnel and one company executive) and one silent participant. These presentations will be conducted virtually via MS Teams DoD 365-J.

All costs related to the oral presentations will be the responsibility of the offeror; no costs will be reimbursed by the Government.

Q2:  Will the Government accept a migration plan submitted as an appendix to the slide presentation and that the migration services strategy plan be evaluated as part of the proposal?

A2:  No.  The offeror must present its approach to integrate with existing DISA solutions and provide the migration services (outlined in PWS section 6.1.8) as part of their oral presentation.  

Q3: Can offerors submit external handouts for network diagrams outside of their slide presentation and not count against the maximum slide count? 

A3: No. The offeror must present the network design as part of their oral proposal.  The offeror may include network diagrams of the proposed design within the presentation slides as visual aids.  These designs will count toward the maximum slide count. 

Q4: Would DISA consider Oral and Technical Proposal submitted separately? Meaning, can Orals be assigned once down selected? 

A4: No. Also, there is no down selection process outlined in the RFP.

Q5: Will the slides for the Technical and Management Approach (Volume II) be due at the same time as Price volume and other elements of the full proposal?

A5: Yes; see the addendum to FAR 52.212-1(a)(7)(vi), Electronic Offers.  The offeror must submit all volumes with their proposal. 

Q6: How will offerors schedule / be notified of their Oral Presentation date and time? 

A6: See addendum to 52.212-1(a)(7)(vii)(B)(7). The Government intends to schedule oral proposals with responsive offerors approximately 10 business days after the solicitation closes. Oral proposals will take place approximately within 30 days after they are scheduled; dates and times will be assigned at random for all responsive offerors.

Q7: Are all offerors given an opportunity to present their technical approach orally via MS Teams following timely and accurate submission of the proposal?

A7: If an offeror's proposal is timely and complete (adheres to the RFP), the Government will invite the offeror to present their technical proposal orally.

2.6 PROPOSAL EVALUATION
All proposals will be evaluated using the criteria specified in Section 4 of this RFP. Each proposal will be reviewed to determine whether it has been submitted in accordance with the proposal requirements. Proposals deemed non-responsive will be rejected from further review and the Offeror will be advised in writing.

Proposals deemed responsive will be evaluated based on the evaluation criteria set forth in this RFP. The County will determine which proposals are reasonably susceptible of being selected for award based on the evaluation criteria. The County may also conduct oral presentations/interviews.

Selection will include an analysis of proposals by an Evaluation Committee composed of County personnel who will review the proposal submittals in accordance with the submittal requirements and the evaluation criteria set forth in Section 4 of this RFP. The committee may request oral interviews and/or site visits. The County will determine which proposals are reasonably susceptible of being selected for award based on the evaluation criteria. Other factors, as detailed in the RFP, will be considered in determining what proposal will be deemed to best meet the needs of Fulton County. Awards will not necessarily be based on cost alone.

Phase 2 – Oral Presentation/Pitch: Companies whose Solution Brief are evaluated to be of the most merit may be invited to provide a in-person pitch to provide further details of their proposed complete solution and the technical and business viability of the solution to PAE Fires subject matter expert panel, potentially consisting of representation across the PAE Fires and Army/DoD Community of Interest. The Government expects to limit the number of companies invited to pitch. As such, a company's solution may be evaluated to be of merit but not invited to pitch. Additionally, the Government may elect to proceed to Phase 3 without inviting any Companies to Pitch, or without receiving any Oral Presentations/Pitches.

Phase 2 – Oral Presentation/Pitch:
Individual pitches, if requested, are expected to provide additional insight into the Solutions Brief and as such will be evaluated against the same factors as the Solution Brief. Individual pitches will be initially evaluated on the basis of merit of the proposed concept in addressing the PWS and the factors above, not against any other pitches held under this announcement. Pitch evaluation will consider the full content of the actual Oral Presentation (including answers or inability to answer any questions posed) and the content of the slides presented. Pitch evaluation will also consider the previously submitted Solution Brief; thus, inconsistencies between the Solution Brief and presentation, or between the digital copies of the slides and the oral presentation itself, may be viewed unfavorably. It is anticipated that pitches will be conducted in-person with an up to one hour presentation and up to 30-minute question/answer session, though specific parameters of the Oral Presentation may change before the presentation date.

No. Evaluation Criteria Scoring Method Weight (Points)
1. Quality of Engagement 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated based on their ability to engage and respond effectively to the committee's questions and comments.

2. Understanding of City Needs and System Integration 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on their demonstrated understanding of the City's operational needs and objectives, as well as their ability to integrate with existing utility systems. This includes compatibility with GIS platforms, SCADA/ADMS data ingestion, CIS, AMI, and IVR interfaces, and the ability to maintain data accuracy, synchronization, and minimal latency across systems.

3. Implementation Methodology and Project Management 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on the clarity and effectiveness of their implementation approach, including defined project phases and timeline, required utility resources, data migration and validation processes, and support for testing, cutover, and go-live activities to ensure a successful deployment.

4. Training, Support, and Lifecycle Services 0-5 Points 25 (25% of Total)
Respondents will be evaluated on their ability to provide comprehensive initial and ongoing training, a clearly defined support structure with appropriate coverage, escalation procedures, and response times, and a proactive approach to software updates, enhancements, and cybersecurity.

D. PRESENTATION/DEMONSTRATION/INTERVIEW - 10 points
Bidders that are susceptible to contract award based on their combined technical and cost score will be invited to provide a Presentation/Demonstration/Interview. Upstate will notify the Bidders and schedule the date and time. The presentation/demonstration/interview will provide an opportunity for these bidders to clarify or elaborate on their proposals, but shall in no way change their original proposals.

All demonstrations will be offered remotely to Upstate utilizing WebEx. Upstate will provide the meeting link once Bidder's presentation date is set.

The presentation/demonstration/interview will be evaluated and scored by the technical review committee. All costs associated with the Bidder's presentation will be borne by the Bidder. See Attachment 12- Presentation/Demonstration/Interview Agenda. There will be no discussion of costs or additional modules that are not included in the Bidder's pricing/proposal during the presentation/demonstration/interview. The presentation/demonstration/interview shall not exceed one (1) hour, there will be thirty (30) minutes after the presentation for questions and answers for a total time of ninety (90) minutes.

The presentation/demonstration/interview itself is worth a total of fifteen (10) points. The breakdown of the ten (10) points is as follows:
Ability of Vendor to meet Upstate's needs- 5 points
Ability to answer evaluator's questions and/or clarify items in its proposal-5 points

Points for the presentation/demonstration/interview will be added to Bidder's technical score to get a final combined score for Technical and Financial criteria.

DEMONSTRATION REQUIREMENTS (MODIFIED)
Demonstrations given by an Offeror under this section are permitted and communication by the Offeror with the Agency or its employees during a demonstration will not violate the restrictions applicable to Offerors. The presentation will be held virtually, and any expenses incurred by the Offeror are the Offeror's sole responsibility. The time allotted per individual Offeror to present and demonstrate facts shall not exceed one hour, to include a question-and-answer session.

The Procurement Officer will notify the responsive Offerors as quickly as possible to schedule a specific date and time.

THIS IS NOT A MARKETING OPPORTUNITY!

(a) The activities of the Offeror are limited to a demonstration of the system proposed and described in the Offeror's written proposal.
(b) Evaluators may ask questions pertaining to the Offeror's demonstration at the conclusion. The Offeror's answers are restricted to statements of fact. The Offeror may be required to document an answer if such a written clarification is determined to be in the best interest of the State.
(c) Offeror will not be allowed or permitted to introduce new information or show products/features not included in their proposal.
(d) Negotiation is not permitted at this stage in the procurement process.
(e) The demonstration should be conducted in a straightforward manner in order to secure a clear and meaningful understanding of the Offeror's proposal.

The Government will evaluate all Oral Proposal Packages (see Section 4.5) and anticipates that selected performers will be given an Other Transaction (OT) for Prototype award with a 32-month (18-month base, 14-month option) period of performance.

Oral presentations are anticipated to take place approximately four weeks after notification of selection. OPP content and format is detailed in Section 4.4, however the final requirements, to include templates, submittal instructions for OPPs, and proposed presentation dates for oral presentations, will be provided in the invitation to submit an OPP and participate in an oral presentation. The Government will review all OPPs, which will not be made public or provided to other proposers. For Phase 1 and Phase 2, proposers must only propose an OT for Prototype with fixed payable Payment Milestones. (Note – Payment Milestones represent a completed event and do not necessarily correspond 1:1 to the Program Milestones listed in Table 2. Payment Milestone schedule is based on key observable events in the critical path to accomplish program objectives. Payments are triggered by successful performance of observable technical events. Fixed payable milestones are payments based on successful completion of the milestone accomplishments agreed to in the milestone plan.)

STEP 2 Evaluation

Offerors invited to Step 2 will be evaluated through technical evaluation and price. No other criteria will be used. Technical evaluation is more important than price.

Technical
The Government will evaluate the offeror's solution proposed during its demonstration, including presentation material, against the evaluation criteria described below. The Government will use a Pass/Fail system for the first two evaluation criteria. If the Government renders the first two factors for the proposed solution as "Pass," the proposal will be evaluated against criteria three through five below, which are in descending order of importance. Each demonstration and supporting material shall be evaluated on its individual merits rather than on a comparative basis with other offerors.

Technical Factors:
• The proposed solution is relevant and directly addresses the problem statement, objectives, and requirements outlined in the selected Focus Area. (Pass/Fail)
• The offeror's proposed data rights assertions adequately meet the Government's stated Focus Area data-rights intent. (Pass/Fail)
• The extent to which the proposed solution possesses strong technical merit, is feasible, and is supported by a clear and credible plan for identifying, managing, and mitigating technical and programmatic risks.
• The extent to which the offeror demonstrates company viability, possesses a qualified management team, and has provided a sound and acceptable notional schedule for project execution.
• The extent to which the solution presents a unique, underutilized, or innovative application of commercial technology, products, or practices that provides a compelling advantage over traditional approaches.

SECTION 4 – PITCHES (PHASE 2)

4.1 General Guidelines
In Phase 2, companies shall provide an in-person pitch at their proposed location or other location agreed upon by the company and the Government to demonstrate and provide further details on their proposed concept / technology / solution and the technical and business viability of the proposed solution. The Government may also leverage external market research during Phase 2 to evaluate a company's viability.

In addition to the Pitch, the Government, at its discretion, may request for consideration an additional written submission to further supplement or clarify the information provided in the Phase 1 Solution Briefs. The Government, at its discretion, may elect to release additional information to companies that have been selected for the pitch.

The pitch format will be as follows:
(1) Up to a 90-minute presentation provided by the company, the Government may request an audio recording of this presentation
(2) Up to a 90-minute question and answer between the Government and company
(3) Up to a 120-minute site visit / tour of facilities by company, if the company location is selected

Individual pitches will be evaluated based on the merit of the proposed concept in addressing the SOW.

4.2 Pitch Content
During the Phase 2 Pitch, the company must detail / address at a minimum:
• Organizational breakdown
   o Explain the team and major stakeholder structure and subject matter expertise within the team.
• Technology Solution
   o Overview of the attributes which will be met by the solution.
   o Design maturity and past operational history (if applicable). If not applicable, detail the approach to overcome this potential barrier.

4.3 Pitch Basis of Evaluation
Individual pitches will be evaluated against the following factors:
(1) Relevance of the solution in addressing the proposed portions of the SOW and the requirements in this document;
(2) The solution has technical merit, adequately addresses Government need and is feasible;
(3) Evaluation of company viability;
(4) Company's approach is a compelling solution to the Government problem;
(5) The cost estimate and details behind this estimate;
(6) A notional production schedule is acceptable; and
(7) The potential impact of data rights assertions.

Upon completion of the Pitch phase evaluations, the Government will notify the Company that:
(1) the proposed concept/technology/solution has been selected for award of an OTA; or
(2) the proposed concept/technology/solution is not of interest to the Government.

SECTION 5 – PROTOTYPE PROJECT AWARDS (PHASE 3)

5.1 General Guidelines
After award of the OTA, the Government may issue the Company a Request for Project Proposal (RPP) for a project-specific proposal in accordance with a Collaborative Task Instruction (CTI). Receipt of an RPP does not guarantee that a Company will receive a prototype project award. The Government reserves the right, at any point prior to award, to cancel the RPP.

If an RPP is provided, the Company will be invited to develop and submit its proposal. Companies may discuss ideas and details of the proposal during the proposal writing process with the Government. Specifics on a CTI will be provided at the time of RPP.

Upon favorable review and available funds, the Government may choose to make a prototype project award to the Agreement Holder.

I.1. Summary. HQ SACT is issuing this Request for Innovative Participation (RFIP) announcement in order to facilitate collaboration between the HQ SACT, SHAPE (Supreme Headquarters Allied Powers Europe) and participants regarding solutions. The purpose of this RFIP is to request participants to submit solutions (either existing and/or under development) to the scenario presented within Annex I and so participate in the NATO Innovation Challenge 2026-1 (iChall26-1). All submissions must conform to this RFIP.

A Selection Committee composed of representatives from HQ SACT and other relevant experts invited by HQ SACT (the "reviewers") will select up to 10 solutions (hereinafter as "the Finalists") amongst the responses submitted. The Finalists will participate in the iChall26-1 Pitch Day projected to be May 28, 2026, in Rennes, FRANCE. Participation in the Pitch Day is only open to in-person. During the Pitch Day, a Jury composed of representatives from HQ SACT and other relevant experts invited by HQ SACT (the "Jury Members") will assess which of the 10 solutions best respond to the scenario presented in Annex I and select the iChall26-1 winners.

I.2. Dates. The event and timelines of iChall26-1 are described in Section III. It will take place between 10 March and 28 May 2026, with the iChall26-1 Pitch Day taking place on-site on 28 May 2026.

Oral Proposals: Directions for oral proposal elements are set forth below:

(a) See Table 1 in this RFP - Proposal Organization for Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach is to be proposed orally. Contractors shall submit slides of the planned oral proposal in accordance with the proposal due dates contained within this RFP. Slides shall be prepared using Microsoft PowerPoint, in any style the contractor deems reasonable; however, font size for slide text must be 14 points or larger using Arial, Times New Roman, or Courier New font. Font used for graphics, footer and page numbers shall be no smaller than ten points. Any other text present on any and all slides shall be 14-point font. Contractors may use both text slides and graphics slides. All Factor 1, Technical/Management Approach, requirements must be included and discussed within the oral proposal to be considered complete. Slides shall be submitted at the same time proposals are due, in order to establish that a complete proposal has been submitted. It is further noted that the slides submitted with the written portions of the proposal MUST be the same slides used in the oral proposal. No additions or changes are permitted. Contractors shall not include narrative discussions (i.e., note pages, handouts, or other similar narrative material) with the slides. The electronically submitted slides for the oral proposals will constitute the proposal submission for Factor 1, Subfactors 1 – 4 and shall contain all information to be covered in the oral proposal. There is a 36-page limit for the slides, and the proposal is limited to the time period identified below. This limitation does not include a cover slide or an agenda/summary slide.

(b) Oral proposals are limited to a maximum of 60 minutes per contractor. Any information not briefed within the time limit will not be evaluated. At the conclusion of the proposal, there will be a 15-30 minute break followed by an estimated additional 30 minutes for a Question and Answer (Q&A) period to allow the Government to seek clarifications, if required. This dialogue is a component of the oral proposal and does not constitute discussions.

M.3 Award Decision
The Government will make awards to the offerors representing the best value within each track.
• Track 1 (Unrestricted): The Government will rank the proposals based on the evaluation factors. The Source Selection Authority (SSA) will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.
• Track 2 (Small Business Set-Aside): The Government will separately rank the proposals within this track. The SSA will select the top-ranked proposals that represent the best value to the Government.

M.1 Basis for Award
This is a competitive best value tradeoff source selection. The evaluation process will be conducted in two phases. Phase I will consist of the evaluation of the written proposals. Phase II will consist of Oral Presentations for those offerors determined to be in the competitive range. The final evaluation will incorporate the results of both phases.
Factor 1 (Technical) is significantly more important than Factor 2 (Past Performance). Factors 1 and 2, when combined, are significantly more important than Factor 3 (Cost/Price). As the non-cost/price factors become more equal among offerors, the importance of Cost/Price will increase.

11.1 Proposal Evaluation Criteria

Category Points
Cost proposal and value 40
Solution design and technical fit 30
Implementation approach and training 10
Warranty and support 10
Vendor qualifications and experience 10
Total 100

Each proposal will be scored on a one (1) to five (5) point scale for each evaluation category listed above. The score for each category will be multiplied by the corresponding point value (weight) to determine the total points awarded for that category. The sum of all category scores will constitute the vendor's total proposal score.

Scoring definitions:
5 – Exceeds requirements
4 – Fully meets requirements
3 – Meets most requirements with minor gaps
2 – Meets some requirements with significant gaps
1 – Does not meet requirements

Technical proposals will be evaluated first. Only vendors whose technical proposals are deemed responsive and within the competitive range will have their price proposals opened and evaluated. Price scores will then be combined with technical scores per the weighting in Section 11.1.

7. Security, Privacy & Compliance Controls
The system must comply with HIPAA (Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification Rules) and the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) where education and health data intersect. SOC 2 Type II, HITRUST, or equivalent certification is preferred.
Security controls must include:
● Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with granular permissions
● Support for SSO and federated identity standards (SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OIDC)
● Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
● Encryption at rest (AES-256+) and in transit (TLS 1.2+)
● Secure key management practices
● Immutable, searchable, and exportable audit logs for user access, record changes, and data exports
The system must support configurable consent management workflows (including CYBHI participation), field- and document-level segmentation of sensitive data, and role-based visibility controls.

BACKGROUND INFORMATION

The New Mexico Early Childhood Education and Care Department (ECECD) oversees the NM PreK program, a high-quality, voluntary program that is available to families in New Mexico with eligible 3- and 4-year-old children, regardless of income. NM PreK utilizes a mixed-delivery system of providers (i.e., child care centers, Head Start, Tribal, and school-based programs) that apply to become NM PreK programs through a competitive grant process. Chosen programs must implement the NM PreK Program Standards.

NM PreK has grown significantly in the past several years under the leadership of Governor Michelle Lujan Grisham and through significant investments from the New Mexico Legislature. In Fiscal Year 2025 (FY25), NM PreK served 4,940 3-year-olds and 13,436 4-year-olds, for a total of 18,376 children. In FY26, NM PreK expanded to serve 20,163 children.

NM PreK programs are required to implement research-based curriculum aligned with the New Mexico Early Learning Guidelines and utilize assessments – formative and summative. Formative assessments are conducted throughout the instructional period to gauge student understanding and guide instruction. Summative assessments are given at the end of an instructional period to evaluate what students have learned.

C.3.7.2 Incident Management (IcM) – Service Desk Procedures

The Contractor shall provide the Government access to a Service Desk. The Service Desk shall be responsive to the Government 24 hours a day, seven days a week. The Contractor shall document, develop, and follow Service Desk procedures using Incident Management/ Service Desk approaches as discussed in Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL). The Service Desk shall act as the central point of contact between Contractor's service providers and the Government users on a day-to-day basis. It is also a focal point for incident reports and for service requests. ITIL 2011 defines an incident as: An unplanned interruption to an IT Service or reduction in the quality of an IT service. Failure of a configuration item that has not yet affected service is also an incident — for example, failure of a module in the printer that has redundant capability such as the failure of a single laser module in a two-laser module printer. ITIL Incident Management manages the lifecycle of all Incidents. The primary objective of Incident Management is to manage the return of service to users as quickly as possible.

A Service Request is a user request for information, advice, or a standard change (a pre-approved change that is low risk, relatively common and follows a procedure e.g. a password reset (ITIL v3)).

The Incident Management process shall start within one hour of the time the Government contacts the Contractor Service Desk. Within four hours from the Government notification, the Contractor shall document and implement a mitigation strategy to remedy the issue. However, in cases where an Issuance Site does not have a serviceable personalization printer the service desk must ensure that actions are taken that return the site to operation within four hours.

2.3.3 Legacy Systems and Cost
A major challenge is the FAA's reliance on decades-old, custom-built systems where cryptographic algorithms are often hard-coded into hardware or firmware. Upgrading these components to support PQC is a significant undertaking, often requiring complete system replacement rather than simple software patching. Unlike a one-time upgrade (like Y2K), the transition to PQC is expected to be a continuous, ongoing process that requires sustained resources for maintenance, updates, and monitoring of new PQC standards. Due to the decades-long lifespan of NAS infrastructure, the FAA requires a complete Software Bill of Materials and rigorous vetting of third-party libraries. Vendors must demonstrate financial health to support systems for 20+ years. Vendors are also expected to provide hands-on training for FAA engineers and controllers to ensure the internal capacity to sustain both PQC-enabled NAS OT systems and PQC enabled IT systems.

3.0 Technical Requirements/Tasks:
Contractor will come to NDSU Peltier Complex Pilot Mill room facility where the MIAG mill is located. Contractor will:
1. Conduct a site visit for a detailed review and documentation of the existing infrastructure and process flow for the MIAG mill.
2. Perform safety evaluation of the equipment and facility against relevant regulatory (OSHA) standards.
3. Develop preliminary engineering documentation: piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&ID), layout drawings, parts and equipment lists, piping schematics, and detailed remediation punchlist.
4. Assess installation options and infrastructure compatibility, including tie-in locations, pipe routing, utility access, and control integration (if deemed applicable).
5. Solicit budgetary pricing from vendors to inform the purchasing strategy and budgetary planning for Phase II (Detailed engineering, procurement, installation, and recommissioning).

5.0 Government Furnished:
