1. Background.

The Environmental Management Service (EMS) at the VA San Diego Healthcare System requires a customer feedback monitoring service to support restroom cleanliness monitoring and service response. The system will allow patients, visitors, and staff to provide real-time feedback regarding restroom conditions and notify EMS personnel when cleaning or supply services are required.

The objective is to improve restroom cleanliness, responsiveness to service issues, and overall patient experience through real-time data collection and reporting.

2. Scope.

The contractor shall provide a software-based restroom feedback monitoring service that collects real-time customer feedback data and alerts EMS personnel of restroom service needs.

The contractor shall provide all software, system hosting, communications services, data processing, reporting capability, and technical support necessary to operate the restroom feedback monitoring system.

The service shall support approximately 58 restroom feedback terminals currently installed throughout the VA San Diego Medical Center. The Government will provide the existing feedback terminals. The contractor shall ensure system connectivity, monitoring capability, and data reporting functionality.

1.8 EVALUATIONS, NEGOTIATIONS, AND AWARD
This procurement is a Request for Proposal/Competitive Negotiation process. Each proposal will be evaluated, and each Proposer will be available for negotiation discussions and meetings at Palace Transit's request. Palace Transit reserves the right to negotiate any component of any proposal submitted. From the time the proposals are submitted until formal award of a contract, all proposals and negotiation discussions will be considered confidential.

Palace Transit, and the highest-ranked proposer will mutually discuss and refine the scope of work and negotiate terms, including compensation and performance schedule. If the agency and the highest-ranked proposer are unable to negotiate a contract at a fair and reasonable compensation level, PT will, either orally or in writing, terminate negotiations with the proposer. The agency may then negotiate with the next highest-ranked proposer. The negotiation process may continue through successive proposers, according to proposal ranking, until an agreement is reached, or the contracting process terminates.

Evaluation Criteria for Proposals
After confirming that a proposal meets the mandatory requirements stated in the Request for Proposal (RFP), evaluators will apply subjective judgment to conduct a comparative assessment based on the following criteria.

Proposal Completeness and Compliance: Evaluation value up to 30 Points
Scope of Work: Evaluation value up to 70 Points
Comparative Assessment: Evaluation value up to 100 Points
Deliverables: Evaluation value up to 280 Points
Cost Proposal: Evaluation value up to 30 Points

The District's current Customer Water Usage Portal provided by Sensus is being discontinued, and a new portal is needed. The current portal allows the District's customers to monitor their hourly water use and set up alerts for high or continuous usage, which supports the District's conservation messaging. Approximately 1,500 District customers currently use the portal. The District invites proposals from Vendors offering Commercial off-the-shelf software solutions as well as from Vendors proposing to design and implement a custom-developed customer portal tailored to the District's requirements.

The District has prepared this Request for Proposals (RFP) to solicit Vendors (Proposers) to propose a new Customer Portal Solution for the District's 28,000 customer accounts. The District currently utilizes the Sensus Customer Portal, which is being discontinued in August 2026. To continue to provide customer access to their detailed water use data, a replacement is needed.

The District currently uses the Sensus AMI System with towers that read all retail meters and provide hourly use data over the air. The data is stored in the Sensus Analytics Platform. The District uses Xpress Bill Pay for customer payments.

4.1 Deliverables. This program will be executed through a multi-track approach, leveraging specialized technical teams working concurrently to achieve both implementation and operational objectives. While Phases 1 and 2 focus on the foundational engineering and deployment of the security tool suite by an Implementation Team, Phase 3 represents the parallel and ongoing work of a distinct Operational Support & Sustainment Team.

4.1.1 Phase 1: Technical Tool Gap Analysis and Solution Strategy
4.1.1.1 Gap Analysis and Solution Recommendations: Assess the CDE to perform a technical gap analysis focused specifically on identifying the security tools and services required to complete the enterprise security suite. This analysis is not a formal PCI DSS assessment or scope-reduction exercise. The contractor shall deliver a formal report detailing recommendations for a complete tool suite (e.g., SIEM, DLP, MFA) and how these tools must integrate into a centralized reporting framework. The contractor is expected to collaborate with the Government's designated QSA firm but not to perform QSA functions.

4.1.1.2 High-Level Executive Summary: Deliver a summary of key findings and recommendations for the required security tools and services to comply with PCI DSS. The contractor must prioritize the use of modern, automated, and cloud-based tools and services.

4.1.1.3 Solution Implementation Strategy: The Contractor must deliver a Solution Implementation Strategy document. This document must serve as the foundational plan and must include recommendations for a complete enterprise tool suite to be deployed specifically within the CDE at each respective installation, a detailed cost analysis for all recommended tools, and a high-level implementation plan that defines the necessary steps, resource requirements, and a project timeline with a target initial completion date of August 2026.

FAR 52.219-4, Notice of Price Evaluation Preference for HUBZone Small Business Concerns (28 OCT 2025)

(a) Evaluation preference.

(ii) Otherwise successful offers from small business concerns.

(b) Waiver of evaluation preference. A HUBZone small business concern may choose to waive the evaluation preference. If the concern waives the preference, the factor will be added to its offer for evaluation purposes.

(c) Joint venture. Work performed by the HUBZone small business parties to the joint venture must be more than administrative functions.

(End of provision)

52.219-01 SMALL BUSINESS PROGRAM REPRESENTATIONS (DEVIATION 2023-O0002) (DEC 2022)
(a) Definitions. As used in this provision-
"Economically disadvantaged women-owned small business (EDWOSB) concern" means a small business concern that is at least 51 percent directly and unconditionally owned by, and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by, one or more women who are citizens of the United States and who are economically disadvantaged in accordance with 13 CFR part 127, and the concern is certified by SBA or an approved third-party certifier in accordance with 13 CFR 127.300. It automatically qualifies as a women-owned small business concern eligible under the WOSB Program.
"Service-disabled veteran-owned small business concern"-
(1) Means a small business concern-
(i) Not less than 51 percent of which is owned by one or more service-disabled veterans or, in the case of any publicly owned business, not less than 51 percent of the stock of which is owned by one or more service-disabled veterans; and
(ii) The management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one or more service-disabled veterans or, in the case of a service-disabled veteran with permanent and severe disability, the spouse or permanent caregiver of such veteran.
"Women-owned small business (WOSB) concern eligible under the WOSB Program" (in accordance with 13 CFR part 127) means a small business concern that is at least 51 percent directly and unconditionally owned by, and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by, one or more women who are citizens of the United States, and the concern is certified by SBA or an approved third-party certifier in accordance with 13 CFR 127.300.
(b)(1) The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code for this acquisition is 541715.
(2) The small business size standard is 541715.
(3) The small business size standard for a concern that submits an offer, other than on a construction or service acquisition, but proposes to furnish an end item that it did not itself manufacture, process, or produce (i.e., nonmanufacturer), is 500 employees if the acquisition-
(i) Is set aside for small business and has a value above the simplified acquisition threshold;
(ii) Uses the HUBZone price evaluation preference regardless of dollar value, unless the offeror waives the price evaluation preference; or
(iii) Is an 8(a), HUBZone, service-disabled veteran-owned, economically disadvantaged women-owned, or women-owned small business set-aside or sole-source award regardless of dollar value.

The AIR program will consist of an initial two-year Phase 1 and a three-year Phase 2 for a total of five (5) years. At the end of Phase 1, only some teams will be selected to continue as performers, based on performance with respect to metrics and milestones. Meeting metrics and milestones does not guarantee program continuation.

Technical Area 1 (TA1) of AIR aims to develop fully autonomous robotic endovascular intervention systems. After a medical professional inserts the catheter system into the femoral or radial artery, the robot will complete an endovascular procedure without human intervention. The system capabilities will be demonstrated in several procedures, including 3D rotational angiogram imaging, vascular embolization, and, most importantly, thrombectomy. Autonomous endovascular systems developed in TA1-A will encompass:
1) Robotic control systems that can manipulate catheters and guidewires
2) Navigation algorithms based on pre-operative imaging and real-time sensing
3) Steerable catheters and guidewires (if required)
4) Solutions for autonomous clot removal and vascular embolization

In addition, TA1-B will develop an in silico testing and validation environment for these robots, an activity that will include the collection of fluoroscopic videos of endovascular procedures, CT angiograms, and other imaging modalities as needed for training.

Note that autonomous blood vessel access is out of scope for the AIR program; a surgeon or surgical technician will obtain vessel access.

Technical Area 2 (TA2) of AIR aims to develop a set of interventional microbots. Performers will specify a target clinical indication and develop microbots that move, sense, and act to diagnose or treat this condition by means of more precise targeting and/or less invasive access.

All proposals will be evaluated using the following evaluation criteria, listed in descending order of importance.

5.4.1 Criteria 1: Overall Scientific and Technical Merit
The proposed technical approach is innovative, feasible, and complete. Task descriptions and associated technical elements provided are complete and in a logical sequence with all proposed deliverables clearly defined such that an outcome that achieves the goal can be expected as a result of the award. The proposal identifies major technical risks and planned mitigation efforts are clearly defined and feasible. In addition, the evaluation may take into consideration the extent to which the proposed intellectual property (IP) rights structure and software components will potentially impact the ability to commercialize the technology.

5.4.2 Criteria 2: Proposer's Capabilities and/or Related Experience
The proposed technical team has the expertise and experience to accomplish the proposed tasks; the proposer's prior experience in similar efforts clearly demonstrates an ability to deliver products that meet the proposed technical performance within the proposed budget and schedule; the proposed team has the expertise to manage the cost and schedule and; similar efforts completed/ongoing by the proposer in this area are fully described, including identification of other Government entities.

5.4.3 Criteria 3: Assessment of Proposed Cost/Price
All proposals will be evaluated to determine the reasonableness or value of the estimated price/cost proposed to accomplish the work in the Task Description Document (TDD). Analysis may be performed to ensure proposed costs are realistic for the proposed scientific and technical approach and capabilities/related experience, accurately reflect the technical goals and objectives of the solicitation, are consistent with the proposer's TDD, and reflect a sufficient understanding of the costs and effort needed to successfully accomplish the proposed technical approach.

NOTE: Proposers are encouraged to propose the best technical solution. For example, proposers are discouraged from proposing low-risk ideas with minimum uncertainty or to staff the proposed effort with junior personnel to be more appealing from a budget perspective. ARPA-H seeks novel solutions that are reflective of the level of effort and risk proposed.

M.2 TPS Evaluation Criteria and Their Relative Importance
Offeror proposals will be evaluated in three areas: Technical, Past Performance and Price. Technical and Past Performance, when combined (Non-Price), are significantly more important than Price. In the Non-Price areas, Technical and Past Performance are equal. The Technical areas are composed of three factors, listed in paragraph 1.0. Offerors should recognize that Price, although of lesser importance than Technical and Past Performance, might contribute substantially to the Source Selection Official's (SSO's) contract award decision. As the evaluation of competing offeror proposals in the Technical and Past Performance areas become more equal in rating, the more important Price will become in selecting the best value for the Government.

The Government will assign an adjectival rating to each of the factors/subfactors identified below as well as assigning an overall adjectival rating to each of the evaluation areas. STATEMENT OF WORK (SOW)
Imaging Management System (MIMS) Licenses and Maintenance Support Services

PART 1
GENERAL INFORMATION
This action is to provide justification to award a sole source contract for maintenance support for the Mine Safety and Health Administration's (MSHA) Approval & Certification Center's (A&CC) MSHA Imaging Management System. This system was purchased over 30 years ago and has been successfully maintained since that time. The Government shall not exercise any supervision or control over the contract service providers performing the services or equipment herein. Such contract service and/or equipment providers shall be accountable solely to the Contractor who, in turn is responsible to the Government.

The contractor shall provide all personnel, equipment, tools, materials, and services necessary to perform the requirements of the project as defined in this SOW except as specified in Part 3 as Government-furnished property and services. The contractor shall perform to the standards in this contract.

1.0 Background Information
The Approval & Certification Center is responsible for the approval and certification of certain mining products for use in underground coal and gassy underground metal and non-metal mines. Technical experts evaluate and test equipment, instruments, and materials for compliance with Federal regulations. Following successful completion of evaluation and testing of a product, a license (approval) is issued authorizing a manufacturer to produce and distribute products for use in mines.

1.10 Place of Performance
Approval & Certification Center (A&CC)
765 Technology Drive
Triadelphia WV 26059

SECTION 1 – INTRODUCTION

1.1 Background and Authority

The Naval Undersea Warfare Center (NUWC) is responsible for delivering undersea warfare (USW) capability that enables decisive warfighting advantage to the Fleet. NUWC is expanding Tactical Oceanography (TAC-O) prototyping efforts that include the development of new onboard and offboard environmental sensors and devices that build operator situational awareness and information systems that enable operators to increase performance of existing systems through expert use of the environment.

NUWC utilizes prototype Other Transaction Agreements (OTA or Agreements) under the authority of 10 U.S.C. § 4022 to partner with nontraditional and traditional defense contractors and non-profit research institutions to carry out prototype projects that are directly relevant to enhancing the mission effectiveness of military personnel and the supporting platforms, systems, components, or materials proposed to be acquired or developed by the DoD, or the improvement of platforms, systems, components, or materials in use by the armed forces.

The Government intends to award up to $80,000,000 through prototype project awards that ultimately result from this CSO.

(a) In accordance with FAR 15.202(c), the Offeror's past performance, including relevant federal, state, and local government and private contracts, and the past performance of any proposed subcontractors and individual joint venture partners, if applicable, will be evaluated. Information provided to, or obtained by the Government, will be utilized to assess the relevant aspects of each Offeror's record of performing services or delivering products similar in size, content, and complexity in correlation to the requirements of this acquisition.

NASA reserves the right to evaluate past performance referenced contracts for subcontractors other than major subcontractors that have the potential to impact performance of the proposed contract.

The Commonwealth of Pennsylvania (Commonwealth) issues this Request for Information (RFI) to solicit feedback that will be used to assist with the development of a solicitation for a Parking Management System (PMS), which may be released by the Commonwealth in the future. Specifically, this request seeks information from which the Commonwealth can formulate specifications and requirements, realistic timeframes, and cost structures for the referenced procurement and project. 3.0 Price Evaluation
The Government reserves the right to award a contract to an offeror other than the offeror proposing the lowest price when the Government's evaluation determines a proposal is significantly superior from a non-price standpoint and warrants payment by the Government of a premium.
Price is defined as the Offeror's price proposal. The Contracting Officer shall evaluate price independently. Pursuant to FAR 17.202(a), the Government's evaluation of offers shall be inclusive of options. Price proposal in Schedule of Items (Section B - Commodity or Services Schedule on the SF 33) shall be evaluated on the base period and options periods in total, including the Option to Extend Services FAR clauses 52.217-8. The line items will be weighted as follows: Entertainment 39%, Phone – 37%, E-mail – 16%, Video call 7%. Any contract line item identified as ancillary/optional shall not be included in the price evaluation.
Risk Assessment
The Government will conduct a proposal risk assessment. The proposal risk assessment reflects the Government's degree of confidence in the offeror's ability to perform the effort described in their Technical proposal. Proposal risk considers if any aspect of the proposed Technical solution could pose potential adverse impacts on price, schedule or performance of the effort.
Award Without Discussions Offerors are advised an award may be made without discussions (see provision 52.215-1, Instructions to Offerors Competitive Acquisition). The Contracting Officer cannot overemphasize the necessity for the initial proposal of an offeror to provide the Government with sufficient information identifying the offeror's best terms from a cost or price and technical/management standpoint.

Project Methodology: Structure and follow a project management approach for planning, organizing, and managing the staff and activities throughout the life of the project. The Bidder's project management approach must facilitate open and timely communication with the Office and a strong working relationship to achieve the overall goal of completed system design performance that meets or exceeds user needs on time and within budget.

Change Management: Formally document and track all changes to the functional design, technical design, and/or to approved deliverables. For each potential change, the Project Manager will work closely with the Office's Project Manager to define and document the change, its benefits, and if necessary, its impact to the project schedule and budget. No work associated with a documented change will begin without first receiving written authorization from the Office's Project Manager.

Workplan: The Project Manager will be required to maintain a detailed project work plan, with content to be approved by the Office's Project Manager, through the full term of the contract. The Bidder must provide a detailed project-management plan outlining major tasks, milestones, and dependencies.

The project work plan will be created and maintained with an automated project management tool (e.g., Microsoft Project) and will include appropriate detail to provide project status, dependencies, and risk/mitigation.

The project work plan must include: Design and Discovery Phase to refine workflows, confirm data sources, and validate user requirements. Identify milestones for prototype demonstrations, stakeholder reviews, and phased releases. Coordinate parallel development of both platforms to ensure interoperability.

3.2.1 Optional Sprint 1: Preliminary Design
The Contractor(s) shall mature conceptual designs to Preliminary Design Review (PDR) level, including refined architecture, analyses, simulations, updated risks, and PDR presentation. Milestone: PDR (3 months after contract award).

3.2.2 Optional Sprint 2: Critical Design
The Contractor(s) shall advance to Critical Design Review (CDR) level, detailing components, integration plans, subsystem verification, and design data packages. Milestone: CDR (6 months after contract award).

3.2.3 Optional Sprint 3: Manufacturing and Prototyping Demonstration
The Contractor(s) shall fabricate components, assemblies, and produce prototypes for which to conduct ground testing/demonstrations, and address manufacturing risks. Milestone: Prototyping Demonstration (12 months after contract award).

BASIS FOR AWARD

Award will be made on a best value tradeoff basis. Accordingly, the Government will award the contract to the responsible and technically acceptable Offeror whose proposal, conforming to the solicitation, represents the best value to the Government in accordance with the evaluation factors and subfactors stated in this solicitation. The proposal that offers the best value is the proposal that, in the Government's estimation, provides the greatest overall benefit to the Government in response to the requirement. In determining which proposal offers the best value, the Government may make tradeoffs between prices and non-price proposal attributes.

Best value will be determined by comparing the value of the differences in the proposal(s) and Offerors as judged by the non-price evaluation factors with differences in their price to the Government. In making this determination, the Government is more concerned with obtaining superior relevant experience, technical capabilities and data usage rights than with making a source selection at the lowest price. However, the Government reserves the right to place the order with an Offeror(s) whose proposal does not offer the most superior non-price attributes or whose proposal offers other than the lowest price, where doing so achieves the greatest overall benefit for the Government. As the non-price factors among the proposals near equality, price will become more important to the Government.

A. BASIS FOR AWARD
The award will be made based on the overall (i.e., best value) proposal that is determined to be the most beneficial to the Government, with appropriate consideration given to the following five (5) evaluation factors:
Factor 1 - Technical Understanding and Approach
Factor 2 - Personnel Experience
Factor 3 - Past Performance
Factor 4 - Small Business Participation
Factor 5 - Price

Evaluation of proposals received under this procurement will follow the Subjective Tradeoff source selection process, outlined in the DoD Source Selection Procedures, dated 20 August 2022.

IMPORTANCE OF FACTORS
Factors 1 and 2 are more important than Factor 3. Factors 1 and 2 are of equal importance. Factor 3 is more important than Factor 4. All non-price factors when combined are significantly more important than price (Factor 5). Offerors will be cautioned that the award may not necessarily be made to the lowest price offered. To receive consideration for award, a rating of satisfactory confidence or neutral confidence must be achieved for Past Performance and a rating of no less than acceptable for other non-price factors.

Brief Program Summary:
As the threat space evolves, a clear and present need emerges for the United States' industrial base to rapidly design, affordably manufacture, and flight demonstrate meaningful long-range hypersonic warfighter capabilities. These concepts should provide the capability to hold time-sensitive targets at risk from long-range, standoff positions.

The objectives of this program are to design and demonstrate affordable and producible hypersonic weapon concepts through a multi-vendor, phased research and development (R&D) activity. The primary objective of this activity is to explore the lowest cost solutions that are rapidly producible and meet the minimum required speed, range, and payload. This activity will integrate mature, innovative technologies from conceptual design to flight demonstration, with the intent to select and fly at least two vehicle concepts. To maximize program execution speed, and preserve flexibility for phased maturation, the program will be executed utilizing options to structure the phased sprints. The Government intends to award baseline contracts (with a base period/option for foundational work, e.g., kickoff, conceptual development, preliminary manufacturing plan, and risk assessment) to multiple qualified vendors through competitive selection.

Subsequent optional sprints (e.g. Sprint 1: PDR, Sprint 2: CDR, Sprint 3: Manufacturing and Prototyping Demonstration, Sprint 4: Flight Test) will be exercised via separate contract options.

Some of ADG-REI's core duties include managing servers, networks, storage, and cloud environments within Amazon Web Services AWS to ensure high reliability, scalability, and monitoring of performance. Not only would the loss of these services increase the risk of system downtime and operational disruption, but it would also severely impact the ability to recover from system failures. The inability to support interactive capabilities, such as real-time communication, data sharing, and workflow integration, will directly affect USCIS's ability to work effectively with its partner agencies like ICE, CBP, and DoS. This would negatively affect the Agency's ability to fulfill its mission and compromise the seamless exchange of mission-critical information. Furthermore, without the continued support provided by ADG-REI under ODOS III, USCIS will face increased vulnerability due to the absence of proactive monitoring, timely security patching, and incident response, putting sensitive data and essential services at risk. Any barrier to competition will be removed upon finalization of the source selection and a transition to new awardees. No other competitive barriers are known at this time, which would lead to a future fair opportunity exception. Section VI - Evaluation and Award

RRB Supplement to FAR 52.212-2 Evaluation of Offers

A. The Government will award a contract resulting from this solicitation, based on price and technical factors including past performance, to the responsible offeror whose offer conforming to the solicitation will be most advantageous to the Government. Offerors proposals shall be evaluated in compliance with FAR 52.212-2, Evaluation of Offers – Commercial Items (Incorporated by Reference) and the procedures outlined in VI.B through VI.E. below.

Best Value Evaluation. Technical evaluation shall be weighted significantly greater than the Price evaluation. Responses will be evaluated according to which firm best can meet the performance requirements of the statement of work as evidenced by their Approach, Quality Control and Staffing qualifications. Past performance, value added characteristics, etc. will be included to determine which proposal is the best value to the government.

B. Business Price Proposal evaluation –
1. The Government will evaluate Offeror's Total Evaluated Price (TEP) proposal as the sum of the offered firm fixed prices in the sub-CLINs (AA and AB) of CLINs: 0001 thru 0005. Note: The RRB shall evaluate pricing on the above optional sub-CLINs whether or not they are exercised per the FAR clause 52.217-5, Evaluation of Options (Jul 1990).
3. The RRB will consider any Offer not proposing pricing in sub-CLINS of CLINs 0001 thru 0005 OR not being able to perform all the required elements in the SOW as described in Section III, as non-responsive to the solicitation and the RRB will not further evaluate such an offer.

The IMS shall be developed and maintained in accordance with generally accepted schedule management practices, including Government Accountability Office (GAO) Schedule Assessment Guide best practices and Defense Contract Management Agency (DCMA) schedule quality guidance. The Government may assess the IMS using recognized schedule quality metrics to evaluate logic integrity, critical path validity, and overall schedule health. The individual projects schedule within the IMS shall not be baselined until Government review and approval is received. All changes to scope or re-baselining requests must be processed through the Government's change control process and approved by the Government Contracting Officer, Program Manager or designee. If the Government determines that the IMS does not meet contractual or quality requirements, the contractor shall correct identified deficiencies and resubmit the IMS within a timeframe specified by the Government, at no additional cost. IMS submissions that are not logic-driven, contain excessive constraints or date overrides, lack a valid critical path, or otherwise fail to support Government schedule integration and oversight may be rejected by the Government. Acceptance of the IMS does not relieve the Contractor of responsibility for maintaining schedule accuracy and realism.

• Necessary for experimental or test purposes in the development of the best supplies and designs for national defense

The objective of this IPR Notice #3 is to acquire a comprehensive and innovative commercial solution—a process, methodology, or technology-enabled framework— that introduce new ways of operating to address critical gaps in strategic technology planning, alignment, and risk management. The Government is seeking a solution to modernize its approach to Enterprise Architecture. The objective is to acquire a solution that defines a future architectural blueprint, creates a data-driven roadmap to optimize the current portfolio, and governs the technology investments that drive the organization toward its target state. The ideal solution will be a functioning and repeatable framework that can be sustained by Government personnel long-term. In response to this IPR #3, PEO DHMS is seeking proposals for solutions that introduce new technologies, processes, or methods for the outcomes identified in the following AOIs: CSO AOI 1: Operations - Modernization Roadmap: A data-driven process to continuously optimize our current technology portfolio and deliver a prioritized and time-phased plan for modernization and investment. CSO AOI 2: Strategy - Target State Architecture: A clear and actionable technology blueprint that defines the Government's future state and directly enables the Government's long-term business strategy. CSO AOI 3: Governance - Performance Framework: A disciplined governance model that ensures on-time on-budget project execution and provides clear oversight for risk and alignment with the Government's Target State Architecture.

SCOPE

This Performance Work Statement (PWS) broadly defines the requirements for Engineering Services and Science Capability Augmentation (ESSCA) II provided to the Marshall Space Flight Center (MSFC) in Huntsville, Alabama; the Stennis Space Center (SSC) in Hancock County, Mississippi; and the Michoud Assembly Facility (MAF) in New Orleans, Louisiana, which is managed by MSFC. Throughout the entirety of the contract, all references to MSFC are inclusive of MAF. In addition, this contract may support NASA Centers including Armstrong Flight Research Center (AFRC), Ames Research Center (ARC), Glenn Research Center (GRC), Goddard Space Flight Center (GSFC), National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Headquarters (HQ), Johnson Space Center (JSC), Kennedy Space Center (KSC), and Langley Research Center (LaRC).

The ESSCA II requirement is a follow-on to the ESSCA contract. Services and skills are to be provided across a broad spectrum of engineering and science disciplines and requested support will include, but not be limited to, research, design, development, analysis, testing, operations, and sustaining engineering.

The ESSCA II contract will provide engineers, scientists, and engineering technicians in support of MSFC and other Centers, as well as programs, projects, and other activities across the Agency, both present and future. The contract also provides scope for end-item deliverables. The contract includes support for facilitating partnerships and identifying new pursuits and opportunities that align with MSFC strategy. ESSCA II will also support reimbursable work for which MSFC, and other Centers, are responsible. This work can include support to the Department of Defense (DoD), other Government agencies, commercial industry, and academia.

Q3. Can the government provide an estimated scope of audio/visual requirements, including whether equipment will be needed for multiple concurrent sessions in separate rooms?
A3. Audio/Visual requirements pertain to an in-person meeting that is held in one large meeting room (~50-75 people). Requirements include a projector, screen, speakers, microphones, and a person that manages a master computer and all equipment during the meeting. In addition, any other miscellaneous equipment that is required to support the equipment listed above (power cords, equipment cart, screen stand, etc.). The main meeting is 3 full days (Tue-Thurs), with setup on Monday and breakdown on Thursday afternoon.

Q4. Can the government provide an estimated level of effort (LOE), anticipated per contract year, for both tasks?
A4. LOE should be determined by the contractor based on expertise available. However, only part-time support is expected. For Task 1, the deliverables required are outlined and examples of each can be found on our website at https://exwc.navfac.navy.mil/lmr. For Task 2, the technical planning meeting (2.1) would just require labor and travel for up to 2 contractor personnel for a 2-day meeting. For the larger In-Progress Review Meeting (2.2), in addition to the meeting facility and A/V costs, it would involve labor and travel for 2 contractor personnel for 5-days to allow for setup and breakdown associated with the 3-day meeting. In addition, labor associated with preparation for the meeting should be included.

SCOPE (SERVICES) OF WORK:
Contractor will develop the software required for the calculation of the score. The software should be intuitive, user friendly and simple to understand. Proposals must price both a (a.) web application and (b.) optional native Android/iOS mobile application (React Native or Flutter single codebase). USSEC will select the mobile approach during final scoping.

Two existing and well used models were identified when developing the GOLD Standard. They are:
• Expected Progeny Differentials (EPDs): Used for decades in the hog and cattle industries to evaluate individual breeding stocks, relative to their peers within their breed, instead of individual analyses
• Corn Suitability Rating (CSR): A singular composite rating and scoring system used to evaluate different tracts of land for sales to better equip buyers with relevant information on future purchases

KEY FEATURES AND DESIGN PRINCIPALS:
• Simplicity and Transparency: Avoids overwhelming users with excessive metrics; delivers one interpretable score with clear explanations that matter to CUSTOMERS/USERS and fosters trust by enabling quick decisions.
• Integration: Complements existing workflows, using lab/NIR data inputs and supporting—not replacing—crude protein, as the transitional benchmark.
• Adoption-Focused: Phased validation, governance and education via pilots, workshops and an adoption playbook. Data remains customer-owned, with secure handling
• Future-Proofing: Modular for expansion and modification like outcome validation and automated data ingestion, without complicating the core experience.

CIRCLE performers will focus initially on repurposing existing, FDA-approved therapies that can effect immuno-inflammatory reprogramming, ideally with tissue-specific precision, based on individualized patient data and computational models of immune system dynamics and their generative mechanisms. If justified, TA3 will support the development of novel immune-modulating approaches as a means of validating specific predictions derived from digital twin models and/or as novel therapeutics. Proposers should focus on interventions that will modulate or reprogram the immune system or other critical illness-associated pathways that will interrupt or diminish the progression of inflammatory processes and cellular stress/damage that are the hallmarks of the condition. Therapeutic interventions should be integrated with, and based on, the data obtained at the bedside and analyzed and interpreted by the computational digital twin models developed within the program. Interventions must be timely and responsive to individualized and case-specific data, incorporating knowledge of the spatial location (organ and tissue origin) of inflammatory processes, as well as the dynamics of critical illness in individual patients. Proposed interventions should be tested under the most patient-realistic conditions practical, whether in simulations, large animal/non-human primate trials, and/or human studies. To the greatest extent possible, both efficacy and safety data from pre-clinical and clinical studies should be incorporated into the databases made available for all CIRCLE performers, as well as FDA-compliant models made available through ARPA-H. CIRCLE performers will be afforded the opportunity to utilize ARPA-H resources to leverage clinical trial consortia to accelerate the process of obtaining FDA approvals for novel therapies.

M2.0 Basis for Contract Award:
"The source selection process under this Solicitation will not be based on the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) or Tradeoff process. Award will be made to the Offerors whose proposals are the best value to the Government based upon a Highest Technically Rated (HTRO) approach."

The government's use of HTRO with a Fair and Reasonable Price evaluation implies the government values innovation, performance, and mission-critical expertise. If task orders are then competed and awarded solely on price (LPTA), it may undermine the very purpose of selecting highly rated technical offerors. This shift in strategy brings a risk of protest and could have regulatory implications.

Many of the highest-rated offerors awarded under the IDIQ may be disincentivized from bidding on task orders competed under an LPTA evaluation method, due to the increased risk and reduced emphasis on technical merit. This approach may ultimately degrade mission performance and undermine the value of selecting offerors based on an HTRO standard at the IDIQ level.

Market research is conducted for each task order requirement that will be placed on SOFGSD. Part of market research is a determination of the best source selection approach. LPTA is part of the Best Value Continuum and a viable process for some requirements. The Government will use the most appropriate process for the requirement.

B.1 GENERAL
The Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services Program (ProTech) 2.0 is a suite of multiple-award Indefinite Delivery, Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contracts currently consisting of four Domains: Satellite, Oceans, Fisheries, and Weather. These Domains provide resources in support of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to include its Line and Staff Offices. The National Environmental Satellite, Data, and Information Service (NESDIS) intends to establish a fifth Domain, Space-Based Environmental Monitoring (SBEM), that will provide a broad scope for the procurement of commercial SBEM Data-as-a-Service.

NESDIS' Commercial Data Program (CDP) is soliciting proposals for a multiple-award IDIQ contract. The ceiling dollar amount for all orders under all ProTech Domains, including the SBEM Domain, is $8,000,000,000.

This contract provides services to the SBEM Domain under NAICS code 518210 - Data Processing, Hosting, and Related Services.

B.2 ORDERING PERIOD
The IDIQ contracts will have one five-year base ordering period and one five-year optional ordering period. There is no guarantee that the option will be exercised on any or all of the awarded contracts. This is not a multiyear contract as defined in FAR 17.102.

B.3 CONTRACT TYPE
ProTech-SBEM is a multiple-award IDIQ contract that allows for task orders to be issued on a Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP) and/or Firm-Fixed-Unit-Price (FFUP) basis. As necessary, services may be acquired through multiyear or multiple year task orders.

52.209-6 (DEV) Protecting the Government's Interest When Subcontracting with Contractors Debarred, Suspended, Proposed for Debarment, or Voluntarily Excluded (Jan 2025) (DEVIATION NOV 2025)
(a) Definition. As used in this clause—
Commercially available off-the-shelf (COTS) item
(1) Means any item of supply (including construction material) that is–
(i) A commercial product (as defined in paragraph (1) of the definition of "commercial product" in Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 2.101);
(ii) Sold in substantial quantities in the commercial marketplace; and
(iii) Offered to the Government, under a contract or subcontract at any tier, without modification, in the same form in which it is sold in the commercial marketplace; and
(2) Does not include bulk cargo, as defined in 46 U.S.C. 40102(4), such as agricultural products and petroleum products.
(b) The Government suspends or debars Contractors to protect the Government's interests. Other than a subcontract for a commercially available off-the-shelf item, the Contractor shall not enter into any subcontract, in excess of the threshold specified in FAR 9.405-2(b) on the date of subcontract award, with a Contractor that is debarred, suspended, or proposed for debarment by any executive agency unless a compelling reason exists to do so.
(c) The Contractor shall require each proposed subcontractor whose subcontract will exceed the threshold specified in FAR 9.405-2(b) on the date of subcontract award, other than a subcontractor providing a commercially available off-the-shelf item, to disclose to the Contractor, in writing, whether as of the time of award of the subcontract, the subcontractor, or its principals, is or is not debarred, suspended, proposed for debarment, or voluntarily excluded by the Federal Government.

Objective 2
Ensure High-Quality Data and Enable Analyst-in-the-Loop Validation

The contractor shall implement a system that ensures the accuracy and reliability of all extracted data. This includes achieving a threshold accuracy of 80% for topographic and maritime features and 90% for aeronautical features, with an objective accuracy of 99%. The system must feature automated quality assessment processes with confidence scores and provide an intuitive interface for analysts to review, validate, and correct extracted features. All analyst feedback shall be used to continuously improve the performance of the AI/ML models.

Objective 3
Enable Seamless Utilization by NGA's GEOINT Ecosystem via Open APIs

FG desires that AFE capabilities be exposed as a service that is callable and accessible from NGA Architecture via open APIs. This requires seamless interoperability with the systems and data repositories responsible for geospatial AFE data generation and AFE repository management. The contractor's solution shall provide a documented set of open APIs and utilize NGA standardized formats (e.g., GDB, Shapefile) for all extracted features to ensure seamless interoperability with current enterprise systems and adaptability for any future integrations.

Objective 4
Meet and Maintain Stringent Performance and Security Standards

The solution must be scalable, reliable, and secure. The system shall be capable of processing a standard 1GB source file in less than 10 minutes and achieve 99% availability in the production environment. The contractor must ensure the system is deployable to both Unclassified and Classified domains and complies with all applicable security directives, including DNI ICD 503 and all requirements outlined in DD Form 254.

M.2 TPS Evaluation Criteria and Their Relative Importance
Offeror proposals will be evaluated in three areas: Technical, Past Performance and Price. Technical and Past Performance, when combined (Non-Price), are significantly more important than Price. In the Non-Price areas, Technical and Past Performance are equal. The Technical areas are composed of three factors, listed in paragraph 1.0. Offerors should recognize that Price, although of lesser importance than Technical and Past Performance, might contribute substantially to the Source Selection Official's (SSO's) contract award decision. As the evaluation of competing offeror proposals in the Technical and Past Performance areas become more equal in rating, the more important Price will become in selecting the best value for the Government.

The Government will assign an adjectival rating to each of the factors/subfactors identified below as well as assigning an overall adjectival rating to each of the evaluation areas. The adjectival rating depicts how well the offeror meets the evaluation areas/factors/subfactors and solicitation requirements.

3.0 Price Evaluation
The Government reserves the right to award a contract to an offeror other than the offeror proposing the lowest price when the Government's evaluation determines a proposal is significantly superior from a non-price standpoint and warrants payment by the Government of a premium.

Prices will be evaluated to ensure they are fair and reasonable, including consideration of the level of effort and the mix of labor proposed to perform the Statement of Objectives.

The price volume must include a narrative portion and a spreadsheet. The narrative portion includes no page limit and should include a cover letter and a Basis of Estimate (BOE). The spreadsheet should provide a detailed build up by element of price for each CLIN.

The BOE should discuss the estimating methodology used to develop the proposed price (e.g., engineering estimates, should-cost analysis, historical benchmarks, subcontractor responses, vendor proposal or awarded purchase orders, catalog pricing, cost drivers and associated estimates of their activity, etc.). Further, it should describe the types of labor, material, and other direct costs (ODCs) as applicable. This should include a description of the qualifications associated with each proposed labor category and mapped to the position titles contemplated in performance of the needed work. It should include a description for material or ODCs as applicable (e.g., computer software) and any relevant performance information and rationale for the proposed quantity. The PWS should provide insight as to the scope of work envisioned under each line item. Offerors can choose to propose value added services under the services CLIN in each option period, at their discretion.

Certified Cost or pricing data is not required however; Offerors shall provide any required data or documentation to demonstrate that the proposed prices are fair and reasonable. The Government may request additional information consistent with FAR 12.212 and FAR 15.403-3 if needed to determine price reasonableness.

52.219-4 Notice of Price Evaluation Preference for HUBZone Small Business Concerns. (Oct 2022)

Notice of Price Evaluation preference for HUBZone Small Business Concerns (Oct 2022)
(a) Evaluation preference.
(1) Offers will be evaluated by adding a factor of 10 percent to the price of all offers, except-
(i) Offers from HUBZone small business concerns that have not waived the evaluation preference; and
(ii) Otherwise successful offers from small business concerns.
(2) The factor of 10 percent shall be applied on a line item basis or to any group of items on which award may be made. Other evaluation factors described in the solicitation shall be applied before application of the factor.
(3) When the two highest rated offerors are a HUBZone small business concern and a large business, and the evaluated offer of the HUBZone small business concern is equal to the evaluated offer of the large business after considering the price evaluation preference, award will be made to the HUBZone small business concern.
(b) Waiver of evaluation preference. A HUBZone small business concern may elect to waive the evaluation preference, in which case the factor will be added to its offer for evaluation purposes.
[ ] Offeror elects to waive the evaluation preference.
(c) Joint venture. A HUBZone joint venture agrees that, in the performance of the contract, at least 40 percent of the aggregate work performed by the joint venture shall be completed by the HUBZone small business parties to the joint venture.

The NITE-STAR Capability Development MA IDIQ is not a mandatory contract vehicle.

This IDIQ is centralized. SSC/TIK is the responsible contracting office for all task/delivery orders (TO/DOs) awarded under this contract vehicle.

TO/DOs awarded under this IDIQ contract are intended to support the objectives of the OTTI Program Executive Officer (PEO) and support will extend to other stakeholders within the space test and training enterprise.

This IDIQ contract requires the issuance of TOs/DOs to authorize work pursuant to FAR clause 52.216-18 Ordering.

An initial DO in the amount of $10,000, for a Post Award Kickoff Conference, will be awarded upon execution of each MA IDIQ Basic Contract. This initial TO will satisfy the requirements in DAFFARS 5316.504(a)(2). The monetary limit for the sum of all TO/DOs against these contract vehicles is $981,000,000.

The procedures for ordering are governed by FAR 16.505 Ordering. With the exception of the initial TO/DOs, the Contracting Officer will issue a Fair Opportunity Request for Offer (FORFO) to all prime contractors, unless a fair opportunity exception exists. TO/DOs will be subject to FAR 16.505(b)(1) fair opportunity processes with specific instructions provided for each FORFO.

It is anticipated that TOs/DOs will be selected on a best-value tradeoff or lowest price technically acceptable but the Government reserves the right to use any selection method for TOs/DOs.

1.0 Description of Services
1.0.1 General.
Headquarters Department of the Air Force (DAF/A10) provides Strategic Deterrence and Nuclear Integration. The purpose of this effort is to provide expert technical, analytical, operational, programmatic, and planning support for DAF/A10 and its component divisions. All work performed shall support DAF national security strategic plans, policies, concepts, and initiatives. The Contractor support provided will cover International Affairs and Foreign Policy, CWMD and Biodefense, the Nuclear Enterprise, Legislative Support, Strategic Deterrence Education, Integrated Air and Missile Defense, Ancillary Support and Program Management.
1.0.2 Scope.
The Contractor shall be responsible for providing all personnel, management, and other resources necessary to deliver the outcomes and meet the requirements detailed in this Performance Work Statement (PWS). The Contractor shall manage its own personnel and is solely responsible for the quality, timeliness, and completion of all specified deliverables.
1.0.3 Background.
Historically, DAF/A10 has relied on Contractor support to assist in policy development and maintenance on national security issues across its portfolio. To augment its organic capabilities, DAF/A10 periodically requires specialized contractor support to provide flexible subject matter expertise, aid in maintaining continuity, and contribute to the professional development and institutional knowledge of the government workforce.

7. EVALUATION FACTORS AND BASIS FOR AWARD
The Government will evaluate quotations to determine which vendors represent the best overall value for establishment of BPA(s).
The Government intends to establish up to two (2) BPAs with responsible ISBEE vendors whose quotations represent the best overall value to the Government, considering price and non-price factors.

7.2 Evaluation Factors (in descending order of importance)
1. Technical Capability and Approach
2. Relevant Experience and Past Performance
3. Quality Control and Compliance
4. Price
Price will be evaluated for reasonableness, completeness, and balance in relation to the technical quotation and the Government's requirements.

7.3 Best Value Tradeoff
All non-price factors, when combined, are significantly more important than price.
The Government will perform a comparative assessment of quotations to determine which quotation provides the best overall value.
The Government may select a higher-priced quotation if the additional technical merit, reduced performance risk, or demonstrated ability to meet or exceed the PWS performance standards (including 95% AQL requirements) justifies the price premium.

5.4 Evaluation Criteria for Proposals

All proposals will be evaluated using the following evaluation criteria, listed in descending order of importance.

5.4.1 Criteria 1: Overall Scientific and Technical Merit
The proposed technical approach is innovative, feasible, and complete. Task descriptions and associated technical elements provided are complete and in a logical sequence with all proposed deliverables clearly defined such that an outcome that achieves the goal can be expected as a result of the award. The proposal identifies major technical risks and planned mitigation efforts are clearly defined and feasible. In addition, the evaluation may take into consideration the extent to which the proposed intellectual property (IP) rights structure and software components will potentially impact the ability to commercialize the technology.

5.4.2 Criteria 2: Proposer's Capabilities and/or Related Experience
The proposed technical team has the expertise and experience to accomplish the proposed tasks; the proposer's prior experience in similar efforts clearly demonstrates an ability to deliver products that meet the proposed technical performance within the proposed budget and schedule; the proposed team has the expertise to manage the cost and schedule and; similar efforts completed/ongoing by the proposer in this area are fully described, including identification of other Government entities.

5.4.3 Criteria 3: Assessment of Proposed Cost/Price
All proposals will be evaluated to determine the reasonableness or value of the estimated price/cost proposed to accomplish the work in the Task Description Document (TDD). Analysis may be performed to ensure proposed costs are realistic for the proposed scientific and technical approach and capabilities/related experience, accurately reflect the technical goals and objectives of the solicitation, are consistent with the proposer's TDD, and reflect a sufficient understanding of the costs and effort needed to successfully accomplish the proposed technical approach.

NOTE: Proposers are encouraged to propose the best technical solution. For example, proposers are discouraged from proposing low-risk ideas with minimum uncertainty or to staff the proposed effort with junior personnel to be more appealing from a budget perspective. ARPA-H seeks novel solutions that are reflective of the level of effort and risk proposed.

9. GENERAL UNCLASSIFIED DESCRIPTION OF THIS PROCUREMENT
The Aerospace Vehicles Division has been conducting Research and Development (R&D) programs through the Structures Engineering Research Program (SERP). SERP is an ID/IQ contract. The MASTR Program will be the follow-on ID/IQ program to SERP. Through the use of MASTR Program task orders, aerospace vehicles R&D in the areas of Aerodynamics, Multidisciplinary Sciences, and Structures will be conducted to deliver future air and space vehicle capabilities to the Warfighter. R&D activities will include, but is not limited to, conceptual design studies, experimental test planning and execution, model fabrication/modification, analytical and experimental data analyses, systems engineering assessments, technology assessments and evaluations, and computational analyses.

Factor 4 - Small Business Participation
The extent of small business participation will be evaluated against the goals established in the solicitation. All Offerors (both other than small and small businesses) will be evaluated on the level of proposed participation of small businesses in the performance of the contract (as small business prime offerors or small business subcontractors) relative to the contract. All Offerors will be evaluated on the extent to which small businesses, as defined in RFO Part 19, are specifically identified in proposals. Offerors will be evaluated on the extent of commitment to use such firms (enforceable commitments will be considered more favorably than non-enforceable ones). Offerors will be evaluated on the extent to which they identify complex and varied work small firms are to perform. Offerors will also be evaluated on the extent of past performance in compliance with RFO 52.219-8, "Utilization of Small Business Concerns" and RFO 52.219-9, "Small Business Subcontracting Plan."

a. The Government will evaluate the extent to which the Offeror meets or exceeds the mandatory goal of 22% of the Total Contract Value for small business participation. The Small Business Participation Plan will be evaluated using the Small Business Rating Method (see Table 6 in Attachment 1 - Source Selection Evaluation Rating Methods).

All apparently successful offerors that are considered other than small businesses must submit an acceptable subcontracting plan in accordance with RFO 52.219-9 to be eligible for award. This does not apply to small business offerors. Small business offerors are required to submit a Small Business Participation Plan only.

3.1 Performance Objectives/Tasks
The horticulture specialist will assist RMA personnel and other interested parties to provide advice on nursery issues related to subtropical and temperate plants in areas covered by the 10 RMA Regional Offices. The Contractor shall perform the following tasks as detailed below:
(i) Advise, assist, and coordinate with RMA personnel and other interested parties on questions and issues related to nursery plant production practices, plant classification and taxonomy, hardiness zone limitations, storage and plant protection procedures and requirements, risk assessment, wholesale marketing, and nursery crop insurance procedures.
(ii) Attend RMA and nursery industry meetings, as necessary.
(iii) Aid and participate with RMA personnel in large claims, as necessary.
(iv) Possess knowledge of the NVS pilot crop insurance program to advise and assist RMA in modifications to the nursery program.

3.2 Constraints
Contractor must possess sufficient expertise in the horticultural sciences to accurately assess and establish plant categories, hardiness zone limitations, and winter storage requirements for plants. Contractor must also possess the ability to advise RMA personnel on horticultural issues related to crop insurance coverage of plants.

As referenced above and for greater context, this task order is essential for USCIS to continue its adjudicative mission as the Agency would experience unacceptable losses that could not be overcome by Government personnel. Without this task order, DHS and USCIS would lose the services needed to develop and maintain the ELIS and Global enterprise case management systems; these systems currently process over 90% of the Agency's adjudication workload. If the task order is not extended, USCIS would be unable to continue supporting the following initiatives that fulfill multiple Executive Orders, including the development of security capabilities that will enhance vetting and identification of national security and public safety threats (Social Media Vetting), as well as the development of Gold Card, and D1/D2 initiatives such adding the adjudications capabilities for the investor program. Additionally, losing the infrastructure support services provided by ADG-REI under this task order will severely inhibit the Agency's ability to maintain and scale its major enterprise benefits case management system, which would be unacceptable and detrimental to the mission of the Agency. USCIS does not have the bandwidth or skillsets required to design, build, secure, and maintain the foundational IT systems that power business operations.

PROPOSAL EVALUATION

All proposals will be evaluated by representatives of WCPSS. WCPSS may, at its sole discretion, ask for additional information and/or elect to conduct interviews with finalists to clarify information provided in the proposals.

The services that are the subject of this RFQ/P are not required to be bid under North Carolina law, and none of the statutory requirements regarding public bidding apply to this RFQ/P. This document, and not those statutes, will govern the selection process.

WCPSS shall not be required to award the contract to the lowest proposed compensation; nor shall WCPSS have any obligation to explain its decision to recommend or not to recommend any particular vendor or to invite or exclude any particular vendor from consideration at any stage of the process. Instead of recommending that contracts be awarded to one or more vendors who presented proposals, WCPSS may, at its sole discretion, reject the proposals and repeat the process, enter into direct contract negotiations with one or more vendors (possibly including vendors who have not previously submitted proposals) or take any other action WCPSS deems advisable under all the circumstances.

WCPSS is expected to make any final selection(s) based upon any factors or considerations WCPSS deems relevant. Factors often considered include qualifications, relevant experience, fee, and ability to perform work in a timely manner. WCPSS retains sole discretion to award the contract to the vendor(s) it believes will best serve the interests of WCPSS and may consider any factors, documents, or information it deems relevant in making that determination.

1.3.1 Project Planning and Management
All HUD IT projects, including all information systems acquired, developed, enhanced, or maintained shall follow the policy, procedures, standards, and guidelines set forth within the IT Management Framework using the Project Planning and Management (PPM) Life Cycle located at HUD's PPM Life Cycle Website:
http://portal.hud.gov/hudportal/HUD?src=/program_offices/cio/ppm/PPMV20HOME
PPM was designed based on the best practices from Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI) for systems development and PMBok for project management. While at the foundation, PPM is designed as a Waterfall methodology, HUD encourages tailoring the PPM to use the many modern solution development methodologies available to enable the planning, development and delivery of useable functionality within 6-9 month increments.
Contractors are encouraged to propose a preferred methodology whether their solution(s) are for custom development, includes prototypes or pilots, Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS)/ Government Off the Shelf (GOTS) configuration, or Software as a Services. Working with the HUD Project Manager(s), the Contractor will tailor PPM to take advantage of contractor proposed expertise in using other methodologies in a manner that will provide HUD the best value and address all of the work necessary for successful project completion, on time, within budget, and delivering intended functionality.
The decisions of PPM tailoring are captured in the Project Tailoring Agreement (PTA), which documents the specific agreement for creating, combining, referring, or omitting specific artifacts applicable to the project, as well as adjust project control gate reviews to be consistent with the tailoring. All tailoring of PPM must be approved by HUD's Deputy Chief Information Officer (DCIO) for Business & IT Modernization. As a default, any project without an approved project tailoring agreement is required to follow all PPM phases and artifacts as presented.

The intent of this Proposal Invitation is to solicit sealed Proposals to establish a contract for various types of Auction Services that may be procured by Cooperative members. 1.5 Scope
1.5.1 AFSVC is seeking expert technical support to advance its PCI DSS compliance strategy. This SOO is for technical support and sustainment of PCI DSS compliance. It is a separate and distinct effort from the PCI DSS audit itself, which is conducted by a separate, Government-engaged Qualified Security Assessor (QSA) firm. To achieve the objective, the contractor must provide the expertise and resources to perform the following two primary tasks:

1.5.1.1 Enterprise Security Suite Implementation: Deploy a standardized suite of advanced security tools (such as SIEM, DLP, and MFA) specifically within the CDE at each respective installation. This effort must result in a centralized reporting capability that feeds data directly into the AFSVC central repository.

1.5.1.2 Targeted Technical Support and Sustainment: For the subset of installations lacking local IT expertise, provide hands-on technical support for ongoing vulnerability management, incident response, and the effective operation of the new enterprise tools. A key outcome of this task is to establish long-term, self-sufficient compliance at these locations through direct support, training, and the creation of robust local Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and Operating Instructions (OIs).

1.5.2 Out of Scope: This effort does not include the provision or management of vulnerability scanning and penetration testing tools, which are already in place.

1.5.3. Upon contract award, the Government will provide the contractor with access to available supporting documentation to facilitate Phase 1 analysis. This may include, but is not limited to: CDE scoping and inventory documentation, network and data flow diagrams, and lists of relevant third-party service providers.

52.219-01 SMALL BUSINESS PROGRAM REPRESENTATIONS (FEB 2024)
(a) Definitions. As used in this provision-
"Economically disadvantaged women-owned small business (EDWOSB) concern" means a small business concern that is at least 51 percent directly and unconditionally owned by, and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by, one or more women who are citizens of the United States and who are economically disadvantaged in accordance with 13 CFR part 127, and the concern is certified by SBA or an approved third-party certifier in accordance with 13 CFR 127.300. It automatically qualifies as a women-owned small business concern eligible under the WOSB Program.
"Service-disabled veteran-owned small business (SDVOSB) concern" means a small business concern-
(1)(i) Not less than 51 percent of which is owned and controlled by one or more service-disabled veterans or, in the case of any publicly owned business, not less than 51 percent of the stock of which is owned by one or more service-disabled veterans; and
(ii) The management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one or more service-disabled veterans or, in the case of a service-disabled veteran with permanent and severe disability, the spouse or permanent caregiver of such veteran or;
(2) A small business concern eligible under the SDVOSB Program in accordance with 13 CFR part 128 (see subpart 19.14).
(3) Service-disabled veteran, as used in this definition, means a veteran as defined in 38 U.S.C.101(2), with a disability that is service-connected, as defined in 38 U.S.C.101(16).
"Service-disabled veteran-owned small business (SDVOSB) Program" means a program that authorizes contracting officers to limit competition, including award on a sole-source basis, to SDVOSB concerns eligible under the SDVOSB Program.
"Women-owned small business (WOSB) concern eligible under the WOSB Program" (in accordance with 13 CFR part 127) means a small business concern that is at least 51 percent directly and unconditionally owned by, and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by, one or more women who are citizens of the United States, and the concern is certified by SBA or an approved third-party certifier in accordance with 13 CFR 127.300.
(b)(1) The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code for this acquisition is 541715.
(2) The small business size standard is 1500.
(3) The small business size standard for a concern that submits an offer, other than on a construction or service acquisition, but proposes to furnish an end item that it did not itself manufacture, process, or produce (i.e., nonmanufacturer), is 500 employees, or 150 employees for information technology value-added resellers under NAICS code 541519, if the acquisition-
(i) Is set aside for small business and has a value above the simplified acquisition threshold;
(ii) Uses the HUBZone price evaluation preference regardless of dollar value, unless the offeror waives the price evaluation preference; or
(iii) Is an 8(a), HUBZone, service-disabled veteran-owned, economically disadvantaged women-owned, or women-owned small business set-aside or sole-source award regardless of dollar value.

The Government will evaluate the offeror's proposal to determine their ability to perform the core service capability and performance outlined in PWS Section 6.2 and 6.2.2. This factor will be rated as either "Acceptable" or "Unacceptable" basis. To be considered acceptable, the offeror's proposal must meet all of the minimum requirements set forth below.

The offeror must demonstrate the ability to:
1. Deliver and sustain the core isolation capability for up to 3.4 million users, including the technical methodology to isolate all Internet code execution while ensuring page load times are no more than 5 seconds and round-trip latency is no more than 100ms (PWS 6.2).
2. Integrate the CBII platform with the DISN Joint Infrastructure (DJI) security stack, including Web Content Filtering (WCF) and Enterprise Break & Inspect (EBI), ensuring all required traffic is inspected without performance degradation performance (PWS 6.2).
3. Implement and manage the Data Loss Prevention (DLP) capability natively within the solution. The approach must detail how the system will capture file uploads and user inputs to web forms and how DLP policy violations will be reported for analysis by the Government (PWS 6.2).
4. Enable, manage, and optimize the optional HEAT Shield capability. The approach must describe how AI/ML will be leveraged to provide cyber defensive auto-mitigations that identify and neutralize highly evasive threats in real-time, and how the effectiveness of this capability will be reported (PWS 6.2.2).

The CyPhER Forge program requires Proposers, including subcontractors/teaming partners, to access Government-furnished-HPC resources. Access to this HPC environment requires a hardware authenticator in the form of a DoD Common Access Card (CAC) or a provisioned YubiKey. To be issued these hardware credentials, personnel must have a favorably adjudicated Tier 1 (T1) background investigation (formerly National Agency Check with Inquiries (NACI)) or a Security Clearance at a minimum, as indicated at https://centers.hpc.mil/users/index.html#accounts. Work on DARPA systems cannot start until this requirement is met.

The Proposer, as the prime, is responsible for initiating investigations for all proposed team members requiring access to HPC through the appropriate channels and all subsequent new hires after award of the OT for Prototype Project Agreement. The Proposer is responsible for managing all personnel security requirements, including use of the National Background Investigation Services (NBIS) Electronic Questionnaires for Investigations Processing (eApp) system.

Proposers will account for investigation timelines in staffing plans, as the timeline can exceed six (6) months for non-U.S. citizens (U.S. Permanent Residents). As such, the Proposer is encouraged to initiate investigations during the PS proposal period to prevent significant post-award delays.

Participation in the CyPhER Forge program, is restricted to U.S. Citizens and U.S. Permanent Residents (Green Card holders), representing U.S. companies only.

M.2 TPS Evaluation Criteria and Their Relative Importance
Offeror proposals will be evaluated in three areas: Technical, Past Performance and Price. Technical and Past Performance, when combined (Non-Price), are significantly more important than Price. In the Non-Price areas, Technical and Past Performance are equal. The Technical areas are composed of three factors, listed in paragraph 1.0. Offerors should recognize that Price, although of lesser importance than Technical and Past Performance, might contribute substantially to the Source Selection Official's (SSO's) contract award decision. As the evaluation of competing offeror proposals in the Technical and Past Performance areas become more equal in rating, the more important Price will become in selecting the best value for the Government.

The Government will assign an adjectival rating to each of the factors/subfactors identified below as well as assigning an overall adjectival rating to each of the evaluation areas. The adjectival rating depicts how well the offeror meets the evaluation areas/factors/subfactors and solicitation requirements. The adjectival definitions are defined below:
EXCEPTIONAL: Offeror's technical proposal exceeds requirements of the solicitation in many areas. Benefits to the Government/Bureau are identifiable.
VERY GOOD: Offeror's technical proposal meets requirements of the solicitation and exceeds requirements in some areas. Benefits to the Government/Bureau are identifiable. Problems may exist, but are minor, and may be resolved through discussions.
SATISFACTORY: Offeror's technical proposal meets the requirements of the solicitation. Some minor problems exist but can be resolved through discussions.
MARGINAL: Offeror's technical proposal does not meet some of the requirements of the solicitation.
UNSATISFACTORY: Offeror's technical proposal fails to meet most of the requirements of the solicitation.

Amendment 10 changed the past performance experience requirement from "within (3) years of solicitation release" to "within (3) years of proposal due date." This presents a large issue for many offerors who may have limited past performance to use and rather than being able to leverage the past performances they originally planned, may now be required to not submit a past performance volume and receive a "neutral" rating. Changing this requirement after offerors have invested almost an entire year developing proposal files, making refinements to those proposal files based on amendments / Q&As, potentially paying consultants to support these volumes, and already submitting PPQs to the customers of these past performances that are no longer viable to be used based on this change. We do not believe that changing this requirement so late in the game is fair to offerors and results in wasted time, effort, and money that many small businesses do not have. Many small businesses have invested a lot of money into NASA SEWP proposal efforts for the Government to change this requirement so late. Would the Government consider changing this requirement back to the 3 years of solicitation release requirement it originally was? Amendment 11 revised the recency date to 3 years from the original RFP release date.

4.8 Business Continuity Plan
The Contractor shall prepare and submit a Business Continuity Plan (BCP) to the Government. The BCP Plan shall be due 30 business days after the date of award and will be updated on an annual basis. The BCP shall document Contractor plans and procedures to maintain support during an emergency, including natural disasters and acts of terrorism. The BCP, at a minimum, shall include the following:
A description of how the Contractor will account for their employees during an emergency
How the Contractor will communicate with the Government during emergencies
A list of primary and alternate Contractor points of contact, each with primary and alternate:
o Telephone numbers
o E-mail addresses
4.8.1
Individual BCPS shall be activated immediately after determining that an emergency has occurred, shall be operational within 72 hours of activation or as directed by the government, and shall be sustainable until the emergency situation is resolved and normal conditions are restored or the contract is terminated, whichever comes first.
In case of a life-threatening emergency, the Contracting Officer Representative shall immediately make contact with the Contractor Project Manager to ascertain the status of any Contractor personnel who were located in Government controlled space affected by the emergency.
